<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**
**Define natural resources and distinguish between biotic and abiotic resources with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Natural resources are components of the natural environment — biotic and abiotic — that humans utilize directly or indirectly to satisfy economic and social wants.

**Biotic resources (1 mark):** Originating from living organisms.
- Examples: Forests (timber, biodiversity), fisheries (fish stock).

**Abiotic resources (1 mark):** Originating from non-living matter and energy.
- Examples: Mineral ores (iron, copper), fossil fuels (coal, petroleum).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Expected Pattern)]**
**Explain the difference between renewable and non-renewable resources. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Renewable resources (1.5 marks):** Resources whose quantity can be replenished by natural ecological processes at rates comparable to or faster than the rate of human consumption. *Example:* Forests (regrow via reforestation), freshwater (replenished by hydrological cycle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-renewable resources (1.5 marks):** Resources that exist in fixed finite quantities and regenerate only over geological timescales (millions of years), making their rate of replenishment effectively zero on human timescales. *Example:* Coal, petroleum, metallic minerals.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Expected Pattern)]*

**(a)** Classify natural resources in detail based on (i) origin, (ii) renewability, and (iii) exhaustibility. Provide two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY)**. Derive the expression for MSY using the logistic growth model and state the stock level at which it occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a) — Classification (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Category | Definition | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Origin** | Biotic | From living organisms | Forests, fisheries |
| | Abiotic | From non-living matter/energy | Minerals, sunlight |
| **(ii) Renewability** | Renewable | Replenishable on human timescale | Freshwater, forests |
| | Non-renewable | Fixed stock, geological formation | Coal, natural gas |
| | Inexhaustible | Continuous supply, essentially unlimited | Solar, wind, tidal |
| **(iii) Exhaustibility** | Stock resources | Finite, depleted by use | Fossil fuels, minerals |
| | Flow resources | Continuous flux, not depleted by individual use | Solar radiation, wind |

(7 marks allocated: 1 for criterion identification, 2 for renewability explanation, 2 for exhaustibility explanation, 2 for examples with correct pairing.)

---

**Solution to Q.A(b) — MSY Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the logistic growth equation:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Incorporate constant harvesting rate $H$:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) - H
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply equilibrium condition $dN/dt = 0$ and solve the quadratic:

$$
H = rN - \frac{rN^2}{K}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Differentiate $H$ with respect to $N$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{dH}{dN} = r - \frac{2rN}{K} = 0 \implies N^* = \frac{K}{2}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute $N^* = K/2$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$
H_{max} = r \cdot \frac{K}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{rK}{4}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** State the result and interpretation:

$$
\boxed{MSY = \frac{rK}{4}, \text{ achieved when } N^* = \frac{K}{2}}
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Engineering/policy relevance: MSY guides sustainable fisheries quotas, forestry harvests, and wildlife management; exceeding MSY triggers stock collapse (e.g., North Atlantic cod collapse, 1992).

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[Internal Choice Alternative]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of **scarcity** in Environmental Economics. Differentiate between **physical scarcity** and **economic scarcity** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Hotelling's Rule**? Derive the rule for a non-renewable resource owner and discuss its implications for resource pricing and inter-generational equity. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a) — Scarcity Concept (7 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept (2 marks):** Scarcity is the fundamental economic problem arising from the gap between unlimited human wants and limited means of satisfying them. In environmental economics, scarcity is a *dynamic* concept — a resource can become scarcer over time due to extraction, population growth, or degradation.

**Physical Scarcity (2.5 marks):**
- Refers to a *finite absolute quantity* in nature.
- Independent of human demand.
- *Example:* Total global reserves of crude oil are physically fixed; even if no one uses it, the stock is limited.

**Economic Scarcity (2.5 marks):**
- Refers to *insufficient supply relative to demand at zero price*.
- A resource may be physically abundant but economically scarce.
- *Example:* Sand (used in construction) is physically abundant globally, but economically scarce in urbanizing regions due to transport costs and demand spikes.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b) — Hotelling's Rule Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Define the no-arbitrage condition. A resource owner must be indifferent between selling one unit today at price $P_t$ and investing at interest rate $\rho$, versus selling it tomorrow at $P_{t+1}$:

$$
(P_t - c)(1 + \rho) = P_{t+1} - c
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Simplify to obtain the price-growth condition:

$$
P_{t+1} - P_t = \rho(P_t - c)
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Convert to continuous time:

$$
\frac{dP_t}{dt} = \rho (P_t - c)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the simplified Hotelling result for $c = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{dP_t}{dt} = \rho \cdot P_t}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve the differential equation to get the exponential price trajectory:

$$
P_t = P_0 \cdot e^{\rho t}
$$

**Step 6 (0.5 marks):** Implications:
- Scarcity rent (Hotelling rent) is captured by the resource owner.
- Prices of non-renewables should rise at the discount rate.
- Promotes **inter-generational equity** — the current generation does not artificially depress prices at the cost of future generations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating "renewable" with "inexhaustible":** Solar and wind are *inexhaustible*, not merely renewable. The KTU examiner deducts 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in MSY derivation:** Always write "tonnes/year" or appropriate units. Examiner allocates 0.5 marks specifically for unit consistency.
> 3. **Skipping the equilibrium step:** Many students jump directly to $MSY = rK/4$ without showing $dN/dt = 0$. Full marks require the complete logical chain.
> 4. **Misidentifying Carrying Capacity (K):** Carrying capacity is the *maximum stock size*, not the *extraction rate*. Mixing these up is a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **In Hotelling's Rule, omitting the no-arbitrage premise:** A common loss of 1.5 marks. Always state that the rule arises from *intertemporal profit maximization*.
> 6. **Confusing biotic/abiotic with renewable/non-renewable:** These are *independent* classifications. A biotic resource can be non-renewable (e.g., a fossilized organism turned into oil) and an abiotic resource can be renewable (e.g., groundwater).
> 7. **Forgetting the "$N^* = K/2$" condition in MSY problems:** The stock level is as important as the yield value itself.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Natural resources** = biophysical stocks from which useful goods and services flow.
- [x] **Three classification axes:** Origin (biotic/abiotic), Renewability (renewable/non-renewable/inexhaustible), Exhaustibility (stock/flow).
- [x] **Renewable resources:** Forests, fisheries, freshwater, soil — replenished naturally.
- [x] **Non-renewable resources:** Coal, oil, gas, metallic minerals — finite stock.
- [x] **Inexhaustible resources:** Solar, wind, tidal, geothermal — essentially unlimited.
- [x] **Sustainability criterion:** Extraction ≤ Regeneration.
- [x] **MSY formula:** $MSY = rK/4$ at $N^* = K/2$.
- [x] **Logistic growth equation:** $dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K)$.
- [x] **Hotelling's Rule:** Resource price grows at the discount rate $\rho$.
- [x] **Hartwick Rule:** Invest rents from non-renewable extraction to maintain total capital.
- [x] **Scarcity types:** Physical (absolute) vs Economic (relative).
- [x] **Four sources of scarcity** (Pearce & Turner): structural, scale-dependent, criticality, contamination-induced.
- [x] **Tragedy of the Commons** applies to common-pool resources (CPRs).
- [x] **Reserve life** = $L = R/P$ (Reserves ÷ Annual Production).
- [x] **Carrying capacity (K):** maximum stock a habitat can sustain indefinitely.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** Never confuse "renewable" with "inexhaustible."
- [x] **Always carry units** in MSY and rate-of-extraction problems.
- [x] **Exhaustion time** of a non-renewable resource with constant extraction E: $T = S_0 / E$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Renewability Continuum:**
> **"RIN"** — **R**enewable, **I**nexhaustible, **N**on-renewable.
> Think: "**R**ain refills, **I**nfinite sun, **N**o more coal."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Depletable and Non-Renewable Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Depletable and Non-Renewable Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Depletable Resources** are natural assets whose total stock is **fixed by nature** and cannot be regenerated within any humanly relevant time horizon. Once extracted and consumed, the unit is permanently lost from the available reserve. The economic problem is therefore not "how much to produce," but "**when to extract**" a finite endowment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Renewable Resources** are a subset/synonym of depletable resources — they exist as **finite stocks** (e.g., crude oil, natural gas, coal, uranium, copper, phosphate, gold). The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the two terms as **functionally equivalent** in Module 4, although some texts reserve "non-renewable" for stocks with **zero natural regeneration rate** (R = 0) and "depletable" for stocks with regeneration but at a rate slower than consumption.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank Account Analogy**
> Imagine you inherit a fixed bank balance of **₹10,00,000** that pays **no interest** and cannot be refilled. You can withdraw any amount today, or wait and withdraw later. If a friend offers you a guaranteed return of **8% per year** on money held in another investment, the optimal strategy is *never* to withdraw a rupee from this account until you absolutely must — because every rupee left in the account retains its purchasing power while every rupee you spend today is an opportunity lost.
>
> The Earth's crust of oil, coal, and minerals is exactly this bank account. **Hotelling's Rule** (Section 2) formalises *how long* owners should wait before "withdrawing."

### 1.3 Classification of Natural Resources (Taxonomy)

| Category | Regeneration Rate | Stock vs. Flow | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| Renewable (Flow) | R > Extraction | Effectively infinite stock | Solar, wind, tidal |
| Renewable (Stock) | 0 < R < Extraction | Finite but replenishing | Forests, fisheries, groundwater (fast cycle) |
| **Depletable / Non-Renewable** | **R ≈ 0** | **Strictly finite fixed stock** | **Crude oil, coal, natural gas, copper, uranium, gold, phosphate** |

### 1.4 Why Depletable Resources Matter in KTU 2024

- They are the **input to nearly every industrial process** and dominate India's energy mix (~**65% coal-based** as of 2024).
- Their pricing affects **macroeconomic stability**, **intergenerational equity**, and **climate policy**.
- Module 4 specifically equips the engineer-economist to evaluate *extraction policy*, *resource taxation*, and *transition planning* toward backstop technologies.

### 1.5 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Proved Reserves (R)** – Stock known to be recoverable under current technology and prices.
- **Reserve Life** = R / Annual Production (in years).
- **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)** – Industry-standard depletion clock.
- **Backstop Price (P_b)** – Price at which a synthetic/renewable substitute becomes competitive.
- **User Cost / Scarcity Rent (λ)** – The opportunity cost of consuming one unit today instead of tomorrow.
- **Hotelling Rule** – The **fundamental arbitrage condition** governing in-situ resource valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Central Economic Problem

A depletable resource owner faces a **multi-period optimisation problem**:

> *Maximise the discounted present value of net revenues from extracting a finite stock S₀ over an infinite (or finite) planning horizon, subject to a cumulative extraction constraint.*

Mathematically:

$$\max_{q(t)} \int_{0}^{T} \left[ P(t) \cdot q(t) - C(q(t)) \right] \cdot e^{-rt} \, dt$$

subject to:
$$\int_{0}^{T} q(t) \, dt \le S_{0}, \quad q(t) \ge 0$$

where the symbols are defined in the cheat sheet below.

### 2.2 Intuition Step-by-Step

1. The owner can sell the resource today at price P(0), earning revenue and reinvesting the proceeds in financial assets yielding rate **r**.
2. Alternatively, the owner can leave it in the ground. The resource left in the ground will be worth **more tomorrow** if its net price rises.
3. **Equilibrium (no arbitrage)** requires that the **net price of the resource in the ground grows at exactly the market interest rate r**. Otherwise, owners would either accelerate extraction (if in-ground price grows slower than r) or hoard it indefinitely (if it grows faster).
4. The growth condition is **Hotelling's Rule**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation & Units Standard** — All formulas use SI-consistent units: price in ₹/tonne or \$/barrel, quantity in tonnes/barrels, time in years, rate r as a decimal per year (e.g., 0.05 for 5%).

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hotelling's Rule (continuous) | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\bigl[P(t) - C'(q)\bigr] = r \cdot \bigl[P(t) - C'(q)\bigr]$ | Net price grows at rate r |
| 2 | Hotelling's Rule (solution) | $P(t) - C'(q_t) = \bigl[P(0) - C'(q_0)\bigr] e^{rt}$ | Closed-form net price path |
| 3 | Scarcity rent (User cost) | $\lambda(t) = P(t) - C'(q_t)$ | Opportunity cost of one unit today |
| 4 | In-situ value | $V(t) = P(t) - C'(q_t)$ | Value of one unit left in the ground |
| 5 | Present Value of Stock | $PV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{P_t q_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Total economic value of reserves |
| 6 | Hartwick Rule | $\text{Invest} \ge \text{Rent} = P_t q_t - C'(q_t)\, q_t$ | Reinvest rents to maintain constant capital |
| 7 | Competitive extraction rate | $P(t) = C'(q_t) + \lambda(t)$ | Marginal cost + scarcity rent |
| 8 | Monopoly extraction rate | $P(t) + P'(q)\, q(t) = C'(q_t) + \lambda_{m}(t)$ | Marginal revenue = MC + rent |
| 9 | Modified Hotelling (monopoly) | $\dot{\lambda}_{m} = r \lambda_{m} - \dot{P}\cdot q$ | Rent path under output restriction |
| 10 | Backstop transition | $t_b = \dfrac{1}{r} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_b}{P(0)-C'(q_0)}\right)$ | Time to reach backstop price |
| 11 | Reserve-to-Production | $R/P = S_0 / q_0$ | Years of supply at current extraction |
| 12 | Growth of net price | $\dot{P}^{net} / P^{net} = r$ | Hotelling's 1st-order condition |
| 13 | Transversality condition | $\lim_{t\to T} \lambda(t)\, S(t)\, e^{-rt} = 0$ | Stock fully depleted optimally |
| 14 | Total rent collected | $Rent = \int_{0}^{T} \lambda(t)\, q(t)\, dt$ | Aggregate Hotelling rent |
| 15 | PV of finite-stock resource | $PV = \dfrac{P_0 S_0}{r}\bigl[1 - (1+r)^{-T}\bigr]$ (constant q case) | Simplified closed form |

### 2.4 Engineering Field Relevance

- **Petroleum Engineering** – Field development planning and EOR timing.
- **Mining Engineering** – Cut-off grade optimisation and sequencing.
- **Energy Policy (KTU Civil/Mech context)** – Coal phase-down economics; sunset date determination.
- **Materials Engineering** – Critical mineral supply security (lithium, rare earths).
- **Climate-Economics** – Social Cost of Carbon integrates Hotelling rent with externality.

### 2.5 Competitive vs. Monopoly Behaviour — KTU-Favoured Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Competition drives extraction faster than monopoly.** A monopolist restricts output to keep P high, extending the resource's economic life. The competitive market equates price with marginal cost plus rent, exhausting the stock sooner. KTU questions frequently test *why* this matters for **Pigouvian taxation** — taxing at rate equal to the scarcity rent λ(t) restores competitive efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hotelling's Rule (Arithmetic-of-Extraction Argument)

We use the simplest possible arbitrage logic (the form KTU examiners reward for 14-mark derivations).

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Consider one unit of the resource in the ground at time 0. The owner can either:

- **(A) Extract and sell** today at price $P_0$, paying marginal extraction cost $C'(q_0)$. The net cash received is:
$$\Pi_{A} = P_0 - C'(q_0)$$

- **(B) Leave it in the ground** and sell at time $t$ at price $P_t$ with marginal cost $C'(q_t)$. To avoid arbitrage, the discounted value at $t=0$ of strategy (B) must equal strategy (A).

**Step 2 — Discounted value of strategy B.**
$$\Pi_{B}^{PV} = \bigl[P_t - C'(q_t)\bigr] \cdot e^{-rt}$$

**Step 3 — No-arbitrage condition.**
For equilibrium, the two strategies must yield the same present value:

$$P_0 - C'(q_0) = \bigl[P_t - C'(q_t)\bigr] e^{-rt}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_t$.**
Multiply both sides by $e^{rt}$:

$$\bigl[P_t - C'(q_t)\bigr] = \bigl[P_0 - C'(q_0)\bigr] e^{rt}$$

**Step 5 — Recognise growth rate.**
Differentiating with respect to $t$ and dividing by $[P_t - C'(q_t)]$ yields:

$$\frac{\frac{d}{dt}[P_t - C'(q_t)]}{P_t - C'(q_t)} = r$$

This is **Hotelling's Rule**: the **net price (price minus marginal extraction cost) of the resource in the ground must appreciate at the rate of interest**.

### 3.2 Optimal Extraction Path via the Control-Theory (Calculus of Variations) Method

This is the **KTU 14-mark standard derivation**. It rigorously justifies Hotelling's Rule from first principles.

**Step 1 — State the social planner's problem.**

$$\max_{q(t)} \int_{0}^{\infty} \bigl[ P(q) \cdot q - C(q) \bigr] e^{-rt} dt$$

subject to:
$$\dot{S}(t) = -q(t), \quad S(0) = S_0, \quad \lim_{t\to\infty} S(t) \ge 0$$

Here $S(t)$ is the **remaining stock** at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Form the current-value Hamiltonian.**
Treating $S$ as the state variable and $q$ as the control:

$$\mathcal{H}(S, q, \lambda) = P(q)\,q - C(q) + \lambda \cdot (-q)$$

where $\lambda(t)$ is the **co-state variable** = current-value scarcity rent (shadow price of the remaining stock).

**Step 3 — First-order condition w.r.t. control.**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial q} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; P(q) + P'(q)\, q - C'(q) - \lambda = 0$$

For a **perfectly competitive** industry (price-taker, $P'(q)=0$):

$$P(q) - C'(q) = \lambda \quad \text{(in-situ value = scarcity rent)}$$

For a **monopoly** (price-setter):
$$P(q) + P'(q)\, q - C'(q) = \lambda_m$$

**Step 4 — Co-state equation (Hotelling's Rule).**
$$\dot{\lambda} - r\lambda = -\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial S} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dot{\lambda} = r\lambda$$

So the **scarcity rent grows at the interest rate** — identical to Hotelling's Rule because $\lambda \equiv P(q) - C'(q)$ under competition.

**Step 5 — Transversality condition (No-Ponzi).**
$$\lim_{t\to\infty} \lambda(t)\, S(t)\, e^{-rt} = 0$$

Combined with $\dot{\lambda} = r\lambda$, this forces the resource to be **fully exhausted in finite time** $T$, because otherwise $\lambda(t) \to \infty$ while $S(t) > 0$, violating TVC.

**Step 6 — Integrated solution.**
Substituting $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 e^{rt}$ back into the FOC:

$$P(q_t) - C'(q_t) = \lambda_0 e^{rt}$$

This is the **closed-form Hotelling path** for the net price.

### 3.3 Two-Period Discrete Worked Example (Highly Examinable)

> **Problem.** A depletable resource has total stock $S_0 = 100$ units. Demand in each period is $P_1 = 200 - q_1$ and $P_2 = 200 - q_2$. Marginal extraction cost is constant $C' = 20$. Interest rate $r = 10\%$. Find the competitive two-period extraction.

**Step 1 — Set up the Lagrangian.**
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=1}^{2} \bigl[(200 - q_t)q_t - 20 q_t\bigr] (1+r)^{1-t} + \mu (S_0 - q_1 - q_2)$$

**Step 2 — FOC for period 1.**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = (200 - 2q_1 - 20) - \mu = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; 180 - 2q_1 = \mu$$

**Step 3 — FOC for period 2 (discounted).**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = \bigl[(200 - 2q_2 - 20)\bigr] (1.10)^{-1} - \mu = 0$$
$$\frac{180 - 2q_2}{1.10} = \mu$$

**Step 4 — Equate (Hotelling's Rule discrete form).**
$$180 - 2q_1 = \frac{180 - 2q_2}{1.10}$$

**Step 5 — Resource constraint.**
$$q_1 + q_2 = 100$$

**Step 6 — Solve the system.**
Let $x = 180 - 2q_1$ and $y = 180 - 2q_2$. Then $x = y/1.10$ and $q_1 + q_2 = 100$.

From $q_1 = 90 - x/2$ and $q_2 = 90 - y/2$:

$$180 - x = 90 - \frac{y}{2} \;\Longrightarrow\; y = 2x - 180$$

Substituting into $x = y/1.10$:
$$x = \frac{2x - 180}{1.10} \;\Longrightarrow\; 1.10 x = 2x - 180 \;\Longrightarrow\; 0.90 x = 180 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 200$$

So $q_1 = 90 - 100 = -10$? That's impossible → check arithmetic.

> **Correction.** Substitute correctly: $q_1 = (180 - x)/2$ and $q_2 = (180 - y)/2$ with $x = y/1.10$ and $y = 2x - 180$:
$$q_1 + q_2 = \frac{(180-x) + (180 - y)}{2} = \frac{360 - x - y}{2} = 100$$
$$360 - x - y = 200 \;\Longrightarrow\; x + y = 160$$

Using $y = 1.10 x$:
$$x + 1.10 x = 160 \;\Longrightarrow\; 2.10 x = 160 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 76.19$$

Then:
$$q_1 = \frac{180 - 76.19}{2} = 51.90$$
$$q_2 = \frac{180 - 83.81}{2} = 48.10$$

**Check:** $51.90 + 48.10 = 100$ ✓
- Period 1 price: $P_1 = 200 - 51.90 = 148.10$
- Period 2 price: $P_2 = 200 - 48.10 = 151.90$
- Net price growth: $(131.90 / 128.10) - 1 ≈ 2.96\%$ (less than 10% because of demand response — standard result).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Competitive vs. Monopoly Outcomes

| Variable | Competitive Market | Monopoly | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Marginal revenue | $P(q)$ | $P(q) + P'(q)\,q$ | Monopolist internalises price effect |
| FOC | $P - C' = \lambda$ | $MR - C' = \lambda_m$ | Both produce rent growth |
| Extraction rate | **Higher** | Lower | Monopolist hoards |
| Stock exhaustion time | **Earlier** | Later | Reserves persist longer under monopoly |
| Total rent (PV) | Lower (rents competed away) | Higher | Producer surplus transferred |
| Policy fix | None needed | Pigouvian tax $\tau = \lambda$ | Tax restores competitive outcome |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Resource Valuation (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Finite Stock S0 in Ground] --> B{Decision Point}
    B --> C[Extract Today]
    B --> D[Leave In-Situ]
    C --> E[Sell at P0, Earn P0-C Prime]
    D --> F[Hold, Expect Higher Pt Later]
    E --> G[Reinvest Proceeds at Rate r]
    F --> H[Appreciates at Rate r]
    G --> I{No-Arbitrage Condition}
    H --> I
    I --> J[Hotelling Rule: Pt - C Prime = Lambda t]
    J --> K[Scarcity Rent Lambda t Grows at r]
    K --> L[Optimal Extraction Path q Star t]
    L --> M[Exhaustion at Time T]
    M --> N[Transition to Backstop Technology]
    N --> O[Synthetic or Renewable Substitute]
```

### 4.2 Hotelling Price Trajectory Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeAxis[Time Horizon 0 to T]
        T0[t = 0: Net Price = Lambda 0]
        T1[t = T/2: Net Price = Lambda 0 exp rT/2]
        T2[t = T: Net Price = Lambda T just before backstop P b]
    end
    T0 --> T1 --> T2
    style TimeAxis fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Competitive vs. Monopoly Extraction — Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CompMarket[Competitive Market Block]
        C1[Demand Curve D t] --> C2[Equilibrium Price P Comp]
        C3[Supply equals MC plus Rent] --> C2
        C2 --> C4[Extraction q Comp t - HIGH]
        C4 --> C5[Earlier Exhaustion T Comp]
    end
    subgraph MonoBlock[Monopoly Block]
        M1[Inverse Demand P q] --> M2[Marginal Revenue MR q]
        M3[MR equals MC plus Rent M] --> M2
        M2 --> M4[Extraction q Mono t - LOW]
        M4 --> M5[Later Exhaustion T Mono]
    end
    C5 --> Policy[Policy Levers]
    M5 --> Policy
    Policy --> P1[Pigouvian Tax Tau equals Lambda Comp]
    Policy --> P2[Subsidy for Backstop R and D]
    Policy --> P3[Reserve Quotas]
```

### 4.4 Intergenerational Equity Flow (Hartwick Rule Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Resource Rent Lambda times q per period] --> S[Hartwick Investment Rule]
    S --> K[Reinvest Rent in Produced Capital]
    K --> W[Maintain Constant Total Capital K plus S]
    W --> Equity[Intergenerational Equity Achieved]
    Equity --> Future[Future Generations Inherit Equivalent Wealth]
```

### 4.5 Decision Matrix for Resource Policy

| Policy Instrument | Targeted Market Failure | Effect on Hotelling Path | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pigouvian Tax** $\tau = \lambda$ | Monopoly under-extraction | Aligns private MC with social MC | Marks for setting $\tau$ at scarcity rent |
| **Subsidy on Backstop R&D** | Slow transition | Lowers $P_b$, advances $t_b$ | Mention Nordhaus climate models |
| **Extraction Quota** | Over-extraction | Forces $\lambda$ path | Auctioned quotas = efficient |
| **Property Rights Reform** | Common-pool externality | Internalises $\lambda$ | Coase theorem application |
