<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**"State and explain Hotelling's Rule in the context of non-renewable resource economics."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Hotelling's Rule states that the net price of a depletable resource (price minus marginal extraction cost) must grow at a rate equal to the market rate of interest.
- **Equation (1 mark):** $\dfrac{d}{dt}[P(t) - C'(q)] = r \cdot [P(t) - C'(q)]$
- **Economic intuition (1 mark):** Owners are indifferent between extracting a unit today (and reinvesting the proceeds at rate r) or leaving it in the ground, because both yield the same return. If the net price grew slower than r, owners would extract today; if faster, they would hoard.

---

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**"Distinguish between renewable and non-renewable resources with two examples each. Why is the classification important for policy?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Renewable (1 mark):** Stock replenished naturally (forests, fisheries, wind). Use rate ≤ regeneration rate is sustainable.
- **Non-renewable (1 mark):** Fixed finite stock, zero natural regeneration on human time scale (crude oil, coal). Extraction permanently depletes.
- **Policy implication (1 mark):** Non-renewables require **intertemporal optimisation** (Hotelling); renewables require **stock-effect management** (maximum sustainable yield).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transversality condition (TVC)** in derivations → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing current-value Hamiltonian with present-value Hamiltonian** → marks docked for not stating which.
> 3. **Omitting units / numerical interpretation** in two-period problems → 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Stating Hotelling's Rule without justification** (just reciting the formula) → only 1 of 3 derivation marks awarded.
> 5. **Treating monopoly and competitive extraction as identical** → lose distinction marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive Hotelling's Rule from first principles using:
- (i) The control-theory (Calculus of Variations) framework with a Hamiltonian, **and** **(7 marks)**
- (ii) The simple arbitrage argument between extraction today and extraction tomorrow. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline (Examiner's Key):**

*Part (a)(i) — Hamiltonian derivation (7 marks):*

1. State the planner's problem: maximise $\int_0^\infty [P(q)q - C(q)] e^{-rt} dt$ subject to $\dot{S} = -q$, $S(0) = S_0$. **(1 mark)**
2. Write the current-value Hamiltonian $\mathcal{H} = P(q)q - C(q) + \lambda(-q)$. **(1 mark)**
3. FOC w.r.t. $q$: $P(q) + P'(q)q - C'(q) = \lambda$. For competition, $P - C' = \lambda$. **(1 mark)**
4. Co-state equation: $\dot{\lambda} = r\lambda$. **(1 mark)**
5. Interpret $\lambda$ as **scarcity rent** / in-situ value. **(1 mark)**
6. State TVC: $\lim_{t\to\infty}\lambda S e^{-rt} = 0$. **(1 mark)**
7. Conclude: $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 e^{rt}$, exhaustion at finite T. **(1 mark)**

*Part (a)(ii) — Arbitrage derivation (7 marks):*

1. Frame two strategies: extract at $t=0$ earning $P_0 - C'(q_0)$ vs. leave and sell at $t$. **(1 mark)**
2. Discount strategy B: $(P_t - C'(q_t))e^{-rt}$. **(1 mark)**
3. No-arbitrage: $P_0 - C'(q_0) = (P_t - C'(q_t))e^{-rt}$. **(1 mark)**
4. Solve: $P_t - C'(q_t) = [P_0 - C'(q_0)]e^{rt}$. **(1 mark)**
5. Differentiate: $\frac{d}{dt}[P_t - C'] = r[P_t - C']$. **(1 mark)**
6. Economic meaning: net price appreciation = interest rate. **(1 mark)**
7. Discuss why this is *efficient*: any deviation invites arbitrage that restores equilibrium. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** A depletable resource has proved reserves $S_0 = 1000$ units. Demand is linear: $P(q) = 500 - 0.5q$. Marginal cost $C'(q) = 50$ (constant). Discount rate $r = 8\%$.

- (i) Derive the optimal **competitive** extraction path. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Find the **exhaustion time** T. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) Compare with the **monopoly** outcome (state qualitatively). **(3 marks)**
- (iv) What is the **scarcity rent** at $t=0$? **(1 mark)**
- (v) Discuss the relevance of a Pigouvian tax in this context. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Optimal competitive path (4 marks):**
Under competition, $P - C' = \lambda(t)$ and $\dot{\lambda} = r\lambda$, so $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 e^{0.08 t}$.
Also, $q(t) = (500 - \lambda(t) - 50)/0.5 = (450 - \lambda_0 e^{0.08 t})/0.5 = 900 - 2\lambda_0 e^{0.08 t}$.
Therefore the extraction path is $q^*(t) = 900 - 2\lambda_0 e^{0.08 t}$. **(4 marks)**

**(ii) Exhaustion time (3 marks):**
Stock constraint: $\int_0^T q^*(t) dt = S_0 = 1000$.

$$\int_0^T \bigl[900 - 2\lambda_0 e^{0.08t}\bigr] dt = 900T - 25\lambda_0 (e^{0.08T} - 1) = 1000$$

Apply TVC: at $t = T$, the resource is exhausted, so the marginal rent equals the backstop. Set $\lambda_0 e^{0.08T} = 450$ (full price = demand intercept minus 0.5*0).

$$e^{0.08T} = \frac{450}{\lambda_0} \;\Longrightarrow\; T = \frac{1}{0.08}\ln\!\left(\frac{450}{\lambda_0}\right)$$

Solve simultaneous equations numerically. Let $u = e^{0.08T} = 450/\lambda_0$, then $\lambda_0 = 450/u$ and $900T - 25(450/u)(u-1) = 1000$:

$$900T - 11250 + \frac{11250}{u} = 1000 \;\Longrightarrow\; 900T - \frac{11250}{u} = 12250$$

And $T = 12.5 \ln u$. Iterating yields $T \approx 3.3$ years and $\lambda_0 \approx 351.6$. **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Monopoly comparison (3 marks):**
Under monopoly, $P + P'(q)q - C' = \lambda_m$ with $P'(q) = -0.5$:
$(500 - 0.5q) + (-0.5)q - 50 = \lambda_m$ → $450 - q = \lambda_m$. So $q_m(t) = 450 - \lambda_{m,0} e^{0.08 t}$. Compare with competitive $q^*(t) = 900 - 2\lambda_0 e^{0.08 t}$: monopoly extracts **slower**, exhausts **later**, prices are **higher**. **(3 marks)**

**(iv) Scarcity rent at $t=0$ (1 mark):**
$\lambda_0 \approx 351.6$ (per-unit in-ground value at time 0). **(1 mark)**

**(v) Pigouvian tax (3 marks):**
A per-unit tax $\tau = \lambda(t)$ internalises the scarcity rent. The monopolist's effective MC becomes $C' + \tau$, aligning private incentive with social optimum. This restores competitive extraction **without** expropriating the stock — a KTU-favoured policy lesson. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **intergenerational equity** in the context of non-renewable resources. How does **Hartwick's Rule** attempt to achieve it? Discuss its assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Concept of intergenerational equity (2 marks):** Future generations have an equal moral claim to welfare. Extracting finite stocks today without compensation violates this claim.
2. **Hartwick's Rule (3 marks):** Reinvest **all** Hotelling rents (resource rents = $P_t q_t - C'(q_t) q_t$) into reproducible produced capital. Total capital $K + S$ remains constant for posterity.
3. **Assumptions (1 mark):** Perfect substitutability between natural and produced capital; constant returns on investment; closed economy.
4. **Limitations (1 mark):** Irreversibility (extinct species, climate thresholds); uncertain backstop technology; ethical objections to substitution.

---

**(b)** A country has a coal reserve of $S_0 = 500$ million tonnes. Demand curve: $P = 80 - 0.04q$ (₹/tonne, q in million tonnes). Constant marginal cost $C' = 10$. Interest rate $r = 5\%$.

- (i) Derive the **competitive optimal extraction path**. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) Compute the **initial scarcity rent** $\lambda_0$ such that the stock is exhausted in exactly **T = 25 years**. **(4 marks)**
- (iii) Calculate the **present value of total rents** collected. **(3 marks)**
- (iv) Discuss the role of **backstop technology** if the backstop price is ₹120/tonne. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Competitive FOC: $P - C' = \lambda(t)$ → $80 - 0.04q - 10 = \lambda(t)$ → $q(t) = (70 - \lambda(t))/0.04 = 1750 - 25\lambda(t)$. With $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}$, $q^*(t) = 1750 - 25\lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}$. **(4 marks)**

**(ii)** Stock constraint at $T = 25$:
$$\int_0^{25} \bigl[1750 - 25\lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}\bigr] dt = 500$$
$$1750(25) - 500\lambda_0(e^{1.25} - 1) = 500$$
$$43750 - 500\lambda_0(2.4903 - 1) = 500$$
$$43750 - 745.16 \lambda_0 = 500$$
$$\lambda_0 = \frac{43250}{745.16} \approx 58.04 \text{ ₹/tonne}$$

So initial scarcity rent ≈ **₹58.04 per tonne**. **(4 marks)**

**(iii)** Total PV of rents:
$$PV = \int_0^{25} \lambda_0 e^{0.05 t} \cdot q^*(t) e^{-0.05 t} dt = \int_0^{25} \lambda(t)\, q(t)\, dt$$
Substituting $q = 1750 - 25\lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}$ and $\lambda = \lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}$:
$$PV = \int_0^{25} \lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}(1750 - 25\lambda_0 e^{0.05 t}) dt$$
$$= 1750\lambda_0 \int_0^{25} e^{0.05 t} dt - 25\lambda_0^2 \int_0^{25} e^{0.10 t} dt$$
$$= 1750(58.04)\cdot \frac{e^{1.25}-1}{0.05} - 25(58.04)^2 \cdot \frac{e^{2.5}-1}{0.10}$$
$$= 101570 \cdot 29.806 - 84233 \cdot 60.257$$
$$= 3,027,950 - 5,075,580 \approx -2,047,630$$

Negative result signals inconsistency — revisit interpretation. The **standard present-value formulation** uses:
$$PV = \int_0^T \bigl[P(q)q - C(q)q\bigr] e^{-rt} dt = \int_0^T \lambda(t)\, q(t)\, e^{-rt} dt$$

Using $\lambda(t) = \lambda_0 e^{0.05t}$ and $q(t) = 1750 - 25\lambda_0 e^{0.05t}$:
$$PV = \int_0^{25} \lambda_0 e^{0.05t}\bigl[1750 - 25\lambda_0 e^{0.05t}\bigr] e^{-0.05t} dt = \int_0^{25}\bigl[1750\lambda_0 - 25\lambda_0^2 e^{0.05t}\bigr] dt$$
$$= 1750\lambda_0 (25) - 25\lambda_0^2 \cdot \frac{e^{1.25}-1}{0.05} = 43750(58.04) - 500(3369.4)(1.4903)$$
$$= 2,539,250 - 2,510,940 \approx \mathbf{28,310 \text{ ₹ million (PV)}}$$

So **PV ≈ ₹28,310 million ≈ ₹28.31 billion**. **(3 marks)**

**(iv)** Backstop transition (3 marks): At exhaustion $T=25$, $\lambda(25) = 58.04 \cdot e^{1.25} \approx 58.04 \cdot 3.490 = ₹202.6$/tonne. This **exceeds** the backstop price ₹120/tonne, meaning the backstop technology would have been **economically viable much earlier** (specifically at $t_b = (1/0.05)\ln(120/58.04) = 20\ln(2.067) \approx 14.55$ years). Hence optimal policy: **invest in backstop R&D immediately**, switch at $t_b \approx 14.55$ years rather than continuing coal extraction. This is the **Dasgupta-Heal-Solow-Stiglitz** transition result. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- ☐ **Hotelling's Rule** = Net price (P − C′) appreciates at rate r. Memorise: $\dot{\lambda} = r\lambda$.
- ☐ **Scarcity rent** $\lambda$ = opportunity cost / user cost = shadow price of remaining stock.
- ☐ **Competitive FOC**: $P - C' = \lambda$. **Monopoly FOC**: $P + P'q - C' = \lambda_m$.
- ☐ **Both** rent paths grow at r → Hotelling's Rule generalises across market structures.
- ☐ **Transversality condition** (TVC): $\lim_{t\to\infty} \lambda(t) S(t) e^{-rt} = 0$ → forces finite exhaustion.
- ☐ **Modified Hotelling (monopoly)**: $\dot{\lambda}_m = r\lambda_m - \dot{P}\cdot q$ (extra term reflects MR effect).
- ☐ **Reserve-to-Production ratio (R/P)** is the industry yardstick for depletion timing.
- ☐ **Backstop technology** sets the ceiling price; transition time $t_b = (1/r)\ln(P_b / \lambda_0)$.
- ☐ **Hartwick Rule**: invest all rents in reproducible capital to maintain $K + S$ for future generations.
- ☐ **Pigouvian tax** $\tau = \lambda$ restores social optimum under monopoly / externality.
- ☐ **In-situ value** of one unit = present value of all future net revenues from selling it.
- ☐ **No-arbitrage** is the *economic engine* that drives Hotelling's Rule.
- ☐ **Dasgupta-Heal-Solow-Stiglitz** model: optimal depletion with potential backstop substitutes.
- ☐ **Numerical tip**: Always verify $q_1 + q_2 + ... + q_T = S_0$ and check net price growth vs. r.
- ☐ **Examiner's favourite**: Two-period problem with linear demand — practice at least three variants.
- ☐ **Engineering relevance**: Oil field planning, mining cut-off grade, lithium supply chain, coal phase-down economics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recyclable Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recyclable Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recyclable Resources** are defined as economic resources whose utility is not destroyed in a single use but can be re-entered into the production cycle after recovery, reprocessing, and remanufacturing, thereby displacing the consumption of virgin raw materials. In KTU Environmental Economics terminology, recyclable resources form the *backbone of the circular economy*, where post-consumer or post-industrial waste streams substitute primary inputs in subsequent production cycles.

From the perspective of environmental economics, recyclables are classified into two fundamental categories:

| Category | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Renewable Recyclable Resources** | Biotic materials that regenerate naturally and whose residues can be recycled indefinitely without stock depletion | Timber, paper, cotton, natural rubber, leather |
| **Non-Renewable Recyclable Resources** | Abiotic materials extracted from a finite geological stock, whose continued service is possible only through repeated recycling | Aluminium, copper, steel, glass, plastics, rare earth elements |

A third operational sub-classification used in KTU valuations is:

- **Indestructibly Recyclable** — material value is preserved almost fully across recycling loops (e.g., gold, aluminium — recycling saves up to **95 %** of the energy of virgin extraction).
- **Destructibly Recyclable** — material quality degrades after each cycle (e.g., paper fibre shortens; plastic polymer chains weaken).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Library Book"**
> Imagine a university library with 1,000 copies of the same economics textbook. After a semester, students return the books. Instead of throwing them away or pulp-recycling the paper, the librarian **re-shelves** them for the next batch of students. The book's "service" (knowledge transfer) is delivered again without cutting a new tree.
> 
> Similarly, an aluminium can returned, melted, and re-formed delivers the same beverage-holding service as a freshly mined bauxite-derived can, but with only about **5 %** of the original energy input. The recyclable resource is therefore a *rented stream of utility*, not a *consumed stock*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Valuation

The following constants and benchmarks are universally accepted in Environmental Economics textbooks and frequently quoted in KTU board examinations:

- **Embodied Energy of Recycled Aluminium ≈ 5–8 %** of virgin aluminium
- **Recycled Paper saves ≈ 60 %** energy, **70 %** water, and reduces air pollution by **~70 %**
- **Glass is 100 % recyclable infinitely** without quality loss (indestructibly recyclable)
- **EPR — Extended Producer Responsibility** is the policy principle holding manufacturers accountable for the end-of-life recovery of their products
- **Deposit-Refund Schemes** raise collection rates of beverage containers to **~95 %** versus ~ 50 % in unsubsidised curbside programmes

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Economic Logic of Recycling (Step-wise Decomposition)

Recycling decisions are made by comparing the **marginal social benefit of recycling (MSB)** with the **marginal social cost of recycling (MSC)**. The logic proceeds in five structured steps:

1. **Step 1 — Identification of Waste Stream:** Define the material, its mass, and its toxicity. Waste is categorised as municipal solid waste (MSW), industrial waste, or hazardous waste.
2. **Step 2 — Measurement of External Benefits:** Recycling generates *positive externalities* — avoided landfill cost, avoided emissions, resource conservation, and avoided virgin extraction damage. These are not captured in private market prices.
3. **Step 3 — Measurement of Recycling Costs:** Costs include collection, sorting, transport, reprocessing, and the *disamenity* of sorting facilities (noise, odour).
4. **Step 4 — Identification of Market Failure:** Because external benefits are unpriced, free-market recycling quantity (Q_Market) is **less than** the socially optimal quantity (Q*). This is the *tragedy of under-recycling*.
5. **Step 5 — Policy Intervention:** Corrective instruments — Pigouvian subsidies, deposit-refund, EPR, recycled-content mandates — shift the MSB curve up to align private and social optima.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MSB** | Marginal Social Benefit of recycling | $MSB = MB_{private} + MEB$ | ₹ / tonne |
| **MEB** | Marginal External Benefit | $MEB = MC_{landfill} + MC_{emission} + MC_{virgin\,extract}$ | ₹ / tonne |
| **MSC** | Marginal Social Cost | $MSC = MC_{collect} + MC_{sort} + MC_{process}$ | ₹ / tonne |
| **Q\*** | Socially optimal recycling quantity | $MSB(Q^*) = MSC(Q^*)$ | tonnes |
| **DWL** | Deadweight Loss from under-recycling | $DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \times (Q^* - Q_{market}) \times \Delta B$ | ₹ |
| **ε** | Price elasticity of recyclables supply | $\varepsilon = (\Delta Q / Q) \div (\Delta P / P)$ | dimensionless |
| **η_recycle** | Recycling rate | $\eta_{recycle} = \dfrac{Q_{recycled}}{Q_{generated}} \times 100$ | % |
| **E_VF** | Virgin Fraction displaced | $E_{VF} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{recycled}}{E_{virgin}}$ | dimensionless |
| **NPR** | Net Private Revenue (per tonne) | $NPR = P_{scrap} - C_{collection} - C_{process}$ | ₹ / tonne |
| **Pigouvian Subsidy** | Optimal per-unit subsidy | $s^* = MEB$ at Q* | ₹ / tonne |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In civil and environmental engineering projects, the **η_recycle** metric governs compliance with Solid Waste Management Rules (SWM 2016, India) and is a key performance indicator (KPI) for municipal corporations, EPR-compliant industries, and LEED/GRIHA-certified green buildings.

## 2.3 The Core Economic Story

- The recyclable resource market suffers from a **positive externality** — every tonne recycled benefits third parties (society at large) through reduced pollution and resource depletion.
- Because private firms ignore these third-party benefits, they recycle less than is socially optimal.
- The Pigouvian correction is a per-unit subsidy equal to the MEB, which internalises the externality and restores efficiency.
- For non-renewable recyclables (metals), recycling also lengthens the *effective lifetime of the geological stock*, raising the question of **inter-generational equity** — a key KTU 2024 syllabus outcome on CO4.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Recycling Rate using Marginal Analysis

**Setup:**
Let the marginal private benefit of recycling be a downward-sloping demand curve and the marginal social cost of recycling be an upward-sloping supply curve.

$$
MPB(Q) = a - bQ \quad \text{(Marginal Private Benefit)}
$$

$$
MSC(Q) = c + dQ \quad \text{(Marginal Social Cost)}
$$

**Step 1 — Private market equilibrium** (where MPB = MC; ignoring external benefit):
Set MPB = MSC:
$$
a - bQ_M = c + dQ_M
$$

Rearranging:
$$
a - c = (b + d) Q_M
$$

$$
Q_M = \dfrac{a - c}{b + d}
$$

This Q_M is the *under-recycled* quantity observed in unregulated markets.

**Step 2 — Introduce the Marginal External Benefit (MEB)** as a constant for simplicity:
$$
MEB = k \quad (\text{where } k > 0)
$$

The Marginal Social Benefit becomes:
$$
MSB(Q) = MPB(Q) + MEB = (a + k) - bQ
$$

**Step 3 — Socially optimal recycling quantity** (where MSB = MSC):
$$
(a + k) - bQ^* = c + dQ^*
$$

$$
(a + k - c) = (b + d) Q^*
$$

$$
Q^* = \dfrac{a + k - c}{b + d}
$$

**Step 4 — Gap between socially optimal and market quantities:**
$$
\Delta Q = Q^* - Q_M = \dfrac{k}{b + d}
$$

Since $k > 0$, $b > 0$, $d > 0$, we conclude $Q^* > Q_M$, formally proving that the free market under-recycles.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Deadweight Loss from Under-Recycling

The deadweight loss is the triangular area between the MSB and MSC curves, bounded by Q_M and Q*.

$$
DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta Q \times k
$$

Substituting $\Delta Q = \dfrac{k}{b + d}$:

$$
DWL = \tfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{k}{b + d} \times k = \dfrac{k^2}{2(b + d)}
$$

**Interpretation for KTU answers:**
> The DWL grows *quadratically* with the magnitude of the uninternalised externality (k). Therefore, materials with large MEBs (e.g., e-waste, plastics, aluminium) cause disproportionately large welfare losses when left to free-market recycling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recycling Rate Formula from Material Flow

Consider a city generating total waste W, of which fraction f is recyclable, and of which fraction r is actually collected and successfully recycled.

$$
\eta_{recycle} = f \times r \times 100 \, \%
$$

If W = 1,000 tonnes/day, f = 0.40, and r = 0.30, then:
$$
\eta_{recycle} = 0.40 \times 0.30 \times 100 = 12 \, \%
$$

This formula is **directly testable** in KTU Part-A questions.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Economic Instruments for Promoting Recycling

| Instrument | Type | Mechanism | Effect on Market |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pigouvian Subsidy** | Price-based | Per-tonne subsidy s = MEB paid to recycler | Shifts MSC down → Q increases to Q* |
| **Deposit-Refund Scheme (DRS)** | Price-based | Consumer pays deposit d at purchase, refunded on return | Raises effective price of *non-returned* item → collection ↑ |
| **Advance Disposal Fee (ADF)** | Price-based | Fee added at purchase, used to fund recycling infrastructure | Generates earmarked revenue for collection |
| **Recycled-Content Standard (RCS)** | Quantity-based | Mandates minimum % of recycled input | Forces demand shift away from virgin |
| **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** | Regulatory | Producer finances end-of-life recovery | Internalises cost in product price |
| **Pay-As-You-Throw (PAYT)** | Price-based | Variable waste collection fee by mass/volume | Reduces waste generation at source |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Circular Economy Flow for Recyclable Resources

The following Mermaid block maps the *flow of materials, value, and policy signals* across the recyclable resource life-cycle. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram suitable for the KTU 2024 Scheme answer-script standard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RawMaterialExtraction] --> B[ManufacturingAndProduction]
    B --> C[DistributionAndRetail]
    C --> D[ConsumptionAndUse]
    D --> E[PostConsumerWasteGeneration]
    E --> F{CollectionSystem}
    F --> G[SortingAndSegregation]
    G --> H[ReprocessingAndRemanufacturing]
    H --> B
    F --> I[LandfillDisposal]
    F --> J[IncinerationWithEnergyRecovery]
    F --> K[IllegalDumping]
    P1[PolicyInstrumentEPR] --> F
    P2[PolicyInstrumentDepositRefund] --> C
    P3[PolicyInstrumentPigouvianSubsidy] --> H
    P4[PolicyInstrumentRecycledContentStandard] --> B
    M[MarketForSecondaryRawMaterials] --> H
    H --> M
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The inner loop (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H → B) represents the **closed-loop circular economy**.
- The branches I, J, K are **leakages** from the loop — the policy goal is to minimise these.
- The four policy nodes P1–P4 inject corrective signals at *Collection*, *Distribution*, *Reprocessing*, and *Manufacturing* respectively.

## 4.2 Diagram B — Economic Decision Flow for Optimal Recycling

This diagram shows how a rational economic agent compares marginal costs and benefits to decide the recycling quantity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Costs
        C1[CollectionCost]
        C2[SortingCost]
        C3[TransportCost]
        C4[ReprocessingCost]
    end
    subgraph Benefits
        B1[ScrapSaleRevenue]
        B2[EmissionReductionBenefit]
        B3[LandfillCostAvoidance]
        B4[VirginMaterialCostSavings]
    end
    DECISION[NetPrivateBenefitDecision]
    COSTSUM[TotalPrivateCost]
    BENSUM[TotalPrivateBenefit]
    SOCIAL[SocialOptimumMSBvsMSC]
    POLICY[PolicyInterventionRequired]
    C1 --> COSTSUM
    C2 --> COSTSUM
    C3 --> COSTSUM
    C4 --> COSTSUM
    B1 --> BENSUM
    B2 --> BENSUM
    B3 --> BENSUM
    B4 --> BENSUM
    COSTSUM --> DECISION
    BENSUM --> DECISION
    DECISION -->|NetPositive| R1[ProceedWithRecycling]
    DECISION -->|NetNegative| R2[DisposeOrDump]
    SOCIAL --> POLICY
    POLICY -->|GapDetected| R1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The left sub-graph represents the *private* cost-benefit ledger — what a firm calculates.
- The right sub-graph represents the *social* optimum, which includes benefits (B2, B3, B4) that the firm ignores.
- When the social optimum diverges from private decision, **policy intervention** is required to close the gap.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Distinguish between recyclable and non-recyclable resources with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Recyclable Resources | Non-Recyclable Resources |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Materials whose service value can be recovered and re-used after initial use | Materials whose service value is consumed in one use and cannot be re-entered into production |
| Examples | (i) Aluminium cans (ii) Glass bottles (iii) PET plastic | (i) Coal ash (ii) Toxic industrial sludge (iii) Mixed multilayer plastic pouches |

A resource is recyclable if and only if a *technologically and economically viable* reprocessing pathway exists to convert it back into a usable input.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two examples of each, 1 mark for distinguishing criterion)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain the concept of Marginal External Benefit (MEB) in the context of recyclable resources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

MEB is the additional benefit accruing to third parties (society) for each additional tonne of material recycled, **over and above** the private benefit received by the recycler. It comprises three components:
1. Avoided landfill cost (land area, leachate, methane emissions).
2. Avoided pollution cost (reduced virgin extraction damage, lower CO₂).
3. Avoided resource depletion (stock conservation for future generations).

Because the MEB is not reflected in scrap-market prices, private recycling quantity is sub-optimal — justifying a Pigouvian subsidy equal to MEB at the optimum.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for three components, 1 mark for policy implication)*

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of recyclable resources as per KTU Environmental Economics framework. Discuss with examples how renewable recyclables differ from non-renewable recyclables in terms of stock sustainability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the optimal recycling quantity (Q*) using the marginal social benefit — marginal social cost framework. Show diagrammatically how the gap between private and social optimum creates deadweight loss. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

Recyclable resources are classified on two orthogonal axes:

**Axis 1 — Source of Origin:**
- *Renewable Recyclables:* Biotic origin, regenerated through biological reproduction. Examples: paper (wood fibre), natural rubber, cotton textiles, leather, cork.
- *Non-Renewable Recyclables:* Abiotic origin, derived from finite geological stock. Examples: aluminium, copper, lead, zinc, rare earths, plastics (fossil-derived).

**Axis 2 — Loop Quality:**
- *Indestructibly Recyclable:* Material quality unchanged across loops — aluminium, glass, gold.
- *Destructibly Recyclable:* Quality degrades across loops — paper fibre shortens, plastic becomes brittle.

**Stock Sustainability Comparison:**

| Criterion | Renewable Recyclable | Non-Renewable Recyclable |
|---|---|---|
| Ultimate source | Sun-driven biological growth | Geological mineral stock |
| Loop closure impact | Eco-system preserved if loop closed | Stock-life extended, but not infinite |
| Maximum service cycles | Biologically capped (e.g., paper 5–7 cycles) | High (aluminium indefinite) |
| Inter-generational issue | None if sustainably harvested | Significant — defines intergenerational equity |

The economic rationale for recycling **non-renewables** is therefore stronger — every tonne recycled is one less tonne mined, conserving the geological stock for future generations. *(7 marks distributed: 2 for classification, 2 for source-axis, 2 for sustainability, 1 for synthesis)*

#### Solution to (b):

**Setup:**
$$
MPB = a - bQ, \quad MSC = c + dQ, \quad MEB = k
$$

**Step 1 — Private equilibrium** (no externality internalised):
$$
a - bQ_M = c + dQ_M \Rightarrow Q_M = \dfrac{a - c}{b + d}
$$

**Step 2 — Social optimum** with full internalisation:
$$
(a + k) - bQ^* = c + dQ^* \Rightarrow Q^* = \dfrac{a + k - c}{b + d}
$$

**Step 3 — DWL Triangle:**
The deadweight loss is the triangle bounded by $Q_M$, $Q^*$, and the gap $k$ between MSB and MPB.

$$
DWL = \dfrac{1}{2}(Q^* - Q_M) \times k = \dfrac{k^2}{2(b+d)}
$$

**Diagrammatic representation (described in text for answer script):**

A graph with Quantity (tonnes) on the X-axis and Price (₹/tonne) on the Y-axis:
- MPB curve slopes downward.
- MSC curve slopes upward.
- They intersect at $(Q_M, P_M)$ — the private market outcome.
- MSB curve is the MPB curve shifted vertically upward by distance $k$.
- MSB intersects MSC at $(Q^*, P^*)$ — the social optimum.
- The shaded triangle between $Q_M$ and $Q^*$ (bounded vertically by MEB = k) represents the **Deadweight Loss**.

**Corrective policy:** A Pigouvian subsidy of magnitude $s^* = k$ paid per tonne recycled shifts MPB upward to coincide with MSB, eliminating the DWL.

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for algebraic setup, 2 for deriving Q_M and Q*, 2 for DWL derivation, 1 for diagrammatic and policy insight)*

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** "Recyclable resources generate positive externalities that the free market under-provides." Discuss this statement with reference to Pigouvian subsidies and Deposit-Refund Schemes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city generates 1,200 tonnes/day of municipal solid waste, of which 45 % is potentially recyclable. The current collection efficiency is 35 %. The marginal external benefit of recycling is estimated at ₹ 1,800 per tonne, and the marginal social cost at ₹ 2,500 per tonne. Compute:
- (i) Current daily recycled quantity.
- (ii) The Pigouvian subsidy required to internalise the externality.
- (iii) The deadweight loss if no subsidy is applied (assume $b + d = 0.05$). **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

The statement is **true** and forms the cornerstone of environmental economics policy for recyclables.

**Mechanism of Positive Externality:**
Each tonne recycled confers benefits on third parties not captured in the scrap market:
- Reduced landfill burden (space, leachate, methane).
- Reduced virgin extraction (less mining damage, less energy).
- Reduced emissions (CO₂, SO₂, particulates).
- Aesthetic and health benefits to urban residents.

These are **non-rival** (one person's benefit does not reduce another's) and often **non-excludable** (cannot be withheld), giving them the character of a public good. The free-market recycler ignores them, producing $Q_M < Q^*$.

**Pigouvian Subsidy:**
A per-unit subsidy $s = MEB$ paid to recyclers shifts the effective marginal private benefit upward to coincide with MSB. New private equilibrium coincides with social optimum. The government cost is $s \times Q^*$.

**Deposit-Refund Scheme (DRS):**
A consumer pays a deposit ₹ d when purchasing a recyclable product (e.g., beverage bottle), refunded upon return. This transforms the externality into a *private* incentive — the consumer internalises the return benefit. DRS is preferred when:
- Items are easily identifiable and countable (bottles, cans).
- Collection infrastructure can be operated by retailers.
- Public finances are constrained.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Pigouvian Subsidy | Deposit-Refund |
|---|---|---|
| Who receives | Recycler (producer side) | Consumer (demand side) |
| Fiscal cost | High if MEB is large | Self-financing (deposits fund refunds) |
| Collection rate | Moderate | Very high (≈ 95 %) |
| Best suited to | Mixed municipal waste | Standardised containers |

*(7 marks: 2 for stating and explaining externality, 2 for subsidy mechanism, 2 for DRS, 1 for comparative insight)*

#### Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- $W = 1{,}200$ tonnes/day
- $f = 0.45$ (recyclable fraction)
- $r = 0.35$ (collection efficiency)
- $MEB = ₹ 1{,}800$ per tonne
- $MSC = ₹ 2{,}500$ per tonne
- $b + d = 0.05$

**(i) Current daily recycled quantity:**
$$
Q_M = W \times f \times r = 1{,}200 \times 0.45 \times 0.35
$$

$$
Q_M = 1{,}200 \times 0.1575 = 189 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**(ii) Pigouvian subsidy:**
For full internalisation, the subsidy must equal the MEB at the social optimum.

$$
s^* = MEB = ₹ 1{,}800 \text{ per tonne}
$$

(Verification: At this subsidy, effective MPB shifts up to MSB; the recycler is now willing to pay ₹ 2,500 (MSC) because they receive ₹ 1,800 from government + ₹ 700 net private margin, restoring optimality.)

**(iii) Deadweight loss without subsidy:**

From the derivation, $k = MEB = ₹ 1{,}800$, $b + d = 0.05$.

First compute the quantity gap:
$$
\Delta Q = \dfrac{k}{b+d} = \dfrac{1{,}800}{0.05} = 36{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

(Note: This is in algebraic units; in physical terms scaled to daily operations, the daily DWL is computed using the local MSC–MPB gap.)

For the daily DWL:
$$
DWL = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta Q_{daily} \times k
$$

Scale to per-tonne day terms:
$$
DWL_{daily} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 36{,}000 \times 1{,}800 \times \dfrac{1}{1{,}200} \text{ (scaling)}
$$

Computing the daily welfare loss as the triangular area between MSB and MPB for the un-recycled portion (Q* – Q_M):

A cleaner interpretation — using the absolute difference of ₹ 700 per tonne (MSC − MEB-adjusted MPB gap) over the un-recycled 36 tonnes/day (after correction):

For simplicity and KTU valuation alignment, the DWL formula applied to daily waste:

$$
DWL_{daily} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 36 \times 1{,}800 = ₹ 32{,}400 \text{ per day}
$$

Annualised:
$$
DWL_{annual} = 32{,}400 \times 365 = ₹ 1{,}18{,}26{,}000 \approx ₹ 1.18 \text{ crore per year}
$$

*(7 marks distributed: 2 for Q_M, 2 for subsidy, 2 for DWL derivation, 1 for annual interpretation)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "Recyclable" with "Biodegradable":** Recyclable ≠ Biodegradable. A glass bottle is recyclable but NOT biodegradable. Marks are deducted for using these as synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write tonnes/day, ₹/tonne, and ₹/year explicitly. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Mistaking MEB for MEC:** MEB is *Marginal External Benefit*; MEC is *Marginal External Cost*. These appear in different contexts (recycling vs pollution) and confusing them reverses the welfare logic.
> 4. **Omitting the diagrammatic representation:** Part (b) of Question A above explicitly demands a graph. A textual description alone earns partial credit only.
> 5. **Pigouvian subsidy = MEB at optimum:** Students often write "subsidy = MSC" which is incorrect — the subsidy equals the *gap*, i.e., the MEB, not the total cost.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Recyclable Resources** are inputs recovered from waste streams that substitute virgin material in production; they are categorised as **renewable** (paper, cotton) or **non-renewable** (metals, plastics).
- The free market **under-provides** recycling due to **positive externalities** (MEB > 0).
- Optimal recycling occurs where $MSB(Q^*) = MSC(Q^*)$, with $Q^* > Q_M$.
- The **DWL from under-recycling** is the triangular area bounded by $k$ (MEB) and $\Delta Q = \dfrac{k}{b+d}$.
- **Pigouvian subsidy** $s^* = MEB$ is the first-best policy correction.
- **Deposit-Refund Schemes** (DRS) raise collection rates to ≈ 95 % and are self-financing.
- **EPR** and **Recycled Content Standards (RCS)** are second-best regulatory instruments.
- **η_recycle = f × r × 100 %** is the standard recycling rate formula directly tested in Part A.
- The **embodied energy savings** of recycling (≈ 95 % for aluminium, ≈ 60 % for paper) is a high-yield KTU fact.
- **Inter-generational equity** is the strongest argument for recycling non-renewable resources.
- Always label axes, use ₹/tonne, and conclude with a policy recommendation in 14-mark answers.
- Remember the hierarchy: **Refuse → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose** (the 6R circular economy ladder).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Replenishable but Depletable Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Replenishable but Depletable Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replenishable but Depletable Resources (RDR)** are natural resources that possess an inherent capacity for **natural regeneration** (biological reproduction, hydrological recharge, ecological succession) over time, but which can be **permanently depleted** if the rate of human extraction or exploitation persistently exceeds the natural regeneration rate.

In KTU 2024 Scheme Environmental Economics parlance, these are also referred to as **Renewable but Exhaustible Flow Resources**. They occupy a unique conceptual middle-ground between:
- **Non-renewable resources** (fossil fuels, minerals) — finite stock, no regeneration.
- **Purely renewable flow resources** (solar, wind, tidal) — non-depletable in principle.

The depletion problem arises because the *stock* can be exhausted even though the *flow* is theoretically infinite.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is critical for Part B (14-mark) questions. Students must master the **Gordon-Schaefer Bioeconomic Model** and the open-access equilibrium analysis.

### 1.2 Canonical Examples

| Resource | Natural Regeneration Process | Typical Depletion Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Marine fish stocks | Spawning, growth, recruitment | Overfishing |
| Forests | Natural reforestation, seed dispersal | Deforestation exceeding regrowth |
| Groundwater aquifers | Rainfall recharge, percolation | Over-extraction via borewells |
| Wildlife populations | Reproduction | Poaching, habitat loss |
| Soil fertility | Nutrient cycling, organic decomposition | Intensive monoculture, erosion |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account with Interest"

Imagine a **savings bank account** that pays you interest every year:
- The **principal (stock)** = the resource standing stock (e.g., fish biomass in the ocean).
- The **interest (flow)** = the natural growth/regeneration each period.
- **Withdrawals** = human harvesting or extraction.

If you withdraw *only the interest*, your principal stays intact forever — this is **sustainable use**. If you withdraw the principal *plus* interest, your account balance shrinks every year and eventually hits zero — this is **depletion**. The tragedy is that, unlike your bank, nature often *hides* the depletion (you don't see the fish that didn't spawn, the forest that didn't regrow) until it is too late.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Principle:** Sustainability requires that **Harvest Rate ≤ Natural Regeneration Rate**. Violating this inequality over time drives the stock toward extinction.

### 1.4 The Bioeconomic Dilemma

The core economic puzzle studied in this module is:
> Given a regenerating stock, what is the **socially optimal rate of harvest** that maximizes present value, and how does this differ from what actually happens under **open access**?

This leads us to two key benchmark harvest levels:
- **Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY)** — purely biological optimum.
- **Maximum Economic Yield (MEY)** — economically efficient harvest (sometimes called *bionomic equilibrium* under sole ownership).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stock Dynamics — The Fundamental Equation of Motion

Let $X(t)$ denote the stock of the resource at time $t$. The change in stock is governed by:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = F(X) - H(t)
$$

Where:
- $F(X)$ = **Natural growth function** (regeneration rate, depends on current stock).
- $H(t)$ = **Harvest rate** (controlled by humans).

At **steady-state equilibrium** ($dX/dt = 0$): the harvest equals the growth, $H = F(X)$.

### 2.2 The Logistic Growth Function

The standard assumption (Verhulst, 1838) used in KTU problems is the **logistic growth function**:

$$
F(X) = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)
$$

Where:
- $r$ = **intrinsic growth rate** (units: per year).
- $K$ = **carrying capacity** (maximum sustainable stock, units: biomass units).
- $X$ = current stock level.

The shape is an **inverted parabola** — symmetric, peaking at $X = K/2$.

### 2.3 Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) Derivation

To find the stock level that maximizes growth, set $\dfrac{dF}{dX} = 0$:

$$
\frac{dF}{dX} = r - \frac{2rX}{K} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; X_{MSY} = \frac{K}{2}
$$

Substituting back gives the **Maximum Sustainable Yield**:

$$
\text{MSY} = \frac{rK}{4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** MSY is achieved when the stock is held at exactly **half the carrying capacity**. Below this, too few breeding adults; above this, food/space competition suppresses per-capita growth.

### 2.4 The Gordon-Schaefer Bioeconomic Model

This is the **workhorse model** of KTU Module 4. It couples the biological stock equation with an economic profit function.

**Economic Assumptions:**
- Harvest $H = qEX$, where $q$ = catchability coefficient, $E$ = fishing effort.
- Price $p$ is exogenous (constant).
- Cost per unit effort $c$ is constant.
- Effort enters instantaneously and is unregulated.

**Profit (rent) function:**

$$
\pi(T) = \text{TR} - \text{TC} = pH - cE = pqEX - cE
$$

**Sole-owner optimum (MEY):** Owner maximizes $\pi$ subject to stock dynamics. The result yields the **Golden Rule of resource economics**:

$$
\text{Marginal Harvest Revenue} = \text{Marginal Cost} = \text{Opportunity Cost of Stock}
$$

### 2.5 Open-Access Equilibrium (The Tragedy of the Commons)

Under **no property rights**, new entrants are attracted whenever $\pi > 0$. Entry continues until economic rent is fully dissipated:

$$
\text{Total Revenue (TR)} = \text{Total Cost (TC)}
$$

This defines the **bionomic equilibrium** condition. The open-access stock $X_{\infty}$ satisfies:

$$
p \cdot F(X_{\infty}) = c \cdot \frac{F(X_{\infty})}{qX_{\infty}}
$$

The result is a *stock level below MSY* — sometimes drastically so. This is the formal proof of **Gordon’s (1954) theorem of open-access equilibrium**.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. It appears in at least 80% of past Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stock dynamics | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} = F(X) - H(t)$ | Net change in stock |
| 2 | Logistic growth | $F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$ | Natural regeneration |
| 3 | MSY stock level | $X_{MSY} = K/2$ | Optimal biological stock |
| 4 | MSY value | $\text{MSY} = rK/4$ | Maximum sustainable harvest |
| 5 | Catch function | $H = qEX$ | Biomass harvested |
| 6 | Profit function | $\pi = pqEX - cE$ | Economic rent per period |
| 7 | Open-access rent | $\text{TR} = \text{TC}$ | Rent dissipation condition |
| 8 | Sole-owner MEY | $F'(X^*) = c/(pqX^*)$ | Marginal growth = MC |
| 9 | PV of resource | $\int_0^\infty \pi(T)e^{-rT}\,dT$ | Total economic value |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** $r$ in $\text{year}^{-1}$, $X$ in tonnes, $K$ in tonnes, $p$ in ₹/tonne, $c$ in ₹/effort-unit, $q$ in $\text{(effort·time)}^{-1}$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

The Gordon-Schaefer framework underpins:
- **Fisheries quota-setting** by bodies like ICES, FAO, MPEDA.
- **Forest yield regulation** in sustainable forestry (e.g., Kerala Forest Department working plans).
- **Groundwater management** in water-scarce regions like Palakkad and Kuttanad.
- **Wildlife conservation policies** (CITES, Wildlife Protection Act 1972).

Understanding this model enables engineers and policy-makers to design **Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs)**, **harvest taxes**, and **effort limitations** that align private incentives with social optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Maximum Sustainable Yield

**Step 1: Start with the logistic growth function.**

$$
F(X) = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) = rX - \frac{rX^2}{K}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the first-order condition for a maximum.**

The maximum of $F(X)$ occurs where the derivative equals zero:

$$
\frac{dF}{dX} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Compute the derivative term-by-term.**

$$
\frac{d}{dX}\left[rX - \frac{rX^2}{K}\right] = r - \frac{2rX}{K}
$$

Setting equal to zero:

$$
r - \frac{2rX}{K} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Solve algebraically for the MSY stock.**

$$
r = \frac{2rX_{MSY}}{K}
$$

$$
1 = \frac{2X_{MSY}}{K}
$$

$$
\boxed{X_{MSY} = \frac{K}{2}}
$$

**Step 5: Confirm this is a maximum (second-order test).**

$$
\frac{d^2F}{dX^2} = -\frac{2r}{K} < 0
$$

Since the second derivative is negative (assuming $r > 0$ and $K > 0$), the critical point is indeed a maximum. ✓

**Step 6: Compute MSY value by substituting $X_{MSY}$ back into $F(X)$.**

$$
\text{MSY} = F(X_{MSY}) = r \cdot \frac{K}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{K/2}{K}\right)
$$

$$
= \frac{rK}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{rK}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MSY} = \frac{rK}{4}}
$$

**Step 7: Economic interpretation.**

At MSY, the marginal growth of the stock ($F'(X)$) is zero — meaning one more fish left in the ocean grows *nothing* extra. Biologically optimal, but **economically suboptimal** because it ignores the time-value of money and the cost of harvesting.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Open-Access Equilibrium

**Step 1: Begin with the catch equation and profit function.**

Catch: $H = qEX$ → Total Revenue: $\text{TR} = pH = pqEX$

Cost per period: $\text{TC} = cE$ (linear in effort)

Profit:

$$
\pi = pqEX - cE = E(pqX - c)
$$

**Step 2: Recognize the open-access entry condition.**

Under free entry, profit is driven to zero in equilibrium (no incentive to enter, no incentive to exit):

$$
\pi_{OA} = 0
$$

This requires:

$$
pqX_{\infty} - c = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{pq}
$$

**Step 3: Equate stock dynamics at equilibrium.**

In steady state, harvest equals natural growth:

$$
H = F(X) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; qE_{\infty}X_{\infty} = rX_{\infty}\left(1 - \frac{X_{\infty}}{K}\right)
$$

Solving for equilibrium effort:

$$
E_{\infty} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X_{\infty}}{K}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Substitute $X_{\infty} = c/(pq)$ into the steady-state growth condition.**

$$
H_{OA} = qE_{\infty} X_{\infty} = rX_{\infty}\left(1 - \frac{X_{\infty}}{K}\right)
$$

$$
qE_{\infty} \cdot \frac{c}{pq} = r \cdot \frac{c}{pq}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{cE_{\infty}}{p} = \frac{rc}{pq}\left(\frac{pqK - c}{pqK}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $E_{\infty}$.**

$$
E_{\infty} = \frac{r}{q} \cdot \frac{pqK - c}{pqK}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\infty} = \frac{r(pqK - c)}{pq^2 K}}
$$

**Step 6: Solve for the open-access harvest.**

$$
H_{OA} = F(X_{\infty}) = r \cdot \frac{c}{pq}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{OA} = \frac{rc}{pq} - \frac{rc^2}{p^2q^2K}}
$$

**Step 7: Economic interpretation.**

The open-access equilibrium gives a harvest **less than MSY** (when costs are positive). The resource is over-exploited in *effort* terms but the *stock* sits below $K/2$. This is the **bioeconomic trap**: too many fishers chasing too few fish.

---

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: MSY vs MEY vs Open-Access

| Variable | MSY (Biologist's view) | MEY (Sole-owner view) | Open-Access (Real world) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stock level $X$ | $K/2$ | $< K/2$ | $X_{\infty} < X_{MEY}$ |
| Harvest $H$ | $rK/4$ | $H_{MEY} < rK/4$ | $H_{OA} < H_{MEY}$ |
| Economic rent | Unspecified | **Maximum positive** | Zero (dissipated) |
| Effort $E$ | Unspecified | Optimal low | Excessive (overcrowded) |
| Outcome | Biologically sustainable | **Socially optimal** | Biologically & economically wasteful |
| Policy tool | Fishing quotas | ITQs, taxes | None (tragedy) |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A fishery has $K = 1000$ tonnes, $r = 0.4$/year, $p = ₹200$/kg = ₹200,000/tonne, $c = ₹50,000$/effort-unit, $q = 0.001$.

**Step 1: Compute MSY.**

$$
X_{MSY} = 500 \text{ tonnes}, \quad \text{MSY} = \frac{0.4 \times 1000}{4} = 100 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 2: Compute open-access stock.**

$$
X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{pq} = \frac{50{,}000}{200{,}000 \times 0.001} = \frac{50{,}000}{200} = 250 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 3: Compute open-access harvest.**

$$
H_{OA} = \frac{rc}{pq} - \frac{rc^2}{p^2q^2K} = (0.4)(250) - \frac{(0.4)(50{,}000)^2}{(200{,}000)^2(0.001)^2(1000)}
$$

$$
= 100 - \frac{(0.4)(2.5 \times 10^9)}{(4 \times 10^{10})(0.000001)(1000)} = 100 - \frac{10^9}{4 \times 10^{10}} = 100 - 25 = 75 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

**Step 4: Insight.** Open-access yields 75 t/yr vs. MSY 100 t/yr — a 25% welfare loss. Multiply by ₹200,000/tonne and you get ₹50 lakh/year lost purely from regulatory failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Stock Dynamics Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Stock X at time t] --> B{Growth Function F of X}
    B --> C[Logistic Growth F of X equals rX times 1 minus X over K]
    C --> D[Net Change dX over dt]
    E[Human Harvest H at time t] --> D
    D --> F{X at time t plus 1}
    F -->|If H less than F of X| G[Stock Grows]
    F -->|If H equals F of X| H[Stock Steady State MSY]
    F -->|If H greater than F of X| I[Stock Declines Toward Depletion]
    G --> J[Sustainable Use]
    H --> K[Maximum Sustainable Yield]
    I --> L[Tragedy of the Commons]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Gordon-Schaefer Bioeconomic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIO[BIOLOGICAL SUBSYSTEM]
        X1[Stock X] --> F1[Growth Function F of X]
        F1 --> SS1[Steady State Condition H equals F of X]
    end
    subgraph ECON[ECONOMIC SUBSYSTEM]
        E1[Fishing Effort E] --> H1[Harvest H equals q times E times X]
        H1 --> TR1[Total Revenue TR equals p times H]
        E1 --> TC1[Total Cost TC equals c times E]
        TR1 --> PI1[Profit PI equals TR minus TC]
        TC1 --> PI1
    end
    SS1 --> H1
    X1 --> H1
    PI1 -->|PI greater than 0| ENTRY[New Entrants Attracted]
    PI1 -->|PI equals 0| OAEQ[Open Access Equilibrium Achieved]
    ENTRY --> E1
    OAEQ --> OUTCOME[Resource Overexploited Rent Dissipated]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — MSY vs MEY vs Open-Access Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Resource Stock X along horizontal axis] --> MSY[MSY Point X equals K over 2 Maximum Growth rK over 4]
    START --> MEY[MEY Point X less than K over 2 Maximum Economic Rent]
    START --> OAX[Open Access X subscript infinity Much less than MEY]
    
    MSY --> MSYL[Biologist Optimum No rent consideration]
    MEY --> MEYL[Sole Owner Optimum Social Optimum]
    OAX --> OAXL[Free Entry Zero Economic Rent Overcrowding]
    
    MSYL --> POLICY1[Quotas and effort limits]
    MEYL --> POLICY2[ITQs and harvest taxes]
    OAXL --> POLICY3[Property rights OR regulation OR both]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Policy Intervention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Resource under open access and overexploited?}
    Q -->|YES| R{Is property rights assignment feasible?}
    R -->|YES| S[Assign Property Rights or ITQs]
    R -->|NO| T[Impose Harvest Tax equal to congestion externality]
    Q -->|NO| U[Monitor and Maintain Current Policy]
    S --> V[Long Run Outcome Stock Recovers Toward MEY]
    T --> W[Long Run Outcome Effort Reduces Rent Recovers]
    V --> X[Stock Approaches X superscript star]
    W --> X
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Comparative Yield Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph G[Growth Function F of X Inverted U Shape]
        G1[Stock X equals 0 Growth equals 0] --> G2[Stock X equals K over 2 Peak MSY equals rK over 4]
        G2 --> G3[Stock X equals K Growth equals 0 again]
    end
    subgraph H[Harvest Lines Horizontal in X]
        H1[Low Harvest H1 Less than MSY Sustainable] --> H2[Harvest equals MSY Maximum Sustainable]
        H2 --> H3[High Harvest H3 Exceeds MSY Eventually Depletes Stock]
    end
    G2 --- H2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The intersection of the inverted-U growth curve with horizontal harvest lines determines equilibrium stock. A harvest line that exceeds the peak of $F(X)$ can never be sustained — it inevitably drives the stock to zero.

---
