## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**

Distinguish between *replenishable but depletable resources* and *non-renewable resources*. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Replenishable but Depletable | Non-Renewable |
|---|---|---|
| Stock | Regenerates naturally | Fixed, finite |
| Depletion | Possible if over-harvested | Inevitable with use |
| Examples | Fish, forests, groundwater | Coal, oil, natural gas |

Examples of RDR: marine fish (sardine), tropical forest (teak).
Examples of non-renewable: petroleum, uranium ore.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *replenishable* with *inexhaustible*. Mention that solar/wind are *non-depletable flow resources*, distinct from RDR. Lose 1 mark if missed.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for distinction table + 1 mark for correct examples.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

Define *Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY)*. What stock level achieves it under the logistic growth assumption?

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MSY** is the largest constant harvest that can be taken from a stock **indefinitely** without reducing the stock over time. Under the logistic growth function $F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$, MSY is achieved at $X_{MSY} = K/2$ with a value of $\text{MSY} = rK/4$.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for the formula.

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Gordon-Schaefer bioeconomic model** of a fishery. Derive the open-access equilibrium condition and show that the open-access stock level is generally *less than* the Maximum Sustainable Yield stock.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A fishery has the following parameters: $r = 0.5$/year, $K = 800$ tonnes, $p = ₹500$/kg, $c = ₹100,000$/effort-unit, $q = 0.002$. Compute the (i) MSY, (ii) open-access equilibrium stock, and (iii) open-access equilibrium harvest.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

The Gordon-Schaefer model (1954) combines the **biological subsystem** with the **economic subsystem**:

**Biological:** Stock dynamics follow

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) - qEX
$$

**Economic:** Catch = $H = qEX$, Profit = $\pi = pqEX - cE$.

Under **open access**, free entry drives profit to zero:

$$
\pi = E(pqX - c) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{pq}
$$

For the fishery to be economically viable at all, we need $X_{\infty} < K$, which implies:

$$
\frac{c}{pq} < K \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c < pqK
$$

Comparing $X_{\infty}$ with $X_{MSY} = K/2$:

$$
X_{\infty} < X_{MSY} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \frac{c}{pq} < \frac{K}{2}
$$

i.e., the open-access stock lies **below** the MSY level whenever $c > pqK/2$. Hence over-exploitation is the rule under open access — this is **Gordon's Theorem**.

**Marks for Part (a):** 2 marks biological subsystem, 2 marks economic subsystem, 2 marks open-access derivation, 1 mark Gordon's theorem conclusion.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: $r = 0.5$, $K = 800$, $p = ₹500{,}000$/tonne, $c = ₹100{,}000$, $q = 0.002$.

**(i) MSY:**

$$
X_{MSY} = \frac{K}{2} = \frac{800}{2} = 400 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
\text{MSY} = \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{0.5 \times 800}{4} = 100 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**(ii) Open-access stock:**

$$
X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{pq} = \frac{100{,}000}{500{,}000 \times 0.002} = \frac{100{,}000}{1000} = 100 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(iii) Open-access harvest:**

$$
H_{OA} = F(X_{\infty}) = rX_{\infty}\left(1 - \frac{X_{\infty}}{K}\right)
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times 100 \times \left(1 - \frac{100}{800}\right) = 50 \times \frac{7}{8} = 43.75 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> - Mixing units: $p = ₹500$/kg is equivalent to ₹500,000/tonne. Mismatch loses 1 mark.
> - Forgetting the multiplication $pq$ in the denominator of $X_{\infty}$. Using only $c/p$ loses 2 marks.
> - Computing MSY value but forgetting the corresponding stock level $X_{MSY} = K/2$. Lose 1 mark.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare and contrast the **Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY)** concept with the **Maximum Economic Yield (MEY)** concept. Under what conditions is MEY lower than MSY? Illustrate with a sketch of the yield curve and cost curves.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **Tragedy of the Commons** as applied to replenishable resources. What policy instruments can be used to internalize the externality and restore efficiency?

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

**MSY** is a purely **biological** optimum — the largest harvest the stock can sustain indefinitely, ignoring costs and prices. It occurs where $dF/dX = 0$, i.e., $X_{MSY} = K/2$.

**MEY** is the **economic** optimum — the harvest that maximizes the present value of net economic rent, accounting for harvesting costs and discount rate. It occurs where **Marginal Revenue = Marginal Cost** (inclusive of user cost / opportunity cost of leaving fish in the sea).

**Conditions for MEY < MSY:**
1. Positive harvesting cost ($c > 0$).
2. Positive discount rate ($\delta > 0$).
3. Stock-dependent catchability.

Graphically:

```
    Yield / Revenue
         |
   MSY --|---------●  (X = K/2)
         |        /│\
   MEY --|---● / │ \
         |  /    │   \
         | /     │    \  Growth F(X)
         |/______│_____\_____
                X_MEY X_MSY   Stock X
```

The MEY stock $X_{MEY}$ lies to the **right** of $X_{MSY}$ (i.e., $X_{MEY} > K/2$), but the **MEY harvest** is *lower* than MSY because the marginal cost of harvesting larger stocks exceeds the marginal revenue from additional catch. This is **Clark's (1973, 1976)** result.

**Marks for Part (a):** 2 marks definitions, 3 marks graphical/algebraic comparison, 2 marks conditions.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Tragedy of the Commons (Hardin, 1968):**
When a replenishable resource is held in common property with no exclusion, each user captures the *full benefit* of their harvest but bears only a *fraction* of the depletion cost imposed on others. This externality drives over-entry, rent dissipation, and ultimately stock collapse.

**Symptoms in RDR context:**
- Excessive fishing effort (crowded harbours, declining CPUE).
- Stock decline below MSY.
- Zero or negative economic rent.
- Eventually biological extinction (e.g., collapse of North Atlantic cod, 1992).

**Policy Instruments to Internalize the Externality:**

| Instrument | Mechanism | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| **Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs)** | Sets total allowable catch; assigns tradable property rights | Difficult initial allocation; monitoring cost |
| **Harvest taxes** | Pigouvian tax equal to congestion externality | Requires stock & effort data |
| **Effort restrictions** | Limits on boats/nets/days at sea | Capital stuffing, disguised effort |
| **Community-based management** | Local collective action, co-management | Free-rider problem |
| **Marine Protected Areas (MPAs)** | Spatial closure | Displaces effort elsewhere |

**Marks for Part (b):** 2 marks Tragedy definition, 2 marks symptoms, 3 marks for the policy table with mechanism explanation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> - Stating Tragedy without naming Hardin (1968) or the externality mechanism. Lose 1 mark.
> - Listing policies without explaining *why* they restore efficiency (must link to internalizing the externality or assigning property rights). Lose 2 marks.
> - Confusing MEY stock location with MEY harvest level: $X_{MEY} > X_{MSY}$ but $H_{MEY} < \text{MSY}$. Mixing these loses 2 marks.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Replenishable but Depletable Resources**

- [x] RDR regenerate naturally but can be depleted by over-harvesting.
- [x] Sustainability condition: **Harvest ≤ Natural Growth**.
- [x] Logistic growth: $F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$ — symmetric inverted parabola.
- [x] **MSY** stock = $K/2$; MSY value = $rK/4$ (always memorize).
- [x] Gordon-Schaefer model: stock dynamics + profit function coupled.
- [x] **Open access** dissipates rent (TR = TC condition).
- [x] Open-access stock $X_{\infty} = c/(pq)$ is typically **below** MSY.
- [x] **MEY < MSY** in harvest terms, but $X_{MEY} > X_{MSY}$.
- [x] Hardin's Tragedy of the Commons arises from absent property rights.
- [x] Policy fixes: ITQs, taxes, MPAs, co-management, effort controls.
- [x] Real-world examples: Kerala's marine fisheries, Western Ghats forests, Palakkad groundwater.

**Memory Hook — The "4-Quadrant Rule":**

| | Biologist | Economist |
|---|---|---|
| **Without Property Rights** | Open-access biology | Rent dissipation |
| **With Property Rights** | Managed MSY | MEY (social optimum) |

**Key Numerical Constants to Memorize:** $X_{MSY}/K = 0.5$, $\text{MSY}/(rK) = 0.25$.

**Frequently Confused Pairs (Don't mix these up!):**
- $X_{MSY}$ (stock) vs $\text{MSY}$ (harvest) — different units!
- MEY stock vs MEY harvest — opposite direction of inequality vs MSY.
- Open-access equilibrium vs bioeconomic equilibrium — same point, different framing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storable Renewable Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Storable Renewable Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storable Renewable Resources** are naturally regenerating biological or ecological assets whose physical stock can be accumulated, held in reserve, and withdrawn at a chosen point in time, decoupling the moment of harvest from the moment of biological growth. The user faces both an *intertemporal allocation problem* (how fast to draw down the stock) and a *harvest-timing problem* (when to harvest), unlike flow resources which must be captured as they regenerate.

**Canonical examples** studied in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Resource | Stock Variable $S$ | Natural Growth $\dot{S}$ |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------------|
| Fishery | Biomass of fish (tonnes) | Spawning, recruitment |
| Forest | Standing timber volume (m³) | Tree diameter increment |
| Wildlife | Animal headcount | Births minus natural deaths |
| Groundwater | Aquifer volume (m³) | Recharge minus natural discharge |
| Soil quality | Topsoil depth / organic carbon | Pedogenesis |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account of Nature"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain-English Analogy):** Imagine a savings bank that pays you interest but also charges a withdrawal fee. The *principal* is the resource stock (e.g., the fish population), the *interest* is biological growth (new fish born), and the *withdrawal fee* rises as you empty the account (because fish become harder to catch). If you withdraw too aggressively, the account balance collapses and so does the interest you earn. The economic question is: **what withdrawal rate keeps the account most valuable over time?**

Three regimes emerge from this analogy:

1. **Pristine (no harvest):** Stock stays at the carrying capacity $K$, but no rent is captured.
2. **Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY):** Withdraw exactly the interest earned, keeping stock at $K/2$. Biologically stable, but ignores scarcity rent.
3. **Open-access / Tragedy of the Commons:** Everyone over-withdraws; stock collapses toward the *bionomic equilibrium* where rent vanishes.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Symbols (Board-Exam Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **$K$**: Carrying capacity, the biological ceiling — units: **tonnes**, **m³**, or **headcount**.
> - **$r$**: Intrinsic growth rate, dimensionless, typically **$0.05$ to $0.5$ per year**.
> - **$p$**: Output price per unit of harvest, units: **₹/tonne**.
> - **$c$**: Cost per unit of harvesting effort, units: **₹/boat-day** or **₹/hour**.
> - **$q$**: Catchability coefficient, converts effort into harvest per unit stock — units: **(effort·time)$^{-1}$**.
> - **$\delta$**: Social rate of discount, dimensionless per year, typically **$0.03$ to $0.10$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Building Blocks of the Bioeconomic Model

The entire module rests on **four interlocking relationships**. Every KTU question on this topic maps onto one or more of them.

**Block 1 — Biological growth law (Logistic equation of Verhulst–Pearl):**

$$\dot{S} = G(S) - H$$

where $G(S)$ is the natural growth function and $H$ is the harvest. For almost all KTU problems, $G(S)$ is the **logistic form**:

$$G(S) = r S \left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right)$$

**Block 2 — Production (harvest) function:**

$$H = q E S$$

Harvest is proportional to effort $E$, catchability $q$, and standing stock $S$.

**Block 3 — Revenue and cost:**

$$\text{TR} = p H = p q E S, \qquad \text{TC} = c E$$

**Block 4 — Economic objective (Present Value of Net Rent):**

$$\text{PV} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ p q E(t) S(t) - c E(t) \right] e^{-\delta t} dt$$

## 2.2 The Three Reference Stocks (Board-Favourite Triplet)

These three stock values are the **single most-tested concept** in this module. Memorise the derivations below.

| Regime | Stock Symbol | Formula | Economic Meaning |
|--------|--------------|---------|------------------|
| Carrying capacity | $S = K$ | — | Pristine, no harvest |
| MSY stock | $S_{MSY}$ | $\dfrac{K}{2}$ | Maximum *biological* yield |
| Open-access stock | $S_{OA}$ | $\dfrac{c}{p q}$ | Zero-rent equilibrium |
| Optimal (sole-owner) | $S^{\star}$ | $\dfrac{K}{2} + \dfrac{c}{2 p q}$ | Maximum *economic* yield |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $S_{OA} = c/(pq)$ appear in every answer key?** Because at open-access, free entry drives economic rent to zero: $p q S = c \Rightarrow S = c/(pq)$. This single equation is worth **at least 3 marks** in any Part A question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** the KTU 2024 scheme can ask on this topic. Use $\vert$ (rendered as $\mid$ or $\vert$) instead of the pipe character to preserve table layout.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Logistic growth | $G(S) = rS(1 - S/K)$ | tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | Sustainable yield (general) | $Y = G(S)$ | tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |
| 3 | MSY | $Y_{MSY} = \dfrac{rK}{4}$ | tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |
| 4 | MSY stock | $S_{MSY} = \dfrac{K}{2}$ | tonnes |
| 5 | Harvest function | $H = qES$ | tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |
| 6 | Profit (instantaneous) | $\pi(S) = r(1-S/K)(pS - c/q)$ | ₹ yr$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Open-access stock | $S_{OA} = \dfrac{c}{pq}$ | tonnes |
| 8 | Open-access effort | $E_{OA} = \dfrac{r}{q}\left(1 - \dfrac{S_{OA}}{K}\right)$ | effort-units |
| 9 | Optimal stock (sole owner) | $S^{\star} = \dfrac{K}{2} + \dfrac{c}{2pq}$ | tonnes |
| 10 | Optimal harvest | $H^{\star} = rS^{\star}(1 - S^{\star}/K)$ | tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |
| 11 | Shadow price of stock | $\mu^{\star} = \dfrac{(p - c/(qS^{\star}))}{(\delta + \text{something})}$ | ₹ per tonne |
| 12 | Growth elasticity at $S^{\star}$ | $\dfrac{G'(S^{\star}) S^{\star}}{G(S^{\star})}$ | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The Gordon-Schaefer framework (1954) is the operational backbone of:

- **India's Marine Fishing Regulation Acts** — fixing Total Allowable Catches (TACs) and Vessel-Day Schemes.
- **Forest Working Plans** — setting Annual Allowable Cuts.
- **CITES quota-setting** for whales, tuna, and timber.
- **IPCC Tier-1 carbon accounting** for forest biomass as a *stock-pollutant* hybrid resource.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Engineering takeaway for KTU students: every time you see a "sustainable yield" or "annual allowable cut" number in a real engineering EIA report, **it is the MSY or MEY of this exact model**. Recognising the formula instantly is a guaranteed marks booster.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Sustainable Yield (Biological Optimum)

We start from the logistic growth function and treat $S$ as a *choice variable* the manager controls (i.e., we ask: at what stock level is sustainable harvest highest?).

**Step 1.** Sustainable harvest equals natural growth in steady state:

$$H_{sust} = G(S) = rS\left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right)$$

**Step 2.** Differentiate $H_{sust}$ with respect to $S$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dH_{sust}}{dS} = r \left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right) + rS \left(-\frac{1}{K}\right) = r \left(1 - \frac{2S}{K}\right)$$

**Step 3.** Set the derivative to zero:

$$r\left(1 - \frac{2S}{K}\right) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S_{MSY} = \frac{K}{2}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $S_{MSY}$ back into the growth function to get MSY:

$$\text{MSY} = r \cdot \frac{K}{2} \left(1 - \frac{K/2}{K}\right) = \frac{rK}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{rK}{4}$$

**Step 5.** Confirm it is a maximum by the second-derivative test:

$$\frac{d^2 H_{sust}}{dS^2} = -\frac{2r}{K} \;<\; 0 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conversion logic:** Step 1 sets the stage by replacing the time-derivative $\dot{S}$ with zero in steady state. Step 2 expands the product rule. Step 3 factorises the bracket. Step 4 re-substitutes the optimum, and Step 5 proves concavity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Open-Access (Bionomic) Equilibrium — Gordon-Schaefer

At **open access**, free entry of vessels drives economic rent to zero because no one can be excluded from the fishery.

**Step 1.** Write instantaneous profit:

$$\pi = p q E S - c E$$

**Step 2.** Set $\pi = 0$ for zero-rent condition:

$$p q E S - c E = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad E(p q S - c) = 0$$

**Step 3.** Discard the trivial solution $E = 0$ (no fishery at all) and solve:

$$S_{OA} = \frac{c}{p q}$$

**Step 4.** Plug $S_{OA}$ into the steady-state condition $G(S) = qES$ to find effort:

$$r S_{OA}\left(1 - \frac{S_{OA}}{K}\right) = q E_{OA} S_{OA}$$

Cancel one $S_{OA}$ (assuming $S_{OA} > 0$):

$$E_{OA} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{S_{OA}}{K}\right) = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{p q K}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Open-access harvest:

$$H_{OA} = r S_{OA}\left(1 - \frac{S_{OA}}{K}\right) = \frac{r c}{p q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{p q K}\right)$$

> **Conversion logic:** Step 2 separates the economic zero-rent condition from biology. Step 3 isolates the stock. Step 4 inverts the harvest equation to find effort. Step 5 closes the loop with the actual harvest level.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sole-Owner (Optimal) Equilibrium — MEY

A sole owner internalises the externality and chooses $S$ to maximise **present value of rent**. We treat the problem in **steady state** first, then add discounting.

### 3.3.1 Steady-state optimum (no discounting, $\delta = 0$)

**Step 1.** Eliminate effort using the steady-state identity $qES = G(S)$, so $E = G(S)/(qS)$:

$$\pi(S) = p G(S) - c \cdot \frac{G(S)}{qS} = G(S) \left[ p - \frac{c}{qS} \right]$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the logistic form and expand:

$$\pi(S) = r S \left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right) \left[ p - \frac{c}{qS} \right] = r \left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right) \left[ pS - \frac{c}{q} \right]$$

**Step 3.** Differentiate with respect to $S$ using the product rule on $\pi = r(1-S/K)(pS - c/q)$:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dS} = r \left[ -\frac{1}{K}\left(pS - \frac{c}{q}\right) + \left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right) p \right]$$

**Step 4.** Simplify the bracket by finding a common denominator $K$:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dS} = \frac{r}{K}\left[ -pS + \frac{c}{q} + pK - pS \right] = \frac{r}{K}\left[ pK + \frac{c}{q} - 2pS \right]$$

**Step 5.** Set derivative to zero and solve:

$$pK + \frac{c}{q} - 2pS^{\star} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S^{\star} = \frac{K}{2} + \frac{c}{2pq}$$

**Step 6.** Optimal harvest by back-substitution:

$$H^{\star} = r S^{\star}\left(1 - \frac{S^{\star}}{K}\right) = \frac{r}{K} S^{\star} (K - S^{\star})$$

### 3.3.2 With positive discount rate $\delta > 0$ (qualitative sketch)

The Hamiltonian condition adds a *user-cost* term: harvest now must cover the forgone future growth valued at the shadow price $\mu$:

$$p = \frac{c}{qS^{\star}} + \frac{\mu}{\delta}\cdot \frac{G'(S^{\star})}{qS^{\star}}$$

Higher $\delta$ tilts the optimum toward **lower stock and higher harvest today** — the standard "develop now, conserve never" trap that Indian policy makers grapple with in groundwater depletion.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> Use the parameter set: $K = 1000$ tonnes, $r = 0.4$ yr$^{-1}$, $p = ₹10$/kg = ₹10,000/tonne (treat as 10 in normalised units), $q = 0.1$, $c = 100$, $\delta = 0$.

| Quantity | Formula Plug-in | Numerical Value | Interpretation |
|----------|-----------------|------------------|----------------|
| $S_{MSY}$ | $K/2$ | $500$ | Biological optimum stock |
| $\text{MSY}$ | $rK/4$ | $100$ tonnes yr$^{-1}$ | Max sustainable catch |
| $S_{OA}$ | $c/(pq) = 100/(10\cdot 0.1)$ | $100$ | Collapsed fishery stock |
| $H_{OA}$ | $r\cdot 100\cdot 0.9$ | $36$ tonnes yr$^{-1}$ | Rent-zero harvest |
| $S^{\star}$ | $K/2 + c/(2pq) = 500 + 50$ | $550$ | Economic optimum stock |
| $H^{\star}$ | $0.4 \cdot 550 \cdot 0.45$ | $99$ tonnes yr$^{-1}$ | MEY harvest |
| $E^{\star}$ | $H^{\star}/(qS^{\star}) = 99/55$ | $1.8$ effort-units | Optimal fleet size |
| $\pi^{\star}$ | $pH^{\star} - cE^{\star} = 990 - 180$ | $810$ ₹-units yr$^{-1}$ | Maximum economic rent |

> **Key insight:** Notice the *ordering* $S_{OA} \;<\; S_{MSY} \;<\; S^{\star} \;<\; K$. Economic optimum exceeds biological optimum because preserving stock for the future has positive scarcity value.

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Adjustment Dynamics Toward Equilibrium

When stock is not at the optimum, the **dynamic equation** governs convergence:

$$\dot{S} = rS\left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right) - qES$$

Linearising around $S^{\star}$ with $E$ chosen by the owner at the optimum:

$$\dot{S} \approx (r - 2rS^{\star}/K - qE^{\star}) (S - S^{\star})$$

Because $E^{\star} = r(1-S^{\star}/K)/q$, the bracket collapses to $-rS^{\star}/K$, which is **strictly negative** — proving local asymptotic stability of $S^{\star}$. This is a KTU favourite "stability" sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Storable Resource Decision Flow

The diagram below traces how the **biological subsystem** and the **economic subsystem** interact to deliver the three reference equilibria. It is designed for direct reproduction in KTU answer sheets.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Carrying Capacity K] --> B[Logistic Growth G of S]
    B --> C{Steady-State Condition: G = H}
    C --> D[Harvest Function H = q times E times S]
    D --> E[Total Revenue TR = p times H]
    D --> F[Total Cost TC = c times E]
    E --> G[Net Rent pi = TR minus TC]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Decision Regime}
    H --> H1[Pristine: S = K, pi = 0]
    H --> H2[Open Access: pi = 0 leads to S_OA]
    H --> H3[Sole Owner: max PV leads to S_star]
    H2 --> I1[S_OA = c divided by pq]
    H2 --> J1[H_OA = r times S_OA times 1 minus S_OA over K]
    H3 --> I2[S_star = K over 2 plus c over 2pq]
    H3 --> J2[H_star = r times S_star times 1 minus S_star over K]
    H1 --> K[Ordering: S_OA < S_MSY < S_star < K]
    I1 --> K
    I2 --> K
    J1 --> K
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[Policy Lever: Property Rights and Catch Quotas]
    L --> M[Sustainable Management Outcome]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Stock-Flow Schematic — The "Renewable Resource Machine"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BiologicalSubsystem
        A1[(Stock S)] -->|Growth G of S| A1
        A1 -->|Harvest H = qES| A2[(Caught Output)]
    end
    subgraph EconomicSubsystem
        A2 -->|Revenue pH| A3[Market Revenue]
        A3 -->|Reinvestment| A4[Effort E]
        A4 -->|Applied to stock| A1
        A2 -->|Cost cE| A5[Harvesting Cost]
        A5 --> A4
    end
    subgraph PolicyLayer
        A4 --> A6{Property Rights Regime}
        A6 -->|Open access| A7[S converges to S_OA]
        A6 -->|Sole owner or quota| A8[S converges to S_star]
    end
```

## 4.3 Reading the Diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation guide for KTU 2024 board papers:**
> - The **inner loop** (Stock $\to$ Growth $\to$ Stock) is the *biological feedback*.
> - The **outer loop** (Stock $\to$ Harvest $\to$ Revenue $\to$ Effort $\to$ Stock) is the *economic feedback*.
> - The **policy layer** is what *shifts* the system between $S_{OA}$ and $S^{\star}$. In Indian fisheries, this corresponds to the Vessel-Day Scheme and the Marine Fishing Regulation Acts.
> - A common exam question asks: "Which arrow changes sign under open access?" — the answer is the **reinvestment arrow**, which becomes weakly positive as long as rent is non-negative.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 4]`**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define a storable renewable resource. Give two examples and explain why storage distinguishes it from a flow resource.**

**Model Answer:**
A storable renewable resource is a naturally regenerating asset whose physical stock can be held over time, decoupling the act of harvesting from the act of biological growth. Two examples are (i) a fishery, where fish biomass can be left in the water and harvested later, and (ii) a forest, where standing timber can be cut on a chosen schedule. Storage creates an *intertemporal* optimisation problem — the user must balance today's harvest against forgone future growth — whereas flow resources (e.g., solar radiation) must be captured instantly. **[3 marks]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Distinguish between Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) and Maximum Economic Yield (MEY).**

**Model Answer:**
MSY is the largest harvest that can be sustained indefinitely under the biological growth law alone, occurring at $S_{MSY} = K/2$ and equal to $rK/4$. MEY is the harvest that maximises the *present value of net economic rent*, accounting for price, cost, and discount rate; it occurs at $S^{\star} = K/2 + c/(2pq)$. In general, $S_{MSY} < S^{\star}$ because the economic optimum preserves a scarcity-rent buffer above the biological optimum. **[3 marks]**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – End-Semester Model Paper, Module 4]`**

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the Gordon-Schaefer open-access equilibrium for a renewable fishery with logistic growth. Show clearly the expressions for the open-access stock $S_{OA}$, effort $E_{OA}$, and harvest $H_{OA}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the open-access and sole-owner equilibria. Use a labelled diagram (description acceptable) to show the ordering of stocks and explain why property rights matter. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Open-Access Equilibrium:**

**Step 1.** Profit function: $\pi = p q E S - c E$.

**Step 2.** Zero-rent condition: $\pi = 0 \Rightarrow S_{OA} = c/(pq)$.

**Step 3.** Steady-state: $r S_{OA} (1 - S_{OA}/K) = q E_{OA} S_{OA}$.

**Step 4.** Solve for effort:

$$E_{OA} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{S_{OA}}{K}\right) = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{p q K}\right)$$

**Step 5.** Open-access harvest:

$$H_{OA} = r \cdot \frac{c}{p q} \left(1 - \frac{c}{p q K}\right)$$

**Step 6.** Comment: As long as $c/(pq) < K$, open-access yields a *finite* harvest at sub-MSY stock. If $c/(pq) \geq K$, the fishery is *biologically extinct*. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Comparison:**

| Feature | Open Access | Sole Owner |
|---------|-------------|------------|
| Governing condition | $\pi = 0$ | $\max \int \pi e^{-\delta t} dt$ |
| Stock | $S_{OA} = c/(pq)$ | $S^{\star} = K/2 + c/(2pq)$ |
| Rent | Zero | Positive |
| Effort | Excess | Optimal |
| Typical outcome | Stock < MSY | Stock > MSY |

The ordering $S_{OA} < S_{MSY} < S^{\star} < K$ holds whenever $c, p, q > 0$. Property rights are the institutional lever that *internalises* the externality of one fisherman's catch on another's future catch, moving the system from $S_{OA}$ to $S^{\star}$. **(7 marks)**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Set up the bioeconomic model of optimal harvest of a storable renewable resource with logistic growth. Derive the optimal stock $S^{\star}$ and optimal harvest $H^{\star}$ under sole ownership (assume zero discount rate). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fishery has the following parameters: carrying capacity $K = 1000$ tonnes, intrinsic growth rate $r = 0.4$/year, price $p = 10$, catchability $q = 0.1$, unit cost $c = 100$. Compute (i) MSY, (ii) the open-access stock, and (iii) the optimal stock and harvest under sole ownership. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Optimal Harvest Derivation:**

**Step 1.** Steady state: $G(S) = qES \Rightarrow E = G(S)/(qS)$.

**Step 2.** Profit in terms of $S$:

$$\pi(S) = p G(S) - c \cdot \frac{G(S)}{qS} = r\left(1 - \frac{S}{K}\right)\left(pS - \frac{c}{q}\right)$$

**Step 3.** First-order condition $d\pi/dS = 0$:

$$\frac{r}{K}\left[pK + \frac{c}{q} - 2pS\right] = 0$$

**Step 4.** Solve:

$$S^{\star} = \frac{K}{2} + \frac{c}{2pq}$$

**Step 5.** Optimal harvest:

$$H^{\star} = r S^{\star}\left(1 - \frac{S^{\star}}{K}\right)$$

**Step 6.** Verify second-order condition: $d^2\pi/dS^2 = -2pr/K < 0$, confirming a maximum. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

| Quantity | Calculation | Value |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| MSY | $rK/4 = 0.4 \cdot 1000/4$ | $100$ tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |
| $S_{MSY}$ | $K/2$ | $500$ tonnes |
| $S_{OA}$ | $c/(pq) = 100/(10\cdot 0.1)$ | $100$ tonnes |
| $S^{\star}$ | $500 + 100/(2 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.1) = 500 + 50$ | $550$ tonnes |
| $H^{\star}$ | $0.4 \cdot 550 \cdot (1 - 550/1000) = 0.4 \cdot 550 \cdot 0.45$ | $99$ tonnes yr$^{-1}$ |

**Comment:** The economic optimum stock (550) lies *above* the biological optimum (500), confirming that MEY is lower than MSY but more profitable in present-value terms. The open-access stock (100) is severely depleted, illustrating the tragedy of the commons. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $S^{\star} = K/2 + c/(2pq)$. Writing $c/(pq)$ instead of $c/(2pq)$ is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Dropping units** in numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit-less final answers.
> 3. **Conflating MEY with MSY**. MEY < MSY (in harvest) but MEY stock > MSY stock. Examiners love to test this asymmetry.
> 4. **Omitting the steady-state assumption** when deriving $S_{OA}$ or $S^{\star}$. Always state $G(S) = H$.
> 5. **Ignoring the property-rights discussion** in 14-mark questions. A full answer requires the institutional lever (quotas, ITQs, co-management) to receive full marks on Part (b).
> 6. **Sign error in second-order condition**. Forgetting to confirm $-2pr/K < 0$ costs 1 mark even if the optimum is correct.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision bullets for the night before the exam:**

- [x] **Definition**: Storable renewable resource = naturally regenerating stock that can be held over time.
- [x] **Four building blocks**: Logistic growth, harvest function, revenue/cost, present-value objective.
- [x] **Three reference stocks**: $S_{MSY} = K/2$, $S_{OA} = c/(pq)$, $S^{\star} = K/2 + c/(2pq)$.
- [x] **Ordering rule**: $S_{OA} < S_{MSY} < S^{\star} < K$ when $c, p, q > 0$.
- [x] **MSY formula**: $\text{MSY} = rK/4$ — pure biology, no economics.
- [x] **Zero-rent condition** is the *signature* of open-access: $p q S_{OA} = c$.
- [x] **Sole-owner condition** is the *first-order* condition $d\pi/dS = 0$.
- [x] **Discount rate effect**: Higher $\delta \Rightarrow$ lower $S^{\star}$, higher $H^{\star}$.
- [x] **Stability**: $S^{\star}$ is locally asymptotically stable; $S_{OA}$ is also stable but at a *lower* welfare level.
- [x] **Policy levers**: Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs), Vessel-Day Schemes, community co-management, property-rights reform.
- [x] **Numerical hack**: For exam speed, compute $pq$ first, then $c/(pq)$ gives $S_{OA}$ in one line.
- [x] **Real-world mapping**: Indian fisheries → Vessel-Day Scheme; forests → Working Plans; wildlife → IUCN quotas.
- [x] **Examiner trap**: Always write the steady-state identity $G(S) = H$ explicitly before deriving.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Renewable Common Property Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Renewable Common Property Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Common Property Resource (CPR)** is a resource system that is **non-excludable** (it is prohibitively costly or physically impossible to prevent potential beneficiaries from accessing it) but **rival** (one user's consumption subtracts from the availability of the resource for others). When such a resource is **renewable**, it possesses the biological or ecological capacity to regenerate itself over time through natural reproduction, growth, or replenishment cycles, provided that the rate of harvest or use does not exceed the natural regeneration rate.

In the precise terminology of the KTU 2024 Environmental Economics syllabus, **Renewable Common Property Resources (RCPRs)** are natural resource stocks—biotic and abiotic—that satisfy three simultaneous economic and ecological conditions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of an RCPR (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> 1. **Non-Excludability** – No individual can be effectively barred from using the resource.
> 2. **Rivalry (Subtractability)** – One agent's use reduces what is available for another.
> 3. **Renewability** – The stock regenerates naturally if the harvest rate is held within the biological limits of the resource base.

**Canonical Examples of RCPRs:**
- **Fisheries** (open-access marine and inland capture fisheries)
- **Forests** (community woodlands, open-access timber, non-timber forest produce)
- **Watersheds and aquifers** (shared groundwater basins, rivers)
- **Grazing commons** (village pasturelands, rangelands)
- **Wildlife populations** (migratory birds, marine mammals, biodiversity pools)
- **The atmospheric commons** (the global atmosphere as a carbon sink)

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Village Pond" Analogy

Imagine a **large village pond** fed continuously by an underground spring. The pond refills itself every monsoon (renewable). The village has 200 families, and nobody owns the pond privately—it belongs to "the community." Nobody can be stopped from drawing water (non-excludable). But if one family pumps 5,000 litres per day, there is measurably less water for the others (rival).

If every family behaves **rationally in their own self-interest**—pumping as much as they can for irrigation, livestock, and washing—then within a few dry seasons the pond's water level collapses, the spring cannot keep up, and the pond dries up. *Everyone loses*. This is precisely the structural problem of renewable common property resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight (Elinor Ostrom, Nobel 2009):**
> The "tragedy" is **not** an inevitable law of nature. Communities all over the world have, for centuries, evolved **institutions, rules, and sanctions** that successfully manage RCPRs without privatisation or state control. Understanding *why* some commons succeed and others collapse is the heart of this KTU module.

## 1.3 Property Rights Regime Classification (Syllabus Anchor)

The following four-fold classification, drawn from standard environmental economics, is **high-yield** for KTU Part A questions:

| Regime Type | Excludable? | Rival? | Example | Sustainability Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Private Good** | Yes | Yes | Farmland, private fishery pond | Usually sustainable if owner has incentives |
| **Common Property Resource (CPR)** | Hard | Yes | Forest, fishery, grazing land | Conditional—depends on institutions |
| **Open-Access Resource (OAR)** | No | Yes | High seas, atmosphere, illegal logging frontier | **Almost always overexploited** |
| **Public Good** | No | No | National defence, lighthouse, clean air | Underprovided by market |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Many students confuse *Common Property Resources* with *Open-Access Resources*. They are **NOT** the same. A CPR has an *established community with rules* (excludability is "hard" but not impossible). An OAR has *no rules and no community boundary*. A CPR with a working institution is sustainable; an OAR is the textbook **Tragedy of the Commons**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — Defined Bold for KTU Recall

- **Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY):** The largest harvest that can be taken from a resource stock indefinitely without depleting the underlying regenerative capacity.
- **Bioeconomic Equilibrium (b*):** The stock level at which open-access harvest effort equates marginal revenue to marginal cost (zero economic rent).
- **Rent Dissipation:** The complete erosion of economic surplus under open access, where the value of the resource is exactly cancelled out by the costs of effort expended in its capture.
- **Carrying Capacity (K):** The maximum population of a species that the habitat can sustain indefinitely.
- **Intrinsic Growth Rate (r):** The natural per-capita rate at which a renewable stock grows in the absence of harvesting.
- **Sustainability Constraint:** Harvest (H) ≤ Natural Growth (G) for every period t.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tragedy of the Commons — Garrett Hardin (1968)

Hardin's seminal paper formalised the logic of open-access overexploitation. The reasoning proceeds in five logical steps, each of which is a *favourite* KTU 14-mark question stem:

**Step 1 — Define the commons.** A finite resource freely available to all rational agents.

**Step 2 — Each agent is utility-maximising.** Every herder, fisher, or logger seeks to maximise private benefit.

**Step 3 — Private cost < Social cost.** The decision-maker captures *all* the benefit of one extra unit of harvest but bears *only a fraction* of the depletion cost (the cost is spread across all users).

**Step 4 — Positive externality of restraint, negative externality of extraction.** My restraint benefits everyone else for free; my over-extraction imposes a cost on everyone else for free.

**Step 5 — Nash Equilibrium is Pareto-Inferior.** The rational, self-interested outcome is mutual ruin, although every agent would be better off under a co-operative agreement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** "Discuss the relevance of the Tragedy of the Commons to renewable resource management." Always contrast Hardin's pessimistic prediction with **Ostrom's empirical refutation** (Governing the Commons, 1990) for full marks.

## 2.2 The Bioeconomic Model of a Renewable Common-Pool Fishery

The standard model (Gordon, 1954; Schaefer, 1957) treats a fishery as the canonical RCPR. Let:

- $X_t$ = fish stock at time $t$ (biomass, tonnes)
- $r$ = intrinsic growth rate of the fish population
- $K$ = environmental carrying capacity (maximum stock)
- $H_t$ = harvest in period $t$
- $E_t$ = fishing effort (number of boats × time)
- $q$ = catchability coefficient (technology parameter)
- $p$ = market price per tonne of fish (assumed constant)
- $c$ = per-unit cost of effort
- $w$ = opportunity wage in alternative employment

### 2.2.1 The Biological Growth Function (Logistic)

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = F(X) = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)
$$

This is the famous **logistic growth equation**. It captures two realities: (i) at very low stock, growth is small because few fish are breeding, and (ii) at very high stock, growth slows because of food/space constraints. Growth is maximised at $X = K/2$, yielding:

$$
F_{\max} = MSY_{\text{biological}} = \frac{rK}{4}
$$

### 2.2.2 The Schaefer Harvest Function

$$
H_t = q \cdot E_t \cdot X_t
$$

So total harvest is proportional to effort, stock, and a technology parameter.

### 2.2.3 Stock Dynamics Under Harvest

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) - qEX
$$

### 2.2.4 Steady-State Open-Access Equilibrium

At the long-run open-access steady state, $dX/dt = 0$, so growth equals harvest:

$$
rX^*\left(1 - \frac{X^*}{K}\right) = qE^*X^*
$$

This simplifies to the **bionomic equilibrium stock**:

$$
X^* = K\left(1 - \frac{qE^*}{r}\right)
$$

In an open-access fishery, free entry of vessels continues until **economic rent is fully dissipated**, i.e., total revenue equals total cost:

$$
pH = cE \quad \Longrightarrow \quad pqE^*X^* = cE^*
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
X^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{c}{pq}, \qquad E^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right), \qquad H^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{r c}{pq}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right)
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the definitive KTU 2024 formula reference for Module 4. Memorise every entry.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logistic Growth | $F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$ | Natural growth of a renewable stock | tonnes/yr |
| 2 | Maximum Sustainable Yield (biological) | $MSY_b = rK/4$ | Peak sustainable harvest | tonnes/yr |
| 3 | MSY Stock Level | $X_{MSY} = K/2$ | Stock at which growth is maximum | tonnes |
| 4 | Schaefer Harvest | $H = qEX$ | Total catch as a function of effort | tonnes/yr |
| 5 | Stock under Harvest | $dX/dt = rX(1 - X/K) - qEX$ | Full bioeconomic dynamics | tonnes/yr |
| 6 | Open-Access Stock | $X^* = c/(pq)$ | Stock at bionomic equilibrium | tonnes |
| 7 | Open-Access Effort | $E^* = (r/q)(1 - c/(pqK))$ | Effort at free entry | boats·days |
| 8 | Open-Access Harvest | $H^* = (rc/pq)(1 - c/(pqK))$ | Catch at bioeconomic eq. | tonnes/yr |
| 9 | Sustainable Rent | $\pi = (pqX - c)E$ | Profit in a regulated fishery | ₹/yr |
| 10 | Sustainability Condition | $H \le rX(1 - X/K)$ | Harvest must not exceed growth | — |
| 11 | Total Economic Value | $TEV = UV + NUV$ | Use + Non-use value of the resource | ₹ |
| 12 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+\delta)^t}$ | Intertemporal optimisation | ₹ |
| 13 | Hartwick Rule | $S = \sum (R_t - C_t)$ reinvested | Sustainability via reinvestment | ₹ |
| 14 | Hotelling's Rule | $dP_t/dt = \delta P_t$ | Resource price rises at discount rate | ₹/yr |
| 15 | Effective Rate of Protection | $\eta = p/p_{\text{ext}} - 1$ | (Equilibrium concept — for context) | — |

> **Caution on Notation:** All vertical bars in formulas use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute value, never the bare `|` character, to keep the table rendering intact.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility — Why Engineers Should Care

Even though PESFT861 is an environmental economics course, the bioeconomic models above are deeply relevant to:

- **Civil Engineering:** Designing sustainable reservoir operations, watershed management, and groundwater extraction regimes.
- **Mechanical/Industrial Engineering:** Lifecycle analysis of renewable versus non-renewable inputs in manufacturing.
- **Biotechnology:** Quantifying sustainable yields of bio-resources (algal biofuels, medicinal plants).
- **Climate-Energy Nexus:** The atmosphere is the ultimate RCPR; carbon pricing is essentially a "rental fee" for its use as a sink.

## 2.5 Ostrom's Design Principles for Sustainable CPRs

Elinor Ostrom identified **eight design principles** that empirically characterise long-enduring CPR institutions. These are **direct high-yield KTU Part A material**:

1. **Clearly Defined Boundaries** — Who has rights, and what are the resource boundaries.
2. **Congruence** — Rules fit local conditions and provisioning rules match appropriation rules.
3. **Collective-Choice Arrangements** — Those affected by rules can participate in modifying them.
4. **Monitoring** — Effective monitoring of resource conditions and user behaviour.
5. **Graduated Sanctions** — Violators face escalating penalties.
6. **Conflict-Resolution Mechanisms** — Cheap, accessible forums for disputes.
7. **Minimal Recognition of Rights to Organise** — External authorities respect self-governance.
8. **Nested Enterprises** (for larger systems) — Governance organised across multiple scales.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Past Pattern:** A 14-mark question frequently asks students to "critically evaluate Hardin's Tragedy of the Commons in light of Ostrom's institutional analysis." Structure your answer as *Hardin's claim → limitations → Ostrom's empirical evidence → conditions for CPR success → conclusion*.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Open-Access Bioeconomic Equilibrium (Gordon-Schaefer)

This is the **single most important derivation** in the module. We will perform it exhaustively, step by step, with every algebraic transition justified.

### Step 1 — Start with the biological growth equation

The natural growth of a fish population in a logistic world is:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = rX - \frac{rX^2}{K}
$$

**Interpretation:** $rX$ is the unrestricted growth at low stock; the term $-rX^2/K$ captures intraspecific competition for food and space that slows growth as $X$ approaches $K$.

### Step 2 — Introduce harvest

When fishing effort $E$ is applied with catchability $q$:

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = rX - \frac{rX^2}{K} - qEX
$$

**Interpretation:** Each unit of effort $E$ removes $qX$ tonnes of fish per period, proportional to the stock available.

### Step 3 — Impose the steady-state condition

In long-run open-access equilibrium, the stock is constant, so $dX/dt = 0$:

$$
0 = rX - \frac{rX^2}{K} - qEX
$$

**Interpretation:** Natural growth exactly compensates the harvest, leaving the stock unchanged over time.

### Step 4 — Factor out the stock variable

Divide both sides by $X$ (assuming $X \neq 0$, since a collapsed fishery has no equilibrium):

$$
0 = r - \frac{rX}{K} - qE
$$

**Interpretation:** Per-unit-of-stock, the net growth must balance the per-unit harvest rate.

### Step 5 — Solve for the equilibrium stock $X^*$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{rX^*}{K} = r - qE^*
$$

$$
X^* = \frac{K(r - qE^*)}{r} = K\left(1 - \frac{qE^*}{r}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** Higher effort pushes the equilibrium stock *down*—intuitively, more boats deplete the fishery.

### Step 6 — Introduce the open-access rent-dissipation condition

In an open-access fishery, the entry of vessels continues until the **total economic rent is zero**. Total revenue is $pH = pqEX$, total cost is $cE$. Setting them equal:

$$
pqE^*X^* = cE^*
$$

Cancel $E^*$ (assuming non-trivial effort, $E^* \neq 0$):

$$
pqX^* = c
$$

**Interpretation:** The marginal value of one more tonne of fish ($p \times q$, since $q$ tonnes are caught per unit effort) times the stock equals the marginal cost $c$. This is the "zero rent" condition.

### Step 7 — Solve for the open-access equilibrium stock

$$
X^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{c}{pq}
$$

**Interpretation:** The open-access equilibrium stock is **independent** of the biological parameters $r$ and $K$. It depends only on the *economic* parameters: price, cost, and catchability. This is a deep insight—**economics, not biology, determines the long-run state of an open-access fishery.**

### Step 8 — Solve for the open-access equilibrium effort

Substitute $X^*_{\text{OA}}$ back into the stock equation from Step 5:

$$
\frac{c}{pq} = K\left(1 - \frac{qE^*}{r}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{c}{pqK} = 1 - \frac{qE^*}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{qE^*}{r} = 1 - \frac{c}{pqK}
$$

$$
E^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** Equilibrium effort is positive only if $pqK > c$, i.e., the revenue from a fully-stocked fishery exceeds cost. If $pqK \le c$, the fishery is *uneconomic even at full stock* and no effort is exerted.

### Step 9 — Solve for the open-access equilibrium harvest

Using $H = qEX$:

$$
H^*_{\text{OA}} = q \cdot \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right) \cdot \frac{c}{pq}
$$

$$
H^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{rc}{pq}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right)
$$

**Interpretation:** Open-access harvest is strictly less than the Maximum Sustainable Yield $rK/4$, except under specific parameter values—proving that **open access systematically underperforms social optimum**.

### Step 10 — Compare with the social optimum

The **socially optimal** stock maximises sustainable rent, $R(X) = pqX \cdot E(X)$ subject to the growth equation. The optimum occurs where:

$$
X^{**}_{\text{SO}} = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right) \cdot 2 \;\; \text{after standard profit maximisation}
$$

After solving the social planner's problem:

$$
X^{**}_{\text{SO}} = \frac{c}{pq} \cdot \frac{pqK}{2c} = \frac{K}{2} \cdot \frac{pqK - c}{pqK}
$$

The crucial comparison is:

$$
X^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{c}{pq} \qquad \text{vs.} \qquad X^{**}_{\text{SO}} > \frac{c}{pq}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the derivation:** The open-access equilibrium stock is *lower* than the social optimum. The fishery is **over-exploited in effort** ($E^*_{\text{OA}}$ is high) and **over-depleted in stock** ($X^*_{\text{OA}}$ is low). This is the formal proof of Hardin's "tragedy." The gap between the two is the **dissipated rent**—a permanent social loss.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Management Regimes

The matrix below is a high-yield KTU synthesis tool, often requested in 14-mark answers.

| Dimension | Open Access (No Rules) | CPR with Ostrom-style Institution | State Regulation (Command & Control) | Privatisation (ITQ / Property Rights) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Property Rights | None | Collective, rule-based | Public, allocated by state | Private, transferable |
| Excludability | No | Yes (locally) | Yes (state-enforced) | Yes |
| Effort at Equilibrium | Excessively high | Modest, stabilised | Set by regulator | Set by market for ITQs |
| Stock at Equilibrium | Below MSY | Near MSY | Near MSY (if well-designed) | Near MSY (if well-designed) |
| Economic Rent | Fully dissipated | Partly captured by community | Captured by state (or dissipated by lobby) | Captured by quota-holders |
| Transaction Cost | Low (informal) | Moderate (self-monitoring) | High (bureaucracy) | Moderate (market) |
| Equity Outcome | Highly unequal (race for fish) | Generally equitable | Depends on political process | Often unequal (initial allocation) |
| Information Needs | None | High (local ecological knowledge) | Very high (centralised science) | High (stock assessment) |
| Risk of Failure | Collapse / extinction | Rule erosion, elite capture | Corruption, regulatory capture | Monopolisation, regulatory capture |
| Real-world Example | Somali coast fisheries 1990s; North Atlantic cod | Japanese *kairyos* forest commons; Maine lobster gangs | Norwegian coastal cod quotas; Indian Forest Dept reserves | New Zealand ITQ; Iceland fisheries |

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> A small inland fishery has the following parameters: $r = 0.4$/yr, $K = 100$ tonnes, $p =$ ₹200/kg = ₹2,00,000/tonne, $c =$ ₹50,000 per unit effort, $q = 0.01$ tonnes/unit-effort/tonne-stock. Calculate (a) MSY, (b) the open-access equilibrium stock, and (c) the open-access harvest.

**Solution:**

**(a) MSY (biological):**

$$
MSY_b = \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{0.4 \times 100}{4} = 10 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

**(b) Open-access stock:**

$$
X^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{c}{pq} = \frac{50{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01} = \frac{50{,}000}{2{,}000} = 25 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(c) Open-access harvest:**

$$
H^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{rc}{pq}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right) = \frac{0.4 \times 50{,}000}{2{,}000}\left(1 - \frac{25}{100}\right) = 10 \times 0.75 = 7.5 \text{ tonnes/yr}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The open-access fishery produces 7.5 tonnes/year compared to a sustainable biological maximum of 10 tonnes/year. The 2.5 tonnes/year gap is the **permanent loss from over-exploitation**—a direct empirical illustration of the Tragedy of the Commons in numbers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**Define the term "Common Property Resource" with two examples. Distinguish it from an "Open-Access Resource."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

A Common Property Resource (CPR) is a resource that is **non-excludable but rivalrous**, and is managed by a defined community of users through collectively agreed rules, norms, and sanctions. *Examples: community-managed village forests, traditional irrigation tanks.*

A CPR differs from an Open-Access Resource (OAR) in that a CPR has an **identifiable user group with de facto property rights** (boundaries are defined, even if exclusion is imperfect), while an OAR has *no defined users and no rules*—it is open to all without restriction. Hence, a CPR with functioning institutions is sustainable, whereas an OAR is the canonical case of the Tragedy of the Commons.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students lose 1 mark by giving only *one* example. KTU's strict marking scheme awards 0.5 marks per example—always cite **two** for full credit.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**What is Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY)? Why does open access lead to harvest levels below MSY?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~75 words):**

**MSY** is the largest average harvest that can be taken from a renewable resource stock indefinitely without diminishing the stock's capacity to regenerate; in the logistic model, $MSY = rK/4$ at stock $X = K/2$.

Under open access, free entry of harvesters continues until **economic rent is fully dissipated** (revenue equals cost). The resulting equilibrium stock is $X^*_{\text{OA}} = c/(pq)$, which is *strictly less* than $X_{MSY} = K/2$ for typical parameter values. Hence the open-access harvest $H^*_{\text{OA}} < MSY$—the resource is over-fished in *stock* and over-exploited in *effort*, producing a permanent welfare loss.

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain Garrett Hardin's "Tragedy of the Commons" with reference to a renewable common-pool fishery. State the assumptions, the central argument, and its limitations.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the Gordon-Schaefer bioeconomic model, derive the open-access equilibrium stock, effort, and harvest. Show that open-access harvest is less than the Maximum Sustainable Yield.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Hardin's Tragedy of the Commons – 7 marks**

**Assumptions [1 mark]:**
1. The resource is finite and rival.
2. The resource is open-access—non-excludable.
3. Users are rational, self-interested utility maximisers.
4. There is no communication or co-operation among users.
5. Each user ignores the impact of their action on others (i.e., the externality is not internalised).

**Central Argument [3 marks]:**
Hardin (1968, *Science*) argued that a commons shared by rational, self-interested actors is *inevitably* degraded to ruin. Each herder gains the *full* benefit of adding one more animal to the commons, but bears only a *fraction* of the negative externality (overgrazing) imposed on all users. The private marginal benefit exceeds the private marginal cost, inducing over-use. The **Nash equilibrium**—every herder keeps adding animals—is *Pareto-inferior* to the co-operative outcome (mutual restraint). Hence "freedom in a commons brings ruin to all."

**Limitations [3 marks]:**
1. **Empirical refutation by Ostrom (1990):** Real-world communities (Swiss alpine pastures, Japanese forests, Maine lobster fisheries) have sustainably managed commons for centuries using local institutions.
2. **Static model:** Hardin ignores institutional evolution and the possibility of collective learning.
3. **Zero communication assumption is unrealistic:** Even in ancient commons, communities negotiated rules.
4. **Biased policy implication:** Hardin's prescription of "mutual coercion" was interpreted as a justification for privatisation or state control, but Ostrom showed a **third path**—community self-governance—often works better.
5. **Heterogeneity of users ignored:** Real commons have users with different stakes, powers, and norms of co-operation.

**(b) Gordon-Schaefer Derivation – 7 marks**

*See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full 10-step derivation.* Below is the **condensed board-exam version** (each result stated clearly with one explanatory line):

**Step 1 — Logistic growth [1 mark]:**

$$
\frac{dX}{dt} = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) - qEX
$$

**Step 2 — Steady state $dX/dt = 0$ [1 mark]:**

$$
rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) = qEX
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for equilibrium stock [1 mark]:**

$$
X^* = K\left(1 - \frac{qE}{r}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Open-access rent-dissipation condition [1.5 marks]:** Total revenue = Total cost:

$$
pqEX = cE \quad \Rightarrow \quad X^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{c}{pq}
$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium effort and harvest [1.5 marks]:**

$$
E^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right), \qquad H^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{rc}{pq}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pqK}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with MSY [1 mark]:**

$$
X_{MSY} = \frac{K}{2}, \quad \text{whereas} \quad X^*_{\text{OA}} = \frac{c}{pq} < \frac{K}{2}
$$

for typical positive cost and price parameters. Since harvest is a concave function of stock peaking at $K/2$, $H^*_{\text{OA}} < MSY = rK/4$. **Hence open access yields less than the sustainable maximum—the Tragedy of the Commons is quantitatively confirmed.** ∎

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Critically evaluate Elinor Ostrom's institutional analysis of renewable common property resources. What conditions enable a community to sustainably manage a commons without privatisation or state control?

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare three policy instruments — Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs), Community-Based Co-Management, and Command-and-Control regulation — for managing a coastal fishery. Discuss their economic efficiency, equity, and enforcement implications.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Ostrom's Institutional Analysis – 7 marks**

**Core Thesis [2 marks]:** Ostrom (1990, *Governing the Commons*) argued empirically that Hardin's "tragedy" is *not* an inevitability. Across case studies from Nepalese irrigation to Swiss forests to Philippine fisheries, she documented communities that have sustainably managed CPRs for centuries. The necessary condition is **institutional design** rather than ownership transfer.

**The Eight Design Principles [3 marks]:** A sustainable CPR institution typically exhibits (i) clearly defined boundaries, (ii) rules congruent with local conditions, (iii) collective-choice arrangements, (iv) effective monitoring, (v) graduated sanctions, (vi) cheap conflict-resolution forums, (vii) external recognition of self-governance, and (viii) nested governance for large systems.

**Critical Evaluation [2 marks]:**
- *Strengths:* Empirically grounded, recognises heterogeneity, gives voice to local knowledge, avoids polarised privatisation-vs-state debate.
- *Weaknesses:* Design principles are *necessary* but not always *sufficient*; scale-bounded; vulnerable to elite capture and state interference; may not cope with rapid exogenous shocks (climate change, market integration).

**Conditions for Success [1 mark]:** Small group size, stable resource boundaries, low discounting of the future, low mobility of users, strong social capital, and external legitimacy.

**(b) Policy Instruments Compared – 7 marks**

| Instrument | Economic Efficiency | Equity | Enforcement | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ITQ (Privatisation)** | High (rent capture, no race for fish) | Often low (initial allocation favours incumbents) | Moderate (quota trading monitored) | Monopolisation, high entry barriers |
| **Community Co-Management** | Moderate to high (local knowledge lowers cost) | High (community-determined sharing rules) | Low to moderate (peer monitoring) | Elite capture, free-rider entry |
| **Command-and-Control** (Total Allowable Catch, gear restrictions) | Often low (mis-specified science, lobby capture) | Moderate (uniform rules) | High cost (state patrols) | Corruption, regulatory capture |

**Synthesis [1 mark]:** No single instrument is universally superior. New Zealand and Iceland have used ITQs successfully; Japan and Chile combine ITQs with co-management; India and the EU rely on TAC plus community co-management. The choice depends on **institutional capacity, ecological knowledge, and political economy** of the fishery. Hybrid regimes typically outperform pure forms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **No diagrams in Part B:** A well-labelled Schaefer curve, open-access graph, or property-rights matrix is worth 1–2 extra marks. Always include a *hand-drawn-style* diagram in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Confusing CPR with OAR:** Examiners report ~30% of answer scripts treat these as synonyms. The distinction is worth 1 mark by itself.
> 3. **No Ostrom counter-evidence:** A Hardin-only answer scores at most 8/14. A balanced Hardin + Ostrom answer scores 12–14.
> 4. **Skipping the numerical comparison in derivation questions:** A derivation that ends at Step 5 without the explicit comparison $X^*_{\text{OA}} < X_{MSY}$ is incomplete. Always close with the *interpretation* of the algebra.
> 5. **Ignoring the Indian context:** Bonus marks (often +1) are awarded for referencing Indian examples — *kairyos* (forest commons), *village panchayat* fisheries, *MGNREGA* watershed works, or the Forest Rights Act 2006.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Renewable Common Property Resources**

- **Definition (must memorise verbatim):** A renewable CPR is *non-excludable, rival, and biologically regenerative* under controlled harvest.
- **Three sustainability conditions:** (1) Harvest ≤ Natural growth, (2) Stock > Threshold, (3) Institution exists to enforce.
- **MSY formula:** $MSY = rK/4$, achieved at $X = K/2$. This is the *biological* benchmark.
- **Open-access equilibrium stock:** $X^*_{\text{OA}} = c/(pq)$ — depends only on *economic* parameters, not biology.
- **Open-access harvest:** $H^*_{\text{OA}} = (rc/pq)(1 - c/(pqK))$ — strictly less than $MSY$ for any positive $c$.
- **Tragedy of the Commons:** Hardin (1968) — rational self-interest in a non-excludable, finite resource leads to Pareto-inferior depletion.
- **Ostrom's Refutation (1990):** Empirically, communities self-manage commons successfully when 8 design principles are satisfied.
- **Property-rights classification (4-fold):** Private good, Club good, **CPR** (managed) / OAR (unmanaged), Public good.
- **Eight Ostrom Design Principles:** Boundaries, Congruence, Collective Choice, Monitoring, Graduated Sanctions, Conflict Resolution, External Recognition, Nested Enterprises.
- **Three policy regimes:** Privatisation (ITQ), Co-Management, Command-and-Control — each with efficiency/equity trade-offs.
- **Indian examples to cite:** Village forests, *johads* (Rajasthan), *kuhl* irrigation (Himachal), Forest Rights Act 2006, marine fisheries regulation.
- **Sustainability constraint (always quote):** $H_t \le F(X_t) = rX_t(1 - X_t/K)$ for all $t$.
- **Rent dissipation concept:** Economic surplus under open access falls to zero because entry continues until $pH = cE$.
- **Hotelling's Rule:** In a *non-renewable* context, the resource price grows at the discount rate — *contrast* with the renewable MSY rule.
- **Common exam trap:** $X_{MSY} = K/2$ is sometimes mistakenly stated as the equilibrium stock under any management; in reality, it is the *biological* optimum, and economic optimal stock differs unless the cost is negligible.

> **Final Examiner Tip:** When answering KTU questions on RCPRs, *always* structure your answer as **Definition → Theory (Hardin + Ostrom) → Mathematics (Gordon-Schaefer) → Real-world example (preferably Indian) → Policy conclusion**. This five-part structure consistently earns 12–14 marks on 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economic Theory of Depletable Resources - Optimal Use of Exhaustible Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economic Theory of Depletable Resources - Optimal Use of Exhaustible Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the strict vocabulary of environmental and welfare economics, a **depletable resource** (also called a **non-renewable** or **exhaustible resource**) is any natural stock of matter or energy that exists in a *fixed and finite quantity* on the relevant geological/biological timescale, and which is being physically transformed, extracted, or dissipated by human economic activity at a rate that is non-negligible compared to its stock.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *"A depletable resource is a resource whose total stock is fixed by nature and cannot be regenerated on a time scale relevant to human economic planning. Its optimal intertemporal allocation is determined by the Hotelling Rule, which equates the rate of appreciation of the in-situ resource price (scarcity rent) to the social discount rate."*

The canonical classification distinguishes between:

| Category | Defining Property | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Stock Resource (Recyclable)** | Finite in nature but recyclable at a cost | Copper, Aluminium, Platinum |
| **Stock Resource (Non-Recyclable)** | Finite, dissipated upon use (energy) | Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium |
| **Flow Resource** | Replenished naturally within planning horizon | Solar, Wind, Tidal, Hydro (run-of-river) |

The central problem of the *Economic Theory of Depletable Resources* is: **Given a known finite stock $R_0$, an extraction cost function $c(q)$, a demand curve $P(q)$, and a social discount rate $r$, choose the extraction path $q(t)$ over the planning horizon $t \in [0, T]$ that maximises the present value of social welfare (or producer profit).**

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Jar of Chocolate Coins"**
> Imagine a transparent glass jar containing exactly **100 chocolate coins**. You are the only person in the world allowed to eat them, and each coin gives you $1 of utility. You can sell coins today at $1.05 each (because the market has a small risk-free interest rate of 5%). You also know that if you save coins, next year the supply will be one coin *less* per year that you hold them back.
>
> **The intuitive question is:** Why on earth would you save coins today (giving up $105 of consumption today) when you could just eat them?
>
> **The economic answer is:** Because by not selling today, the *price* of a coin in the jar will rise at *exactly* 5% per year — precisely compensating you for waiting. If you expect the price to rise faster than 5%, you should hoard coins; if slower, you should flood the market. **The Hotelling Rule is precisely the statement that, at the social optimum, the expected price rise equals the discount rate — making the owner indifferent between extracting today and extracting tomorrow.**

The same logic applies, in trillion-dollar magnitudes, to:
- OPEC deciding whether to pump an extra barrel of crude oil today or hold it underground.
- The Australian government deciding whether to allow BHP to export an extra tonne of iron ore from the Pilbara today or in 2035.
- India deciding whether to auction coal blocks for mining this fiscal year or preserve them for cleaner-energy future generations.

---

### 1.3 Key Concepts Introduced at the Outset

- **Total In-Situ Price (or Full Price / Shadow Price)** of the resource in the ground, denoted $\lambda_t$. It is the maximum amount a rational owner would accept today for permanently surrendering one more unit of stock.
- **Marginal Extraction Cost** $c_t$, the cost of lifting one more unit to the surface.
- **Net Price (or User Cost)** $\mu_t = \lambda_t - c_t$. This is the pure scarcity rent, i.e., the value attributable to scarcity alone, not to the physical act of extraction.
- **Hotelling Rent** — synonymous with net price / scarcity rent / in-situ value.
- **Backstop Technology** — a near-perfect substitute (e.g., solar + storage, fusion, thorium reactors) available in unlimited supply at a known constant backstop price $P_b$.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Standard Metrics to remember:**
> - Social discount rate $r$ — typically **3% to 10%** (KTU problems use $r \in \{0.05, 0.08, 0.10\}$).
> - **Hartwick's Rule** constant — reinvestment rate of Hotelling rent.
> - Standard optimisation horizon $T$ — either *finite* (with known remaining stock $R_0$) or *infinite* (with a backstop technology arriving at time $T^*$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Economic Problem in Canonical Form

A competitive owner of an exhaustible resource chooses an extraction path $\{q_t\}_{t=0}^{\infty}$ to maximise the present value of net revenues (Hotelling rent), subject to two binding constraints:

1. **The Resource Stock Constraint** — the cumulative extraction can never exceed the initial reserve:
$$\int_0^{\infty} q_t \, dt \le R_0$$

2. **The Non-Negativity Constraint** — extraction cannot be negative:
$$q_t \ge 0$$

### 2.2 Logic Chain of Optimal Intertemporal Allocation

The intuition is built in **four rigorous steps**:

**Step 1 — Opportunity Cost Identification.** A unit of resource held in the ground today is a claim on future consumption. Its opportunity cost is *exactly the rate of return that could be earned on the financial market*. Hence the resource-owner must earn at least the market interest rate $r$ by *not* selling today.

**Step 2 — Price of Scarcity.** Define the *in-situ price* $\lambda_t$ of a unit of resource still in the ground. If the owner sold one unit today, received $\lambda_t$, and invested the proceeds in a bond, it would grow to $\lambda_t (1+r)$ next period. If instead the owner keeps the unit in the ground, the next period its in-situ value should be $\lambda_{t+1}$. Equating these two equivalent "portfolios" gives the **No-Arbitrage Condition**.

**Step 3 — Hotelling's No-Arbitrage Equation (Discrete Time).**

$$P_{t+1} - P_t = r \cdot P_t \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad P_{t+1} = P_t (1+r)$$

This is the **Hotelling Rule**: the *price of the resource in the ground must rise at exactly the rate of interest*, period after period.

**Step 4 — Market Price vs. Scarcity Rent (Net vs. Gross Price).** The *market price* observed in commodity markets ($P_t^{\text{mkt}}$) equals marginal extraction cost plus scarcity rent:
$$P_t^{\text{mkt}} = c_t + \lambda_t$$

The component $\lambda_t$ is the *pure scarcity rent*; it is **zero for renewable resources and positive for exhaustible resources**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the only ones you need for any 14-mark KTU question on this topic.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hotelling Rule (Discrete)** | $P_{t+1} = P_t(1+r)$ | In-situ price rises at the interest rate |
| 2 | **Hotelling Rule (Continuous)** | $\dfrac{\dot{P}_t}{P_t} = r$ | Growth rate of price equals discount rate |
| 3 | **Net Price / Scarcity Rent** | $\mu_t = P_t - c(q_t)$ | Price premium due to scarcity alone |
| 4 | **Net Price Growth Path** | $\mu_t = \mu_0 \, e^{rt}$ | Scarcity rent grows exponentially at $r$ |
| 5 | **Stock Constraint** | $\sum_{t=0}^{T} q_t = R_0$ | Cumulative extraction equals initial reserve |
| 6 | **Present Value Objective** | $\max \sum_{t=0}^{T} P_t q_t (1+r)^{-t}$ | Maximise PV of revenues |
| 7 | **Optimal Extraction (Inverse Demand)** | $q_t = D(P_t)$ | Optimal $q_t$ comes from inverse demand |
| 8 | **Backstop Activation Condition** | $P_t = P_b$ | Extract all remaining when market price equals backstop price |
| 9 | **Modified Hotelling (with cost change)** | $\dfrac{\dot{\mu}_t}{\mu_t} = r - \dfrac{\dot{c}_t}{\mu_t}$ | Cost-decline case |
| 10 | **Hartwick's Rule** | Investment = Hotelling Rent | Reinvest rent to keep capital constant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Utility:**
> The same mathematical framework underlies *mine-planning software* (e.g., Datamine, Surpac), *oil reservoir simulation* (CMG, Eclipse), *long-term grid planning* (CEA, India), and *carbon-bubble financial risk models* used by the Bank of England, BlackRock, and RBI.

---

### 2.4 Why and How the Rule Works — Intuition Locked-In

The Hotelling Rule is essentially the **capital-asset pricing model for the ground**. Just as a dividend-paying stock's price must rise at the risk-free rate minus its dividend yield, the in-situ price of an exhaustible resource must rise at the rate of interest because the resource *is itself a dividend-yielding asset* (the dividend being the physical flow of extracted units).

The rule fails in real life when:
1. **Market power** exists (OPEC cartel, state-owned monopolies) — they set $P_t$ above the Hotelling path to maximise *joint* profit.
2. **Interest rates are distorted** (subsidised credit, quantitative easing).
3. **Uncertainty about $R_0$** (true reserves are unknown; e.g., fracking revolution increased US oil reserves).
4. **Substitute technologies arrive unexpectedly** (solar PV collapse in cost curve in 2010-2020 stranded coal assets).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hotelling's Rule — Complete Exhaustive Proof

**Setup.** Let a competitive firm own a finite, known stock $R_0 > 0$ of an exhaustible resource. Let $p_t$ denote the *market (commodity) price* of one unit of extracted resource at the beginning of period $t$. Let $c(q_t)$ be the per-unit cost of extraction, with $\partial c / \partial q \ge 0$ (rising marginal cost). The firm chooses $\{q_t\}_{t=0}^{\infty}$ to maximise the present value of profits.

**Step 1: Write the Lagrangian.** The dynamic optimisation problem is:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \left[ p_t q_t - c(q_t) \right] (1+r)^{-t} + \lambda \left[ R_0 - \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} q_t \right]$$

where $\lambda$ is the multiplier on the binding resource constraint.

**Step 2: First-Order Condition with respect to $q_t$.** Differentiate term-by-term. Because the constraint and the period-utility are *separable* across time, the FOC for each $t$ is:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_t} = (p_t - c'(q_t))(1+r)^{-t} - \lambda = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$(p_t - c'(q_t))(1+r)^{-t} = \lambda \quad \text{for every } t$$

**Step 3: Equate Across Two Periods.** Apply the FOC at period $t$ and period $t+1$:

$$(p_t - c'(q_t))(1+r)^{-t} = (p_{t+1} - c'(q_{t+1}))(1+r)^{-(t+1)}$$

**Step 4: Multiply Through by $(1+r)^t$:**

$$p_t - c'(q_t) = (p_{t+1} - c'(q_{t+1}))(1+r)^{-1}$$

**Step 5: Invert and Rearrange:**

$$p_t - c'(q_t) = \frac{p_{t+1} - c'(q_{t+1})}{1+r}$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad p_{t+1} - c'(q_{t+1}) = (1+r)\bigl[p_t - c'(q_t)\bigr]$$

**Step 6: Define the Net Price (Scarcity Rent).**

Let $\mu_t \equiv p_t - c'(q_t)$ denote the net price in period $t$. The relation becomes:

$$\mu_{t+1} = (1+r)\,\mu_t$$

**Step 7: This is the Hotelling Rule.** Solving the recursion forward:

$$\mu_t = (1+r)^t \mu_0$$

or in *continuous-time* form (taking limits as $\Delta t \to 0$):

$$\frac{\dot{\mu}_t}{\mu_t} = r \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_t = \mu_0 \, e^{rt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The price growing in the equation is the *net price* (price minus marginal extraction cost), **not** the market price. In a constant-cost world ($c'(q) = \text{constant}$), market price and net price grow at the same rate and the rule is often stated for $p_t$. In a cost-decline world (e.g., learning-curve solar), the market price may fall while net price still rises.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Optimal Extraction Path Under Constant Marginal Cost

Assume:
- Constant marginal cost: $c(q_t) = c$ for all $t$.
- Inverse demand: $p_t = a - b q_t$.
- Initial stock $R_0$, planning horizon $T$, discount rate $r$.

**Step 1: Inverse Demand gives us Hotelling Path.**

Because $\mu_t = p_t - c = (a - b q_t) - c = (a-c) - b q_t$, and Hotelling requires $\mu_t = \mu_0(1+r)^t$:

$$(a - c) - b q_t = \mu_0 (1+r)^t$$

**Step 2: Solve for $q_t$.**

$$q_t = \frac{(a - c) - \mu_0 (1+r)^t}{b}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Stock Constraint.**

$$R_0 = \sum_{t=0}^{T} q_t = \frac{1}{b}\left[(a-c)(T+1) - \mu_0 \sum_{t=0}^{T} (1+r)^t\right]$$

**Step 4: Use the Geometric-Series Formula.**

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T} (1+r)^t = \frac{(1+r)^{T+1} - 1}{r}$$

**Step 5: Solve for the Initial Scarcity Rent $\mu_0$.**

$$\boxed{\;\mu_0 \;=\; \frac{(a-c)(T+1) - b\,R_0}{\dfrac{(1+r)^{T+1}-1}{r}}\;}$$

**Step 6: Equilibrium Price Path.** Substituting back:

$$p_t = c + \mu_0 (1+r)^t$$

**Step 7: Equilibrium Quantity Path.**

$$q_t = \frac{(a-c) - \mu_0 (1+r)^t}{b}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** As $t \to T$ (the last period), $q_T$ becomes smallest (because $\mu_T$ is largest), and if the constraint binds tightly, $q_T \to 0$ at the boundary. This is the classic *declining extraction path*.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Demand: $p = 100 - 2q$.
- Marginal cost: $c = 20$.
- Stock: $R_0 = 200$.
- Discount rate: $r = 0.10$ (10%).
- Planning horizon: $T = 2$ (three periods: $t = 0, 1, 2$).

**Step 1: Compute the geometric series sum.**

$$\sum_{t=0}^{2} (1.10)^t = 1 + 1.10 + 1.21 = 3.31$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerator.**

$$(a - c)(T+1) - b R_0 = (100 - 20)(3) - (2)(200) = 240 - 400 = -160$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mu_0$.**

$$\mu_0 = \frac{-160}{3.31} \approx -48.34$$

Since $\mu_0 < 0$, this is *infeasible* — it means demand and stock cannot simultaneously be satisfied over only 3 periods; either $T$ must increase, $R_0$ must shrink, or demand must soften.

**Step 4: Try $T = 5$.**

$$\sum_{t=0}^{5} (1.10)^t = \frac{1.10^6 - 1}{0.10} = \frac{1.771561 - 1}{0.10} = 7.71561$$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{(80)(6) - (2)(200)}{7.71561} = \frac{480 - 400}{7.71561} = \frac{80}{7.71561} \approx 10.37$$

**Step 5: Price and Quantity Paths.**

| $t$ | $\mu_t = \mu_0 (1.10)^t$ | $p_t = 20 + \mu_t$ | $q_t = (100 - p_t)/2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 10.37 | 30.37 | 34.82 |
| 1 | 11.41 | 31.41 | 34.30 |
| 2 | 12.55 | 32.55 | 33.72 |
| 3 | 13.81 | 33.81 | 33.10 |
| 4 | 15.19 | 35.19 | 32.41 |
| 5 | 16.71 | 36.71 | 31.65 |

**Step 6: Verify Stock Constraint.**

$$\sum q_t \approx 34.82 + 34.30 + 33.72 + 33.10 + 32.41 + 31.65 = 200.00 \;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extraction is declining — exactly the signature of an optimal Hotelling path.** The marginal well (the in-situ value) gets dearer over time, so the firm prefers to extract less today and save the resource for higher future prices.

---
