### 3.4 Derivation of Backstop Activation Time

Suppose a **backstop technology** exists at constant backstop price $P_b$ and is available in infinite quantity at time $T^*$ onward.

The owner will exhaust the natural resource *exactly* when the Hotelling price path reaches the backstop price:

$$P_b = P_0 (1+r)^{T^*} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad T^* = \frac{\ln(P_b / P_0)}{\ln(1+r)}$$

At $T^*$ the resource becomes economically obsolete, so all remaining reserves are extracted in the final period (or in the limit, at infinite rate). This is the formal foundation of **"peak oil"** and the **carbon-budget / stranded-asset** literature.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Hotelling's Rule

| Real-World Phenomenon | Why Hotelling Fails | KTU-Expected Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| OPEC supply restraint | Market power; monopoly markup | Cournot / cartel model |
| Shale revolution (US, 2010s) | $R_0$ re-estimated upward | Stochastic reserve model |
| Solar PV collapse in cost | Backstop arrives earlier than expected | Endogenous technological change |
| Coal stranded assets | Carbon tax forces early exit | Modified Hotelling with Pigouvian tax |
| Sovereign wealth funds | Owner is government, not firm | Social-planner optimum equals Hotelling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Optimal Extraction Decision

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *decision logic* of the social planner (or competitive owner) at each moment in time when deciding *how much* of the exhaustible resource to extract.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT BLOCK: Initial Stock R0"] --> B["STATE BLOCK: In-Situ Price Lambda_t"]
    C["INPUT BLOCK: Demand Curve P_q"] --> B
    D["INPUT BLOCK: Extraction Cost c_q"] --> B
    E["INPUT BLOCK: Discount Rate r"] --> B
    B --> F["DECISION BLOCK: Compute Net Price Mu_t = Lambda_t - c_q"]
    F --> G{"COMPARISON BLOCK: Is Mu_t greater than Future Mu_t+1 over 1+r ?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["ACTION BLOCK: Extract MORE now"]
    G -- No --> I["ACTION BLOCK: Defer Extraction"]
    H --> J["UPDATE BLOCK: Reduce Stock R_t = R_t-1 minus q_t"]
    I --> J
    J --> K{"CONSTRAINT BLOCK: Has Stock hit zero ?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["TERMINAL BLOCK: Switch to Backstop Technology P_b"]
    K -- No --> B
    L --> M["OUTPUT BLOCK: Final Welfare Max PV of Profits"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The owner continuously compares the *instantaneous* scarcity rent $\mu_t$ against the *capitalised* future rent $\mu_{t+1}/(1+r)$. Whenever the discounted future rent exceeds the present rent, the optimal action is to defer extraction. The Hotelling Rule is the *fixed point* at which the owner is exactly indifferent, and consequently extraction follows a smooth declining path.

---

### 4.2 Schematic of the Price-Appreciation Equilibrium Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["P at time 0"] --> P1["P at time 1 equals P0 times 1+r"]
    P1 --> P2["P at time 2 equals P1 times 1+r"]
    P2 --> P3["P at time 3 equals P2 times 1+r"]
    P3 --> PD["... continues growing exponentially ..."]
    PD --> PB["P at time T equals P_b Backstop Price"]
    PB --> EXT["Extract ALL Remaining Reserves in Final Period"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Each arrow multiplies the price by $(1+r)$. The Hotelling equilibrium is a *geometric growth curve* in the $(P, t)$-plane. The flat dashed arrow into $P_b$ represents the backstop-technology ceiling.

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Map: From Micro-First-Order-Conditions to Macro Sustainability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FOC["Micro: FOC gives Mu_t equals 1+r times Mu_t-1"] --> HOT["Hotelling Rule"]
    HOT --> INT["Macro: Rising Scarcity Rent over Time"]
    INT --> HART["Hartwick's Rule: Reinvest Rent in Reproducible Capital"]
    HART --> SUST["Sustainability: Constant Total Capital K_t + R_t"]
    SUST --> WELF["Intergenerational Welfare Maximisation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hartwick's Rule (1977):** A country that exhausts its natural resource stock can maintain *constant total wealth* (and hence sustainable consumption) by investing the Hotelling rent in reproducible capital (machinery, education, infrastructure). Norway's Government Pension Fund Global — funded by North Sea oil revenues — is the textbook real-world example.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **State and explain the Hotelling Rule for the optimal extraction of an exhaustible resource. Why is it called a "no-arbitrage" condition?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Hotelling Rule states that the *net price* (scarcity rent) of an exhaustible resource in the ground must appreciate at a rate equal to the social discount rate:
>
> $$\mu_{t+1} = (1+r)\,\mu_t$$
>
> It is called a no-arbitrage condition because an owner holding one unit of resource in the ground must earn the *same return* as if she had sold the unit today at price $\mu_t$ and invested the proceeds in a risk-free bond yielding rate $r$. If the in-situ price rose *faster* than $r$, arbitrageurs would buy and hoard the resource; if slower, they would sell. Equilibrium forces equality, ensuring the owner's rate of return from holding the resource equals the market interest rate. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Distinguish between *Total In-Situ Price* and *Net Price* of an exhaustible resource. Why does the latter rise over time while the former may not?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Total In-Situ Price** $\lambda_t$ is the maximum amount an owner would accept today to surrender one unit of stock permanently; it equals the *market price* $p_t$ of the extracted commodity. The **Net Price** $\mu_t = p_t - c'(q_t)$ is the *scarcity rent* — the price premium attributable to depletion alone, *after* deducting marginal extraction cost.
>
> The net price necessarily rises over time (by Hotelling's Rule, $\mu_t = \mu_0 e^{rt}$) because each remaining unit becomes scarcer. The market price may *fall* if extraction technology improves (learning-curve cost decline) faster than scarcity rents rise — for example, the falling real price of solar PV despite growing demand. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating market price with net price.** Many students write "$P_{t+1} = (1+r)P_t$" without subtracting marginal cost. This is wrong whenever $c'(q) \ne 0$. **Always write the rule for $\mu_t$, not $p_t$, in the general case.** (Lose up to 4 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting the resource constraint.** The Lagrangian MUST include the stock constraint $\lambda(R_0 - \sum q_t)$. Students who drop it lose the entire FOC derivation. (Lose up to 5 marks.)
> 3. **Mixing discrete and continuous time.** Be consistent. The discrete-time recursion $(1+r)$ becomes the continuous growth rate $r$ in continuous time — they are NOT identical multipliers.
> 4. **Ignoring the backstop technology.** Without a backstop, the model has no finite endpoint. Always specify either "infinite horizon with backstop" or "finite horizon $T$" explicitly.
> 5. **Arithmetic slips in geometric series.** Memorise $\sum_{t=0}^{T}(1+r)^t = \frac{(1+r)^{T+1}-1}{r}$. Many students compute $\frac{(1+r)^T - 1}{r}$ — off by one period — and silently carry the error.

---

#### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Question A:**
*"An economy is endowed with a finite stock of an exhaustible resource $R_0 = 300$ units. The inverse demand for the extracted commodity is $p_t = 200 - q_t$. The constant marginal cost of extraction is $c = 20$. The social discount rate is $r = 10\%$. The planning horizon is $T = 4$ periods.*
*(a) [7 marks] Derive the optimal extraction path $\{q_t\}_{t=0}^{4}$ and price path $\{p_t\}_{t=0}^{4}$ using the Hotelling Rule. Show every step.*
*(b) [7 marks] Suppose a backstop technology becomes available at price $P_b = 80$ after period $T^*$. Determine $T^*$ and recompute the optimal path. Comment on the policy implication."*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Set up the Lagrangian** *(1 mark)*

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=0}^{4} \bigl[p_t q_t - c(q_t)\bigr](1.10)^{-t} + \lambda\left[R_0 - \sum_{t=0}^{4} q_t\right]$$

Substituting $p_t = 200 - q_t$, $c = 20$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=0}^{4} \bigl[(200 - q_t)q_t - 20 q_t\bigr](1.10)^{-t} + \lambda\left[300 - \sum_{t=0}^{4} q_t\right]$$

**Step 2: First-Order Condition** *(1 mark)*

Differentiating w.r.t. $q_t$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_t} = (200 - 2q_t - 20)(1.10)^{-t} - \lambda = 0$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad (180 - 2q_t)(1.10)^{-t} = \lambda \quad \forall t$$

**Step 3: Equate Across Periods — Apply Hotelling** *(1 mark)*

For any two periods $t$ and $t+1$:

$$(180 - 2q_t) = \frac{180 - 2q_{t+1}}{1.10}$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad 180 - 2q_{t+1} = 1.10(180 - 2q_t)$$

**Step 4: Solve the Difference Equation** *(1 mark)*

Define $\mu_t = 180 - 2q_t$. Then $\mu_t = (1.10)^t \mu_0$, giving:

$$q_t = \frac{180 - \mu_0 (1.10)^t}{2}$$

**Step 5: Apply Stock Constraint** *(1 mark)*

$$\sum_{t=0}^{4} q_t = 300$$

$$\frac{1}{2}\left[5 \cdot 180 - \mu_0 \sum_{t=0}^{4}(1.10)^t\right] = 300$$

**Step 6: Compute the Geometric Series**

$$\sum_{t=0}^{4}(1.10)^t = 1 + 1.10 + 1.21 + 1.331 + 1.4641 = 6.1051$$

**Step 7: Solve for $\mu_0$** *(1 mark)*

$$\frac{900 - 6.1051\,\mu_0}{2} = 300 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 900 - 6.1051\,\mu_0 = 600$$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{300}{6.1051} \approx 49.14$$

**Step 8: Tabulate the Solution** *(1 mark)*

| $t$ | $\mu_t$ | $p_t = 20 + \mu_t$ | $q_t = (180 - \mu_t)/2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 49.14 | 69.14 | 65.43 |
| 1 | 54.05 | 74.05 | 62.97 |
| 2 | 59.46 | 79.46 | 60.27 |
| 3 | 65.40 | 85.40 | 57.30 |
| 4 | 71.94 | 91.94 | 54.03 |
| **Sum** | | | **300.00 ✓** |

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Backstop Activation Condition** *(2 marks)*

The natural resource is depleted *when* the Hotelling path first reaches the backstop price $P_b = 80$:

$$P_b = P_0 (1.10)^{T^*} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 80 = 69.14 (1.10)^{T^*}$$

$$(1.10)^{T^*} = \frac{80}{69.14} \approx 1.1571$$

**Step 2: Solve for $T^*$** *(2 marks)*

$$T^* \cdot \ln(1.10) = \ln(1.1571)$$

$$T^* = \frac{\ln(1.1571)}{\ln(1.10)} = \frac{0.1460}{0.0953} \approx 1.53$$

So the backstop activates **between $t=1$ and $t=2$**, closer to $t=2$. Since extraction must be non-negative, the optimal policy is to extract all remaining reserves at $t = 2$ (the period in which the Hotelling price would first breach $P_b$).

**Step 3: Recompute the Path with Backstop Active at $t \ge 2$** *(2 marks)*

For $t = 0, 1$, Hotelling rule applies as before with $\mu_0 = 49.14$:

- $p_0 = 69.14$, $q_0 = 65.43$
- $p_1 = 74.05$, $q_1 = 62.97$

At $t = 2$, the market price is capped at $P_b = 80$, so:
$$q_2 = 300 - 65.43 - 62.97 = 171.60$$

For $t \ge 3$, the natural resource is exhausted; the backstop provides unlimited supply at $P_b = 80$, with $q_t^{\text{backstop}} = D(80) = 200 - 80 = 120$ units per period.

**Step 4: Policy Implication** *(1 mark)*

> The backstop accelerates resource exhaustion by 3 periods (from $t=4$ down to $t=2$). Crucially, the **scarcity rent is foregone** because the backstop caps the price — the social planner must either (i) impose a Pigouvian tax to internalise inter-generational externalities, or (ii) nationalise the resource and use the backstop savings as a Sovereign Wealth Fund à la Hartwick's Rule.

---

#### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Question B:**
*"The marginal cost of extracting an exhaustible resource rises with cumulative extraction due to declining ore-grade. Specifically, $c(q_t) = q_t^2 / 100$, while the inverse demand is $p_t = 500 - 0.5 q_t$. Initial stock $R_0 = 1000$, discount rate $r = 5\%$, planning horizon $T = 3$.*
*(a) [7 marks] Set up the social planner's optimisation problem. Derive the Hotelling Rule for this cost-rising case and show that the in-situ price grows faster than the interest rate.*
*(b) [7 marks] Compute the optimal extraction $\{q_t\}$ and discuss what happens to the consumer surplus over the planning horizon."*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Set up the Lagrangian** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\max_{\{q_t\}} \sum_{t=0}^{3}\bigl[(500 - 0.5 q_t)q_t - q_t^2/100\bigr](1.05)^{-t}$$

subject to $\sum_{t=0}^{3} q_t = 1000$.

**Step 2: Compute Marginal Revenue and Marginal Cost** *(1 mark)*

- Marginal revenue: $MR_t = 500 - q_t$
- Marginal cost: $c'(q_t) = q_t/50$

**Step 3: FOC** *(1 mark)*

$$(500 - q_t - q_t/50)(1.05)^{-t} = \lambda$$

$$\Longrightarrow \quad (500 - 1.02\,q_t)(1.05)^{-t} = \lambda$$

**Step 4: Hotelling's Rule for Net Price** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\mu_t = 500 - 1.02\,q_t \quad\text{and}\quad \mu_{t+1} = (1.05)\,\mu_t$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;q_t = \frac{500 - \mu_0(1.05)^t}{1.02}\;}$$

**Step 5: Why Does In-Situ Price Grow Faster?** *(1 mark)*

Because marginal cost rises with cumulative extraction, the *net price* (in-situ rent) must rise not only at rate $r = 5\%$ but also absorb the rising marginal cost. The **modified Hotelling rule** is:

$$\frac{\dot{\mu}_t}{\mu_t} = r + \frac{\dot{c}'(q_t)}{\mu_t} > r$$

**Step 6: Apparent Paradox Resolved** *(1 mark)*

This is not a violation of Hotelling — it is precisely Hotelling's rule applied to the *correct* net price, which now grows *faster* than $r$ to compensate owners for the rising cost of mining lower-grade ore. The market price $p_t$ grows even faster.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Stock Constraint** *(2 marks)*

$$\sum_{t=0}^{3} q_t = 1000 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{1}{1.02}\sum_{t=0}^{3}\bigl[500 - \mu_0(1.05)^t\bigr] = 1000$$

**Step 2: Geometric Series** *(1 mark)*

$$\sum_{t=0}^{3}(1.05)^t = \frac{1.05^4 - 1}{0.05} = \frac{1.21550625 - 1}{0.05} = 4.310125$$

**Step 3: Solve for $\mu_0$** *(2 marks)*

$$\frac{1}{1.02}\bigl[4 \times 500 - 4.310125\,\mu_0\bigr] = 1000$$

$$2000 - 4.310125\,\mu_0 = 1020$$

$$\mu_0 = \frac{980}{4.310125} \approx 227.35$$

**Step 4: Compute the Path** *(1 mark)*

| $t$ | $\mu_t = 227.35(1.05)^t$ | $q_t = (500 - \mu_t)/1.02$ | $p_t = 500 - 0.5 q_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 227.35 | 267.50 | 366.25 |
| 1 | 238.72 | 256.00 | 372.00 |
| 2 | 250.66 | 244.45 | 377.78 |
| 3 | 263.19 | 232.50 | 383.75 |
| **Sum** | | **1000.45 ≈ 1000 ✓** | |

**Step 5: Consumer Surplus Analysis** *(1 mark)*

Consumer surplus in period $t$:

$$CS_t = \tfrac{1}{2}(500 - p_t) q_t = \tfrac{1}{2}(500 - p_t)^2$$

- $CS_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}(133.75)^2 \approx 8945$
- $CS_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}(128.00)^2 \approx 8192$
- $CS_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(122.22)^2 \approx 7469$
- $CS_3 = \tfrac{1}{2}(116.25)^2 \approx 6757$

Consumer surplus *declines* over time because the market price rises (from 366.25 to 383.75) and the quantity shrinks. **This is a welfare loss to current generations from a policy of inter-temporal optimisation** — a classic equity-vs-efficiency trade-off in environmental economics. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4 Hotelling Theory**

- [ ] **Hotelling's Rule (Discrete):** $\mu_{t+1} = (1+r)\,\mu_t$
- [ ] **Hotelling's Rule (Continuous):** $\dot{\mu}_t / \mu_t = r$
- [ ] **Always state the rule for NET price $\mu_t = p_t - c'(q_t)$, NOT market price $p_t$.**
- [ ] **Stock constraint:** $\sum q_t = R_0$ — the binding constraint that distinguishes exhaustible from renewable.
- [ ] **Modified rule with rising cost:** $\mu_t$ rises *faster* than $r$ to absorb the cost increase.
- [ ] **Modified rule with declining cost (e.g., learning curve):** $p_t$ may *fall* even as $\mu_t$ rises.
- [ ] **Backstop activation:** $T^* = \ln(P_b/P_0)/\ln(1+r)$ — the resource is exhausted when the Hotelling path hits the backstop ceiling.
- [ ] **Hartwick's Rule:** Reinvest 100% of Hotelling rent in reproducible capital to maintain constant total wealth.
- [ ] **Market failure forms:** monopoly power, uncertainty about $R_0$, interest-rate distortion, externalities.
- [ ] **Real-world applications:** OPEC pricing, sovereign wealth funds, stranded-asset risk, mine planning, carbon budgets, Peak Oil debates.
- [ ] **Numerical exam tip:** Memorise the geometric-series formula and verify the stock constraint numerically after every calculation.
- [ ] **Examiner pitfall to avoid:** Don't write "Hotelling Rule: $P_{t+1} = (1+r)P_t$" without subtracting marginal cost — this loses marks whenever extraction is costly.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Economic Theory of Depletable Resources**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Resources Accounting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Resources Accounting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Resource Accounting (NRA)** is a comprehensive statistical and economic framework designed to systematically **measure, monitor, and report the stocks and flows of natural resources** (both physical units and monetary values) within a defined geographical boundary (nation, state, or firm) over a specific accounting period, and to integrate these environmental data into the conventional System of National Accounts (SNA).

According to the **United Nations System of Environmental-Economic Accounting (UN-SEEA) Central Framework (2014)**, NRA is built on the fundamental premise that natural resources constitute a form of *Natural Capital* that generates a continuous flow of goods, ecosystem services, and raw materials — and therefore must be **depreciated** just like man-made (produced) capital when consumed for economic production.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Categories of Natural Capital

| Natural Capital Type | Examples | Stock vs Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Mineral & Energy Resources | Coal, Oil, Iron, Natural Gas | Stock (depletable) |
| Land Resources | Soil, Sub-soil Assets | Stock |
| Soil Resources | Topsoil depth, Quality | Stock (regenerative) |
| Timber & Forest Resources | Standing Timber Volume | Stock (biological) |
| Aquatic Resources | Fish, Aquifer Reserves | Stock (biological) |
| Water Resources | River flows, Groundwater | Stock + Flow |
| Atmospheric Resources | Air Quality, Carbon Sinks | Stock (absorptive) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Farm Balance Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a farmer who earns ₹10,00,000 in a year by selling 5 acres of his 50-acre ancestral land. In conventional accounting, the farmer's income is recorded as ₹10,00,000. But ecologically and economically, he has *also* lost ₹5,00,000 worth of land asset (5 acres × ₹1,00,000/acre) — yet this loss is **NOT** subtracted. He looks rich on paper, but his **net wealth has declined by 50% of the land asset value over 10 years**.

**Natural Resource Accounting does exactly this correction at the national level.** It tells the government: *"Your GDP is rising, but the country is consuming its mineral/forest/water wealth to do so. Subtract that depletion to get the TRUE sustainable income."*

## 1.3 Why NRA is Critical for KTU 2024 — The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Justifications for NRA (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **Sustainability Indicator:** Tracks whether current consumption rate is replaceable by regeneration.
> 2. **Policy Corrective:** Highlights hidden subsidies and over-extraction (e.g., free groundwater).
> 3. **Inter-Generational Equity:** Ensures that future generations inherit a non-depleted stock of natural assets.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Standard Unit of Energy Conversion:** 1 tonne of oil equivalent (toe) = **41.868 GJ**
- **Universal Accounting Period:** Usually **1 fiscal year (April–March in India)**
- **Standard Discount Rate for Hotelling Rent:** Social discount rate, typically **3% to 10%** depending on country
- **Currency Convention:** All monetary values expressed in *constant* (real) terms using a base-year deflator to remove inflation effects

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Architecture of NRA

NRA is not a single equation — it is a **multi-layered accounting architecture** with the following logical hierarchy:

### Step 1: Stock Measurement (Opening and Closing Balances)
At the start (T₀) and end (T₁) of the accounting period, measure the *physical quantity* of each natural resource.

$$\text{Stock}_{\text{closing}} = \text{Stock}_{\text{opening}} + \text{Natural Growth} - \text{Extraction} - \text{Natural Losses}$$

### Step 2: Flow Measurement
Track the additions (discoveries, regeneration, discoveries) and reductions (extraction, degradation, emissions) during the year.

### Step 3: Monetary Valuation
Convert the physical changes into monetary terms using market prices, shadow prices, or imputed values.

### Step 4: Adjustment to Macro Aggregates
Deduct the **depletion-adjusted value** from Gross Domestic Product (GDP) to obtain an **Environmentally Adjusted Net Domestic Product (EDP)** — popularly known as **Green GDP**.

## 2.2 The Two Branches of NRA

> [!NOTE]
> **Branch A — Physical Resource Accounting:** Records only the *quantitative* changes (cubic metres of water, tonnes of coal, hectares of forest). No monetary value assigned.
>
> **Branch B — Monetary Resource Accounting:** Assigns a *rupee/dollar value* to the stocks and flows, enabling integration with national income accounts.

## 2.3 Three Standard Methods of Monetary Valuation (KTU High-Yield)

| Method | Core Logic | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Net Price Method (Resource Rent)** | Value = (Market Price − Average Cost) × Quantity Extracted | Sub-soil assets, minerals, oil |
| **User Cost (Hotelling) Approach** | Resource rent + expected appreciation of the resource value over time | Depletable resources (Hotelling's Rule) |
| **Maintenance / Replacement Cost** | Cost required to *replace* the depleted resource (e.g., reforestation cost) | Forest, soil, fish stock |

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **must-memorize equations** for any KTU examination on Natural Resource Accounting. Every equation is mathematically verifiable from first principles of welfare economics.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resource Rent (Net Price)** | $R_t = (P_t - C_t) \cdot Q_t$ | Where $P$ = market price, $C$ = unit extraction cost, $Q$ = quantity |
| 2 | **Unit Resource Rent** | $r_t = (P_t - C_t)$ | Per-unit profit from extraction |
| 3 | **Hotelling's Rule (Steady State)** | $P_t - C_t = (P_{t+1} - C_{t+1}) \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+\rho}$ | The resource rent grows at the discount rate $\rho$ |
| 4 | **Adjusted Net Domestic Product (EDP)** | $\text{EDP} = \text{NDP} - D_n - D_p$ | Where $D_n$ = depreciation of natural capital, $D_p$ = depreciation of produced capital |
| 5 | **Depletion of Natural Resource** | $D_n = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i \cdot q_i)$ | Sum of (resource rent × quantity extracted) across all $N$ resources |
| 6 | **Genuine Savings (GS)** | $\text{GS} = GNS - C_p - C_n + E$ | Gross Nat'l Savings minus produced capital consumption, minus natural capital depletion, plus education expenditure |
| 7 | **Genuine Investment (GNI)** | $\text{GNI} = I_p - C_p - C_n + E$ | Net of all capital depreciations |
| 8 | **Present Value of In-situ Resource** | $V_0 = \dfrac{R_t}{\rho}$ | Resource rent capitalized at the social discount rate |
| 9 | **Hartwick Rule (Sustainability)** | $C_n \le I_p$ | Total rent from natural resources must be reinvested in man-made capital |
| 10 | **Green GDP Ratio** | $\text{Green GDP \%} = \dfrac{\text{EDP}}{\text{GDP}} \times 100$ | How much of conventional GDP survives after ecological adjustment |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When writing Hotelling's Rule, students often incorrectly write it as $P_t = P_{t+1}$ or miss the discount factor $\frac{1}{1+\rho}$. The CORRECT form includes the in-situ net price growing at the rate of discount.

## 2.5 The Hartwick Rule — The Heart of Sustainability Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hartwick's Rule (1977):** A country can maintain a *constant* level of consumption across generations IF it invests **all** rents from exhaustible resource extraction into reproducible (man-made) capital. The rule is the foundational bridge between **weak sustainability** and **inter-generational equity** in KTU syllabus.

Mathematically, if $R$ is the total resource rent in period $t$ and $K$ is man-made capital stock:

$$C_t \le \text{constant} \quad \text{iff} \quad \int_{0}^{\infty} R_t \, dt \le K_0 + \int_{0}^{\infty} I_t \, dt$$

Where $C_t$ is aggregate consumption and $I_t$ is gross investment in reproducible capital.

## 2.6 UN System of Environmental-Economic Accounting (SEEA)

The **UN-SEEA** is the international statistical standard endorsed by the UN Statistical Commission in 2012. It has two layers:

1. **SEEA Central Framework (SEEA-CF):** Standard physical and monetary accounts for water, energy, land, and emissions.
2. **SEEA Experimental Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA-EEA):** Measures ecosystem services and condition at landscape scale (experimental as of 2024).

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of NRA in Engineering & Policy

- **Mining sector:** Determines the *true* tax base for royalty and auction pricing of coal/iron blocks.
- **Hydropower project EIA:** Computes the opportunity cost of submergence of forest land.
- **Fisheries management:** Sets Total Allowable Catch (TAC) by tracking Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY).
- **Climate policy:** Forms the basis for **Net-Zero accounting** under Article 6 of the Paris Agreement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Hotelling User Cost Formula

**Premise:** A firm owns a fixed stock $S_0$ of a depletable resource. It extracts quantity $q_t$ at time $t$, earning a price $P_t$ and incurring unit cost $C_t$. The social discount rate is $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Total Profit in Discrete Time:**
The firm wants to maximize the present value of total profit over $T$ periods:

$$V = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(P_t - C_t) \cdot q_t}{(1+\rho)^t}$$

**Step 2 — Stock Constraint:**
The cumulative extraction cannot exceed the initial stock:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{T} q_t \le S_0$$

**Step 3 — First-Order Condition (Lagrangian Multiplier $\lambda$):**
Set up the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(P_t - C_t) \cdot q_t}{(1+\rho)^t} - \lambda \cdot \left( \sum_{t=0}^{T} q_t - S_0 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Partial Derivative w.r.t. $q_t$:**
Differentiate $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $q_t$ and set equal to zero (interior optimum):

$$\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_t} = \dfrac{P_t - C_t}{(1+\rho)^t} - \lambda = 0$$

**Step 5 — The In-Situ Net Price (Shadow Value of the Resource):**
Rearrange to obtain the **in-situ price** $\lambda$, which is the discounted value of future rents per unit of resource left in ground:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{P_t - C_t}{(1+\rho)^t}$$

**Step 6 — Same Condition for Time $t+1$:**
Applying the same first-order condition to period $t+1$:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{P_{t+1} - C_{t+1}}{(1+\rho)^{t+1}}$$

**Step 7 — Equate and Rearrange to Obtain Hotelling's Rule:**

$$\dfrac{P_t - C_t}{(1+\rho)^t} = \dfrac{P_{t+1} - C_{t+1}}{(1+\rho)^{t+1}}$$

Multiply both sides by $(1+\rho)^{t+1}$:

$$(P_t - C_t) \cdot (1+\rho) = P_{t+1} - C_{t+1}$$

**Final Result — Hotelling's Rule:**

$$\boxed{P_{t+1} - C_{t+1} = (1+\rho) \cdot (P_t - C_t)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Economic Interpretation:** The per-unit resource rent must grow at exactly the social discount rate $\rho$. This is also called the *"Hotelling r% rule"* — a fundamental result in environmental economics. KTU often tests this in **Part A (3 marks)**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Adjusted Net Domestic Product (Green GDP)

**Step 1 — Start with Conventional Net Domestic Product (NDP):**
NDP is the conventional measure of national income after accounting for *produced capital* depreciation:

$$\text{NDP} = C + I_p + G + (X - M) - D_p$$

Where $D_p$ is depreciation of produced capital, $I_p$ is gross investment in produced capital.

**Step 2 — Identify Two Additional Environmental Depreciation Terms:**

- $D_n$ = Depletion of natural capital (minerals, oil, forests)
- $D_e$ = Costs of environmental degradation (pollution, health costs, CO₂ damage)

**Step 3 — Subtract Both from NDP:**

$$\text{EDP} = \text{NDP} - D_n - D_e$$

**Step 4 — Express in Expanded Form:**

$$\text{EDP} = C + I_p + G + (X - M) - D_p - D_n - D_e$$

**Step 5 — Regroup to Show Genuine Savings:**

$$\text{EDP} = C + (I_p - D_p) - D_n - D_e + G + (X-M)$$

Define **Genuine Net Investment** as $I_p - D_p - D_n$ (net investment after natural depletion):

$$\text{EDP} = C + I_p - D_p - D_n - D_e + G + (X-M)$$

**Final Compact Formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{EDP} = \text{GDP} - D_p - D_n - D_e}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many Indian states (Goa, Karnataka) have piloted Green GDP calculations. India's national-level Green GDP initiative is being scaled up under the **MoEFCC's NRM (Natural Resource Accounting) Programme**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Genuine Savings (World Bank Methodology)

The World Bank computes **Adjusted Net Savings (ANS)** as a measure of national sustainability.

**Step 1 — Start with Gross National Savings (GNS):**

$$\text{GNS} = S_p + S_g + S_x$$

Where $S_p$ = private savings, $S_g$ = government savings, $S_x$ = net foreign savings.

**Step 2 — Subtract Produced Capital Depreciation ($C_p$):**

$$\text{NNS} = \text{GNS} - C_p$$

**Step 3 — Subtract Natural Resource Depletion ($C_n$):**

$$\text{NNS}_{\text{adj}} = \text{NNS} - C_n$$

**Step 4 — Add Education Expenditure ($E$) as Human Capital Investment:**

$$\text{ANS} = \text{NNS}_{\text{adj}} + E$$

**Final World Bank Formula:**

$$\boxed{\text{ANS} = \text{GNS} - C_p - C_n + E}$$

**Step 5 — Sustainability Criterion (Pearce-Atkinson):**
The economy is *weakly sustainable* if and only if:

$$\text{ANS} \ge 0$$

If ANS < 0, the country is **eroding its capital base** (mining its future for present consumption).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Problem — Model Question Pattern:**
> A country has the following data for the year 2024:
> - Gross National Savings (GNS) = ₹50,000 crore
> - Depreciation of produced capital ($C_p$) = ₹10,000 crore
> - Depletion of natural resources ($C_n$): Coal = ₹5,000 crore; Oil = ₹4,000 crore; Forest = ₹1,000 crore
> - Education expenditure ($E$) = ₹6,000 crore
> - Pollution damage cost ($D_e$) = ₹3,000 crore
> - GDP = ₹2,00,000 crore

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Total Natural Resource Depletion:**

$$C_n = 5000 + 4000 + 1000 = 10{,}000 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Adjusted Net Savings (ANS):**

$$\text{ANS} = 50{,}000 - 10{,}000 - 10{,}000 + 6{,}000 = 36{,}000 \text{ crore}$$

Since ANS > 0, the country is **weakly sustainable**.

**Step 3 — Compute Environmentally Adjusted Domestic Product (EDP):**

$$\text{EDP} = \text{GDP} - D_p - C_n - D_e = 2{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}000 - 10{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 1{,}77{,}000 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 4 — Green GDP Ratio:**

$$\text{Green GDP \%} = \dfrac{1{,}77{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 88.5\%$$

**Interpretation:** Only **88.5%** of conventional GDP reflects truly sustainable income; **11.5%** is being borrowed from the future.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The SEEA Framework Architecture

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the international accounting architecture endorsed by the United Nations Statistical Commission.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[UN System of National Accounts SNA] --> nodeB[Conventional Macro Accounts]
    nodeA --> nodeC[SEEA Central Framework 2014]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Physical Flow Accounts]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Monetary Flow Accounts]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Asset Stock Accounts]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Water Accounts]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Energy Accounts]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Material Flow Accounts]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Emission Accounts]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Environmental Taxes]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Resource Rent Extraction]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Environmental Protection Expenditure]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Mineral and Energy Resources]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Land and Soil Resources]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Timber Resources]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[Aquatic Resources]
    nodeA --> nodeG[SEEA Experimental Ecosystem Accounting]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Ecosystem Condition]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Ecosystem Services]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[Ecosystem Monetary Valuation]
    nodeB --> nodeH[GDP NDP]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Adjusted Indicators EDP Green GDP]
```

## 4.2 Step-by-Step NRA Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[Step 1: Define Geographical Boundary] --> stepB[Step 2: Identify Resource Categories]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Open Physical Stock Audit]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Record Annual Flows Additions]
    stepC --> stepE[Step 4b: Record Annual Flows Reductions]
    stepD --> stepF[Step 5: Close Physical Stock Audit]
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Apply Valuation Method Net Price or User Cost]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Compute Depreciation of Natural Capital]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Adjust NDP to obtain EDP]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Compute Genuine Savings]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Policy Recommendation and Reporting]
```

## 4.3 Three Pillars of NRA — Classification Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    pillar1[Pillar 1: Physical Accounting] --> sub1[Stock Quantities]
    pillar1 --> sub2[Flow Quantities]
    pillar1 --> sub3[Quality Indicators]
    pillar2[Pillar 2: Monetary Accounting] --> sub4[Net Price Method]
    pillar2 --> sub5[User Cost Hotelling Method]
    pillar2 --> sub6[Replacement Cost Method]
    pillar3[Pillar 3: Hybrid Integrated Accounting] --> sub7[Green GDP]
    pillar3 --> sub8[Genuine Savings]
    pillar3 --> sub9[SEEA Integrated Tables]
```

## 4.4 The Sustainability Decision Rule — Visual Logic

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Compute Adjusted Net Savings ANS] --> decision{Is ANS greater than or equal to zero}
    decision -->|YES| sustain[Country is Weakly Sustainable]
    decision -->|NO| unsustain[Country is Eroding Capital Base]
    sustain --> invest[Reinvest Rents in Reproducible Capital]
    unsustain --> policy[Implement Corrective Policy: Pricing Reforms, Resource Tax, Reforestation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Define Natural Resource Accounting. Mention its two main approaches.

**Model Answer:**

Natural Resource Accounting is a statistical framework for measuring, monitoring, and reporting the **stocks and flows of natural resources** in both physical and monetary terms, and integrating this information with the conventional System of National Accounts (SNA) to obtain a more realistic measure of national income and wealth.

The two main approaches are:

1. **Physical Resource Accounting:** Measures only the quantitative changes in natural resource stocks (e.g., cubic metres of water, tonnes of coal, hectares of forest) without monetary valuation.
2. **Monetary Resource Accounting:** Assigns a monetary value to the stocks and flows using methods such as the **Net Price Method**, **User Cost (Hotelling) Approach**, and **Replacement Cost Method**, allowing integration with national income statistics to compute Green GDP.

---

### Q2. State and explain Hartwick's Rule of sustainable development.

**Model Answer:**

**Hartwick's Rule (1977):** A country can maintain a *constant level of consumption* across generations if it invests **all rents** obtained from the extraction of exhaustible natural resources into reproducible (man-made) capital.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\sum_{t=0}^{\infty} C_t = \text{constant} \quad \text{provided} \quad \int_{0}^{\infty} R_t \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} I_t \, dt$$

Where $R_t$ is the resource rent and $I_t$ is investment in produced capital.

**Example:** Norway's Government Pension Fund (the *sovereign wealth fund*) channels oil revenues into diversified financial assets, embodying Hartwick's Rule in practice. The accumulated fund is now over **$1.5 trillion (approx. ₹12 lakh crore)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — December 2024 Model Question Pattern]**
> *Note: KTU Part B Module questions carry internal choice. We provide TWO alternate questions (A and B); the student attempts ONE.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Choose any ONE)*

**Q. (a) Explain the United Nations System of Environmental-Economic Accounting (SEEA) framework. Discuss the SEEA Central Framework and SEEA Experimental Ecosystem Accounting. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **UN-SEEA framework** is the international statistical standard for environmental-economic accounting, endorsed by the UN Statistical Commission in 2012 and updated in 2024. It is structured in two complementary layers:

**1. SEEA Central Framework (SEEA-CF):**
This is the internationally agreed-upon standard applied worldwide. It consists of:

- **Physical Flow Accounts:** Track the movement of natural inputs (water, energy, raw materials) into the economy, and outputs (emissions, waste, effluents) back into the environment. Examples include Water Flow Accounts, Energy Accounts, and Air Emissions Accounts.
- **Monetary Flow Accounts:** Capture environmental taxes, environmental protection expenditure, and resource rent extraction in monetary terms.
- **Asset Stock Accounts:** Record opening and closing balances of natural assets such as mineral reserves, timber resources, land, and water resources.

**2. SEEA Experimental Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA-EEA):**
This is a more recent and evolving framework (as of 2024 still in experimental stage) that:

- Measures **ecosystem condition** (e.g., forest canopy cover, water quality indices).
- Quantifies **ecosystem services** (e.g., carbon sequestration, pollination, flood control).
- Assigns **monetary values** using techniques from welfare economics (hedonic pricing, contingent valuation, benefit transfer).

The two frameworks are designed to be **complementary**: the Central Framework focuses on *individual resources*; the Ecosystem Accounting focuses on *interconnected ecosystems and their services*.

**Conclusion:** SEEA allows governments to compute the **Adjusted Net Domestic Product (EDP)** or **Green GDP**, ensuring that national income statistics reflect the true cost of environmental degradation. India has adopted SEEA principles in its **NRM Programme of the MoEFCC**.

---

**Q. (b) Derive Hotelling's Rule. Explain its significance in natural resource management. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.1):**

The firm maximizes the present value of profits from a fixed stock $S_0$ of an exhaustible resource, with extraction $q_t$ in each period $t$ and social discount rate $\rho$:

$$V = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(P_t - C_t) \cdot q_t}{(1+\rho)^t}$$

Subject to the constraint $\sum q_t \le S_0$. Setting up the Lagrangian and taking the first-order condition:

$$\dfrac{P_t - C_t}{(1+\rho)^t} = \lambda \quad \forall \, t$$

Equating the in-situ price $\lambda$ across two consecutive periods:

$$\dfrac{P_t - C_t}{(1+\rho)^t} = \dfrac{P_{t+1} - C_{t+1}}{(1+\rho)^{t+1}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P_{t+1} - C_{t+1} = (1+\rho)(P_t - C_t)}$$

**Significance in Natural Resource Management:**

1. **Pricing Guidance:** Suggests that resource owners should price depletion such that the in-situ value grows at the rate of discount.
2. **Sustainability:** If the resource rent grows faster than $\rho$, the resource is being under-utilized; if slower, it is being over-exploited.
3. **Tax Policy:** Provides the theoretical basis for **resource rent taxes** (used in India's coal and mining sectors).
4. **Inter-generational Equity:** Aligns with the concept that resource owners should be compensated for not extracting today, ensuring future generations' interests are protected.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q. (a) What is Genuine Savings? Derive its formula and explain its use as a sustainability indicator. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Genuine Savings (also called **Adjusted Net Savings — ANS**) is a sustainability indicator developed by the World Bank that measures the *true* rate of saving in an economy after accounting for the depletion of natural capital, depreciation of produced capital, and investment in human capital.

**Derivation (from Section 3.3):**

Starting from Gross National Savings (GNS), we sequentially subtract depletion costs and add human capital investment:

$$\text{ANS} = \text{GNS} - C_p - C_n + E$$

Where:
- $\text{GNS}$ = Gross National Savings
- $C_p$ = Consumption of produced capital (depreciation)
- $C_n$ = Depletion of natural capital (minerals, forests, energy)
- $E$ = Current expenditure on education (proxy for human capital formation)

**Sustainability Criterion (Pearce-Atkinson Rule):**

$$\text{ANS} \ge 0 \implies \text{Weak Sustainability Achieved}$$

**Use as a Sustainability Indicator:**

1. **Cross-Country Comparison:** The World Bank publishes ANS for nearly 200 countries. Countries with negative ANS are consuming their natural capital and not replacing it (e.g., many oil-exporting nations show low ANS despite high GDP).
2. **Policy Diagnosis:** A positive ANS indicates the economy is *broadly* on a sustainable path, while a negative ANS is a red flag for policy reform.
3. **Limitation:** Genuine Savings is a measure of *weak* sustainability, which assumes substitutability between natural and man-made capital. It does not capture critical natural capital losses (e.g., extinction of species).

---

**Q. (b) Compute Adjusted Net Savings for a country with the following data and interpret the result. (7 Marks)**

| Item | Value (in ₹ crore) |
|---|---|
| Gross National Savings | 80,000 |
| Depreciation of produced capital | 15,000 |
| Depletion of coal | 6,000 |
| Depletion of oil and gas | 5,000 |
| Net forest depletion | 2,000 |
| CO₂ damage cost | 3,000 |
| Education expenditure | 7,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Total Natural Capital Depletion ($C_n$):**

$$C_n = 6{,}000 + 5{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 13{,}000 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Adjusted Net Savings (ANS):**

$$\text{ANS} = \text{GNS} - C_p - C_n + E$$

$$\text{ANS} = 80{,}000 - 15{,}000 - 13{,}000 + 7{,}000$$

$$\text{ANS} = 59{,}000 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since **ANS = ₹59,000 crore > 0**, the country is **weakly sustainable**. It is generating positive wealth after replacing produced and natural capital depreciation, and also investing in human capital. However, the natural depletion of ₹13,000 crore is a *warning signal* — if the depletion rate accelerates (e.g., rapid coal extraction), ANS could turn negative within a few years.

**Step 4 — Sustainability Ratio:**

$$\text{Sustainability Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{ANS}}{\text{GNS}} = \dfrac{59{,}000}{80{,}000} = 73.75\%$$

The country retains about **73.75%** of its gross savings as *genuine* (non-depleting) savings — a healthy but not exemplary figure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing GDP with NDP:** Many students write Green GDP formula using GDP instead of NDP. The correct base is **NDP** (after produced capital depreciation), then subtract natural capital depreciation.
> 2. **Forgetting the discount factor in Hotelling's Rule:** Always write the in-situ price $\lambda$ as $\frac{P_t - C_t}{(1+\rho)^t}$, not just $(P_t - C_t)$. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Mixing SEEA-CF with SEEA-EEA:** The two are NOT the same. SEEA-CF is the operational standard; SEEA-EEA is still *experimental*. Writing them as equivalent costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping units:** Every numerical answer must specify units (₹ crore, tonnes, hectares, etc.). KTU examiners deduct up to 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Omitting interpretation:** Every numerical calculation MUST end with an economic interpretation. A computation without a conclusion receives only partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Definition of NRA:** Statistical framework integrating natural resource stocks/flows with SNA.
- ☐ **Two branches:** Physical Resource Accounting vs. Monetary Resource Accounting.
- ☐ **Three valuation methods:** Net Price, User Cost (Hotelling), Replacement Cost.
- ☐ **Hotelling's Rule:** $(P_{t+1} - C_{t+1}) = (1+\rho)(P_t - C_t)$ — the in-situ rent grows at the discount rate.
- ☐ **Green GDP (EDP):** $\text{EDP} = \text{NDP} - D_n - D_e$
- ☐ **Adjusted Net Savings:** $\text{ANS} = \text{GNS} - C_p - C_n + E$
- ☐ **Hartwick's Rule:** Reinvest *all* resource rents in reproducible capital for constant consumption.
- ☐ **Sustainability Criterion:** ANS ≥ 0 implies weak sustainability.
- ☐ **SEEA framework:** Central Framework (operational) + Experimental Ecosystem Accounting (under development).
- ☐ **Green GDP Ratio:** $\frac{\text{EDP}}{\text{GDP}} \times 100$
- ☐ **Hartwick + Hotelling together** = the analytical engine of sustainable resource policy.
- ☐ **India's initiative:** MoEFCC's Natural Resource Accounting (NRA) Programme since 2018; Norway's sovereign wealth fund as a real-world example.
- ☐ **Limitation of ANS:** Captures *weak* sustainability only; ignores critical natural capital.

> **End of Module 4 — Natural Resources Accounting Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course PESFT861, Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Forestry and Fisheries Exploitation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Economics of Forestry and Fisheries Exploitation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Environmental Economics of Forestry

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
*Environmental Economics of Forestry* is the branch of resource economics that applies cost–benefit analysis, welfare economics, and intertemporal optimisation to the management of forest ecosystems. It explicitly accounts for the fact that forests are **stock-flow renewable resources** yielding multiple use-values — timber, non-timber forest products (NTFPs), ecosystem services (carbon sequestration, watershed protection, biodiversity) — while simultaneously being subject to market failures, externalities, and intergenerational equity constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Terminology:** A forest is treated as a **natural capital stock** (X, measured in cubic metres or biomass tonnes) that grows according to a biological growth function $F(X)$ and is harvested as a flow $h(t)$ per unit time.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a bank account that pays you interest every year but also lets you withdraw any amount. Forestry economics is the *financial planning* of this account. If you withdraw only the **interest**, your capital stays intact (sustainable yield). If you withdraw the principal, the account shrinks, and eventually collapses. The "interest rate" of a forest is its **biological growth rate** ($r$); the "principal" is the standing volume of trees. The question forestry economics answers is: *How much should we cut, and when, to maximise the present value of all future harvests?*

## 1.2 Environmental Economics of Fisheries

**Formal Definition:**
*Fisheries Economics* is the applied study of the optimal allocation of renewable, common-pool fishery resources between present consumption and future stock preservation. It combines **population biology** (stock dynamics) with **economic theory** (cost, price, effort allocation) to derive bio-economic equilibria under alternative property-rights regimes — open access, regulated, and privatised.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The seminal framework is the **Gordon–Schaefer bio-economic model** (1954), which combines the **Schaefer logistic growth** with **Gordon's open-access rent dissipation** to explain overcapacity and overfishing.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Picture a village pond full of fish that *everyone* can fish in freely (no owner). Each fisher asks: *"If I don't cast my net today, someone else will catch the fish."* So all fishers compete, push in too many boats, and the pond becomes empty. The *rent* (the surplus value of the fish above the cost of catching) is **dissipated** through excessive effort. Fisheries economics formalises this "tragedy" and designs instruments — quotas, taxes, individual transferable quotas (ITQs), property rights — to prevent it.

## 1.3 Why Forestry and Fisheries Are Studied Together

Both share three critical economic features that distinguish them from mineral or fossil-fuel extraction:

| Feature | Forestry | Fisheries |
|---|---|---|
| Resource type | Renewable stock | Renewable stock |
| Growth law | Logistic (or site-specific) | Logistic (Schaefer) |
| Failure mode | Deforestation / biodiversity loss | Overfishing / stock collapse |
| Property rights | Mixed (state, private, communal) | Mostly open-access historically |
| Externalities | Carbon, watershed, biodiversity | By-catch, habitat, food security |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The module unifies these two sectors through the analytical lens of **optimal harvesting of a renewable resource**, the **maximum sustainable yield (MSY)** benchmark, and the **maximum economic yield (MEY)** refinement.

## 1.4 Core Biological & Economic Metrics

- **Standing Stock ($X$):** biomass or volume at time $t$, units: **m³** (forest) or **tonnes** (fish).
- **Growth Function $F(X)$:** natural rate of stock regeneration, units: **m³/year** or **tonnes/year**.
- **Harvest (Catch) $h$:** flow removed, units: **m³/year** or **tonnes/year**.
- **Effort $E$:** number of boats, hours fished, or labour input, unitless or **boat-days**.
- **Catchability Coefficient $q$:** effectiveness of one unit of effort, units: **1/boat-day**.
- **Intrinsic Growth Rate $r$:** maximum percentage growth, units: **1/year**.
- **Carrying Capacity $K$:** equilibrium stock in absence of harvest, units: **m³** or **tonnes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These six variables $(X, h, E, q, r, K)$ are the *lingua franca* of every KTU exam question on bio-economic modelling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biological Foundation — Schaefer Logistic Growth

A renewable stock grows logistically when its growth rate slows as it approaches the **carrying capacity $K$**:

$$F(X) = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)$$

- When $X$ is small, growth ≈ $rX$ (exponential).
- When $X = K$, growth = 0 (saturation).
- Growth is **maximised** at $X = K/2$, giving the famous **MSY**:

$$\text{MSY} = \frac{rK}{4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The "hump" of the parabolic growth function peaks at the **midpoint of the stock** and equals **one-quarter of $rK$**.

## 2.2 The Schaefer Catch Equation

The actual harvest depends on how many boats are fishing and how good their gear is:

$$h = q \cdot E \cdot X$$

- $q$ = catchability coefficient.
- $E$ = effective effort.
- $X$ = stock size.

## 2.3 The Gordon Open-Access Bioeconomic Model

Gordon (1954) embedded Schaefer biology into rent-maximising behaviour. The **total revenue (TR)** and **total cost (TC)** of the fishery are:

$$\text{TR} = p \cdot h = p \cdot q \cdot E \cdot X$$

$$\text{TC} = c \cdot E$$

where $p$ is the ex-vessel price per unit harvest and $c$ is the per-unit cost of effort.

**Pure Economic Rent (Profit):**

$$\pi = p \cdot q \cdot E \cdot X - c \cdot E$$

**Steady-state Open-Access Condition** (rent fully dissipated, $\pi = 0$):

$$p \cdot q \cdot X_{\infty} = c$$

This implies that, on an open-access fishery, the stock is driven down until the **value of the marginal catch equals the marginal cost of effort**.

## 2.4 The Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) and Maximum Economic Yield (MEY)

### MSY — Biological Optimum
Stock and effort that maximise *sustainable physical yield*:

$$X_{\text{MSY}} = \frac{K}{2}, \quad h_{\text{MSY}} = \frac{rK}{4}, \quad E_{\text{MSY}} = \frac{r}{2q}$$

### MEY — Economic Optimum
Stock and effort that maximise *pure economic rent* (sustainable profit):

$$X_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 + \frac{c}{pKa}\right), \quad h_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{rK}{4}\left[1 - \left(\frac{c}{pKa}\right)^{2}\right]$$

where $a$ is the Schaefer catchability intercept (i.e., $a = q \cdot E_{\max}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always remember:** $X_{\text{MEY}} > X_{\text{MSY}}$. The economically optimal stock is **larger** than the biologically optimal stock because leaving more fish in the water raises future catch value and lowers unit costs — a *conservation-friendly* result of basic microeconomics.

## 2.5 The Faustmann Rotation Model for Forestry

The classical optimal rotation model asks: *Given a forest that grows and is harvested, replanted, and harvested again, when is the optimal cut?*

The **Faustmann optimal rotation period $T^{\star}$** solves:

$$V(T) = \frac{P(T) \cdot S(T) \cdot e^{-rT}}{1 - e^{-rT}} - C$$

Differentiating and solving the **Faustmann condition**:

$$S'(T^{\star}) \cdot P(T^{\star}) = \frac{r \cdot [P(T^{\star}) \cdot S(T^{\star})]}{1 - e^{-rT^{\star}}}$$

**Plain-English reading:** Cut when the *value increment from one more year of growth* equals the *opportunity cost of letting the timber stand* (the foregone interest + the value of being able to harvest sooner).

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher discount rate ⇒ shorter rotation** (plant fast-growing pine). **Lower discount rate ⇒ longer rotation** (allow old-growth). This is a recurring KTU question.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Boundary / Limiting Case | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logistic growth | $F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$ | $F(0)=0,\ F(K)=0$ | stock-units / year |
| 2 | Schaefer catch | $h = qEX$ | $q$ constant | stock-units / year |
| 3 | Sustainable catch at $X$ | $h_s(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$ | max at $X = K/2$ | stock-units / year |
| 4 | MSY | $\text{MSY} = rK/4$ | $X_{\text{MSY}}=K/2$ | stock-units / year |
| 5 | Open-access stock | $X_{\infty} = c/(pq)$ | rent = 0 | stock-units |
| 6 | Economic rent | $\pi = pqEX - cE$ | max ⇒ MEY | currency / year |
| 7 | MEY stock | $X_{\text{MEY}} = (K/2)[1 + c/(pKa)]$ | $X_{\text{MEY}} > K/2$ | stock-units |
| 8 | MEY effort | $E_{\text{MEY}} = (r/2q)[1 - c/(pKa)]$ | always $<E_{\text{MSY}}$ | effort |
| 9 | Faustmann rotation | $S'(T) = rS(T)/[1-e^{-rT}]$ | $r\uparrow \Rightarrow T^{\star}\downarrow$ | years |
| 10 | Net Present Value (forest) | $\text{NPV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{R_t}{(1+\rho)^t} - C_0$ | $\rho$ = social discount rate | currency |
| 11 | Carbon sequestration value | $V_C = P_{CO_2} \cdot \beta \cdot X$ | $P_{CO_2}$ = carbon price | currency / year |
| 12 | Externality Pigouvian tax | $t^{\star} = \text{MEC at efficient }X$ | equals marginal damage | currency / unit |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $h = qX$ (forgetting effort) or write $F(X) = rX$ (forgetting the logistic dampener). The logistic form $rX(1 - X/K)$ is **the** form examiners expect.

## 2.7 Property Rights & Policy Instruments

| Regime | Behaviour | Long-run stock | Long-run rent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open access | $pqX = c$ | $X_{\infty} < X_{\text{MSY}}$ | 0 (dissipated) |
| Regulated (MSY) | $X = K/2$ | $K/2$ | Positive (small) |
| Regulated (MEY) | $X = X_{\text{MEY}}$ | $K/2 < X_{\text{MEY}} < K$ | Maximum sustainable |
| ITQ / Private | Effort internalised | Approaches $K$ | Maximised |

**Policy Instruments (KTU-favourite list):**

- **Individual Transferable Quotas (ITQs):** allocate catch shares, allow trading.
- **Pigouvian tax:** $t = \text{Marginal External Cost (MEC)}$ at efficient stock.
- **Catch limits vs effort limits:** catch limits are biologically safer.
- **Marine Protected Areas (MPAs):** spatial closure — a *non-market* instrument.
- **REDD+ (forestry):** Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and Forest Degradation — international payment for ecosystem services.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MSY from the Logistic Growth Function

**Step 1: Write the steady-state sustainable catch.**
At a steady state, harvest equals natural growth: $h_s(X) = F(X)$.

$$h_s(X) = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) = rX - \frac{rX^2}{K}$$

**Step 2: Maximise with respect to $X$ by taking the first derivative.**

$$\frac{dh_s}{dX} = r - \frac{2rX}{K}$$

**Step 3: Set the derivative to zero to find the critical point.**

$$r - \frac{2rX^{\star}}{K} = 0$$

$$2rX^{\star} = rK \quad \Rightarrow \quad X^{\star} = \frac{K}{2}$$

This is the **MSY stock**. The factor of 2 in the derivative is the source of the "half-K" result.

**Step 4: Substitute $X^{\star} = K/2$ back into the harvest function.**

$$h_s\!\left(\frac{K}{2}\right) = r \cdot \frac{K}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{K/2}{K}\right) = \frac{rK}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{rK}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MSY} = \frac{rK}{4}, \quad X_{\text{MSY}} = \frac{K}{2}}$$

**Step 5: Confirm second-order concavity.**

$$\frac{d^2 h_s}{dX^2} = -\frac{2r}{K} < 0$$

Since the second derivative is negative, the critical point is a **maximum**, not a minimum. The growth function is a downward parabola in $X$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gordon Open-Access Equilibrium Stock

**Step 1: Write the rent (profit) function.**

$$\pi(E, X) = p \cdot q \cdot E \cdot X - c \cdot E$$

**Step 2: Apply the open-access condition.** On an open-access fishery, fishers enter until economic rent is fully dissipated (free entry $\Rightarrow$ zero profit in long run):

$$\pi = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for $X_{\infty}$.**

$$p q E X_{\infty} - c E = 0$$

$$E (p q X_{\infty} - c) = 0$$

Since $E > 0$ (the fishery is in use):

$$p q X_{\infty} = c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{p q}}$$

**Step 4: Compare with MSY stock.** Substitute $X_{\infty}$ into the catch equation:

$$h_{\infty} = qE \cdot X_{\infty} = qE \cdot \frac{c}{pq} = \frac{cE}{p}$$

The **sustainable open-access catch equals total cost / price** — i.e., fishermen collectively spend exactly the gross value of the catch as effort cost. **No surplus remains for society.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MEY Stock and Effort

**Step 1: Write the steady-state stock equation.** Setting harvest = growth in a managed fishery:

$$qEX = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)$$

Divide both sides by $X$ (assuming $X > 0$):

$$qE = r\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)$$

This is the **steady-state effort–stock relationship** — a downward-sloping line in $(E, X)$ space.

**Step 2: Substitute $E(X)$ into the rent function.**

$$\pi(X) = p q E(X) X - c E(X) = E(X) \cdot (p q X - c)$$

Insert $E(X) = (r/q)(1 - X/K)$:

$$\pi(X) = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)(p q X - c)$$

**Step 3: Maximise $\pi$ with respect to $X$.** Expand and differentiate:

$$\pi(X) = \frac{r}{q}\left(pqX - c - pq \frac{X^2}{K} + \frac{cX}{K}\right)$$

Let $\alpha = pq$, $\beta = c/K$, $\gamma = pq/K$. Then:

$$\pi(X) = \frac{r}{q}\left(\alpha X - c - \gamma X^2 + \beta X\right) = \frac{r}{q}\left(- \gamma X^2 + (\alpha + \beta) X - c\right)$$

Take the first derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\pi}{dX} = \frac{r}{q}\left(-2\gamma X + \alpha + \beta\right) = 0$$

$$X_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{2\gamma} = \frac{pq + c/K}{2pq/K} = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 + \frac{c}{pqK}\right)$$

$$\boxed{X_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 + \frac{c}{pKa}\right) \quad \text{where } a = qE_{\max} \text{ (catchability intercept)}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute back to get effort.**

$$E_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X_{\text{MEY}}}{K}\right) = \frac{r}{q}\left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{c}{2pKa}\right] = \frac{r}{2q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pKa}\right)$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{r}{2q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pKa}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Economic interpretation:** $c/(pKa)$ is the *cost-to-revenue ratio* at maximum effort. As $c \to 0$ (free fishing), $E_{\text{MEY}} \to E_{\text{MSY}}$. As $c \to pKa$, $E_{\text{MEY}} \to 0$ (fishery becomes unprofitable and shuts down).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Faustmann Optimal Forest Rotation

**Step 1: Set up the infinite-horizon timber NPV.** Let the forest be harvested, replanted, and harvested again in perpetuity. Let $S(T)$ be timber volume at age $T$ and $P(T)$ be stumpage price per unit volume. The infinite NPV is a **geometric series** with ratio $e^{-rT}$:

$$\text{NPV} = [P(T)S(T) - C] \cdot \left(1 + e^{-rT} + e^{-2rT} + \dots \right) = \frac{P(T)S(T) - C}{1 - e^{-rT}}$$

where $C$ is the replanting/establishment cost and $r$ is the (real, social) discount rate.

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $T$ and set to zero.** For interior optimum:

$$\frac{d\,\text{NPV}}{dT} = 0$$

Apply the quotient rule:

$$\frac{d\,\text{NPV}}{dT} = \frac{[P'(T)S(T) + P(T)S'(T)](1 - e^{-rT}) - [P(T)S(T) - C](-r e^{-rT})}{(1 - e^{-rT})^2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Multiply through by $(1 - e^{-rT})^2$ and set numerator to zero:**

$$[P'(T)S + PS'](1 - e^{-rT}) + r e^{-rT}(PS - C) = 0$$

Assume a constant price $P$ (so $P' = 0$):

$$P \cdot S'(T)(1 - e^{-rT}) + r e^{-rT}(P \cdot S - C) = 0$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into the Faustmann condition:**

$$P \cdot S'(T) = \frac{r [P \cdot S(T) - C]}{1 - e^{-rT}}$$

$$\boxed{P \cdot S'(T^{\star}) = r \cdot \text{NPV at } T^{\star}}$$

**Plain-English reading:**
*The forest should be cut at age $T^{\star}$ when the marginal value of one more year of growth equals the per-year opportunity cost (the social discount rate) applied to the full NPV of the land.*

## 3.5 Comparative-Static Derivations (KTU-favourite sub-questions)

| Shock | Effect on $X_{\text{MEY}}$ | Effect on $E_{\text{MEY}}$ | Intuition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Price $p \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Higher revenue makes more effort worthwhile at higher stock. |
| Cost $c \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Higher cost ⇒ fish less but at a higher stock to make remaining effort pay. |
| Discount rate $r \uparrow$ | — | — | No direct effect on MEY in steady state; rotation $T^{\star} \downarrow$ in forestry. |
| Carrying capacity $K \uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | More biologically productive ⇒ more harvest possible. |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question)

**Problem:** A lake fishery has $r = 0.4$ /year, $K = 1000$ tonnes, $p = 200$ ₹/kg (or ₹0.2/tonne scaled), $q = 0.001$ per boat-day, $c = 50$ ₹/boat-day. Compute MSY, open-access stock, MEY stock, and MEY effort.

> [!NOTE]
> Use $p = 200{,}000$ ₹/tonne, $q = 0.001$, $c = 50$ ₹/boat-day, $a = qE_{\max}$. We assume $E_{\max}$ large enough that $a$ is not the binding constraint; the relevant ratio $c/(pKa) = 50/(200{,}000 \cdot qK) = 50/(200{,}000 \cdot 0.001 \cdot 1000) = 50/200 = 0.25$.

**Step 1: MSY and MSY stock.**

$$\text{MSY} = \frac{rK}{4} = \frac{0.4 \times 1000}{4} = 100 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

$$X_{\text{MSY}} = \frac{K}{2} = 500 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 2: Open-access stock.**

$$X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{pq} = \frac{50}{200{,}000 \times 0.001} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a *catastrophically* low stock — illustrating that open access collapses the fishery.

**Step 3: MEY stock.**

$$X_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 + \frac{c}{pKa}\right) = 500 \times (1 + 0.25) = 625 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: MEY effort.**

$$E_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{r}{2q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pKa}\right) = \frac{0.4}{0.002} \times (1 - 0.25) = 200 \times 0.75 = 150 \text{ boat-days}$$

**Step 5: Sustainable catch at MEY.**

$$h_{\text{MEY}} = q E_{\text{MEY}} X_{\text{MEY}} = 0.001 \times 150 \times 625 = 93.75 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 6: Verify it is below MSY.** Indeed, $93.75 < 100$. The MEY harvest is **lower than MSY**, but generates positive rent — the classical KTU trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Bio-Economic Decision Flow for a Renewable Resource

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resource Stock X at time t] --> B[Logistic Growth Function F of X]
    B --> C[Sustainable Yield h_s equals F of X]
    A --> D[Effective Fishing Effort E]
    D --> E[Schaefer Catch h equals q times E times X]
    C --> F{Manager Objective}
    E --> F
    F -->|Maximise Yield| G[MSY equals rK over 4 at X equals K over 2]
    F -->|Maximise Rent| H[MEY at X greater than K over 2]
    F -->|No Property Rights| I[Open Access Rent Dissipation]
    G --> J[Policy Choice]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Quotas ITQ]
    J --> L[Pigouvian Tax equals MEC]
    J --> M[MPA Closure]
    J --> N[REDD Plus Forestry]
    K --> O[New Stock Level X at time t plus 1]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Schematic — MSY vs MEY in Stock–Effort Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseSpace[Stock-Effort Phase Plane]
        axisX[Horizontal axis Stock X]
        axisY[Vertical axis Effort E]
    end
    axisX --> P0[Origin O]
    P0 --> P1[Schaefer Steady State Curve qE equals r times 1 minus X over K]
    P1 --> P2[MSY point at X equals K over 2 E equals r over 2q]
    P1 --> P3[MEY point at X greater than K over 2 E smaller than r over 2q]
    P1 --> P4[Open Access point at X equals c over pq E much larger]
    P2 --> P5[Maximum Sustainable Yield equals rK over 4]
    P3 --> P6[Maximum Economic Rent greater than zero]
    P4 --> P7[Rent equals zero Rent Dissipated]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Forest Rotation Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forest Stand Age T] --> B{Meets Faustmann Condition}
    B -->|Yes| C[Harvest at T star]
    B -->|No| D{Compare Marginal Growth r S to Discount r NPV}
    D -->|Marginal Growth greater| E[Wait One More Year]
    D -->|Marginal Growth smaller| F[Harvest Immediately]
    E --> A
    F --> C
    C --> G[Replant and Reset T to 0]
    G --> A
    H[Higher Discount Rate r] --> I[T star decreases]
    J[Lower Discount Rate r] --> K[T star increases]
    H --> B
    J --> B
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Open Access vs MEY on a Single Stock Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Carrying Capacity K] --> B[MSY Stock equals K over 2]
    A --> C[MEY Stock equals K over 2 times 1 plus c over pKa]
    A --> D[Open Access Stock equals c over pq]
    B --> E[Yield equals rK over 4]
    C --> F[Yield smaller than rK over 4 but rent maximum]
    D --> G[Yield very small rent equals zero]
    E --> H[Tradeoff: more fish in the water or more rent]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Policy Choice: ITQ, Tax, MPA, REDD+]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Externalities in Forestry & Fisheries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Harvest Decision h] --> B[Private Cost equals c times E]
    A --> C[External Cost equals MEC times h]
    B --> D[Private Net Benefit]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Is MEC positive}
    E -->|Yes| F[Social Cost greater than Private Cost]
    E -->|No| G[No Externality]
    F --> H[Pigouvian Tax equals MEC at efficient X]
    H --> I[Optimal h less than Private h]
    G --> J[Market Outcome equals Social Optimum]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block is a *steady-state* anchor, not a time-path. The arrows indicate the *direction of causation* in the analytical model, not chronological flow.
