<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) and Maximum Economic Yield (MEY). Why is $X_{\text{MEY}} > X_{\text{MSY}}$?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MSY** *(1 mark)*: MSY is the largest average catch that can be harvested from a stock indefinitely without depleting it. Mathematically, $\text{MSY} = rK/4$, occurring at $X_{\text{MSY}} = K/2$.

- **MEY** *(1 mark)*: MEY is the harvest level that maximises the *pure economic rent* (sustainable profit) of the fishery, not just the physical catch.

- **Why $X_{\text{MEY}} > X_{\text{MSY}}$** *(1 mark)*: Leaving a larger stock in the water (i) raises the marginal productivity of remaining effort, (ii) lowers unit harvesting cost per fish, and (iii) preserves future catch value. Therefore, the economically optimal stock exceeds the biologically optimal stock by the factor $(1 + c/(pKa))$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State the Faustmann condition for the optimal rotation period of a forest. How does the discount rate affect the rotation period?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Faustmann Condition** *(1 mark)*:
$$P \cdot S'(T^{\star}) = \frac{r \cdot [P \cdot S(T^{\star}) - C]}{1 - e^{-rT^{\star}}}$$
The marginal value of one additional year of timber growth must equal the per-year opportunity cost (social discount rate) applied to the infinite-horizon NPV of the land.

- **Discount rate effect** *(1 mark)*: A higher discount rate $r$ shortens the optimal rotation $T^{\star}$ because the opportunity cost of waiting rises; future timber values are penalised. A lower $r$ lengthens $T^{\star}$, favouring older, larger, higher-value trees.

- **Real-world example** *(1 mark)*: In India, private teak plantations use $r \approx 8\text{–}12\%$, giving rotations of 30–50 years; community forests with low $r$ allow 80–100+ year rotations for biodiversity and carbon services.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of the Schaefer logistic growth model, derive the expressions for the Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) and the corresponding stock $X_{\text{MSY}}$. State the second-order condition for a maximum. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Gordon–Schaefer bio-economic model, derive the open-access equilibrium stock $X_{\infty}$ and show that the long-run economic rent is zero. Discuss any two policy instruments that can shift the fishery from open access towards MEY. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Define the logistic growth function.**
A renewable stock $X$ grows according to:
$$F(X) = rX\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Define sustainable catch.**
At a steady state, harvest equals growth: $h_s(X) = F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — First derivative.**
$$\frac{dh_s}{dX} = r - \frac{2rX}{K}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Set to zero and solve.**
$$r - \frac{2rX^{\star}}{K} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{\text{MSY}} = \frac{K}{2}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Substitute back.**
$$h_s(K/2) = r \cdot \frac{K}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{rK}{4} = \text{MSY}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Second-order condition.**
$$\frac{d^2 h_s}{dX^2} = -\frac{2r}{K} < 0$$
The function is strictly concave, so $X = K/2$ is a **global maximum** on $[0, K]$.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Interpretation.**
Biologically, the stock is most productive when it is at half its carrying capacity: enough breeders for high recruitment, yet not so crowded that intra-species competition depresses growth.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Gordon rent equation.**
$$\pi(E, X) = p q E X - c E$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Open-access assumption.**
Free entry drives economic profit to zero in long-run equilibrium: $\pi = 0$.

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Solve for stock.**
$$E(pqX_{\infty} - c) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{\infty} = \frac{c}{pq}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Verify rent = 0.**
Substituting $X_{\infty}$ back: $pqX_{\infty} = c$, so $\pi = E(c - c) = 0$. All surplus value is dissipated as cost of excessive effort.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Instrument 1: ITQ.**
Issue each fisher a share of the total allowable catch equal to $h_{\text{MEY}} / N_{\text{fishers}}$. Tradable, prevents race-for-fish, aligns private incentives with the social optimum.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Instrument 2: Pigouvian tax.**
Impose a tax $t = \text{MEC at } X_{\text{MEY}}$ (Marginal External Cost) per unit of effort. The private cost becomes $c + t$, the new open-access condition yields $X = c'/(pq)$ matching $X_{\text{MEY}}$.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Synthesis.**
Both instruments **internalise the externality of open access**, moving $X$ from $c/(pq)$ upwards towards $X_{\text{MEY}}$ and converting the dissipated rent into sustained social surplus.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Marks are lost when students write $h_s = rX$ (omitting the logistic term) or forget the second-order condition. Always state concavity explicitly.
> **Part (b):** Students often state "ITQ works" without explaining *why* (the tradability + the share allocation rule). Vague policy statements lose 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Maximum Economic Yield (MEY) stock and effort for a regulated fishery using the Gordon–Schaefer framework. Show analytically that $X_{\text{MEY}} > X_{\text{MSY}}$ and $E_{\text{MEY}} < E_{\text{MSY}}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Faustmann model of optimal forest rotation. Derive the Faustmann condition and discuss how (i) a higher social discount rate and (ii) the inclusion of carbon-sequestration value would each affect the optimal rotation period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Steady-state effort–stock relation.**
$$qE = r\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Substitute into rent.**
$$\pi(X) = E(X) \cdot (pqX - c) = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X}{K}\right)(pqX - c)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Differentiate and solve.**
Let $\alpha = pq$ and $\gamma = pq/K$. Then:
$$\pi(X) = \frac{r}{q}\left(-\gamma X^2 + \alpha X - c + \frac{cX}{K}\right)$$

$$\frac{d\pi}{dX} = \frac{r}{q}\left(-2\gamma X + \alpha + \frac{c}{K}\right) = 0$$

$$X_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{\alpha + c/K}{2\gamma} = \frac{K}{2}\left(1 + \frac{c}{pKa}\right)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Derive effort.**
$$E_{\text{MEY}} = \frac{r}{q}\left(1 - \frac{X_{\text{MEY}}}{K}\right) = \frac{r}{2q}\left(1 - \frac{c}{pKa}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Compare with MSY.**
$X_{\text{MSY}} = K/2$, $E_{\text{MSY}} = r/(2q)$. The term $c/(pKa) > 0$ implies:
- $X_{\text{MEY}} = (K/2)(1 + \text{positive}) > K/2 = X_{\text{MSY}}$ ✓
- $E_{\text{MEY}} = (r/2q)(1 - \text{positive}) < r/(2q) = E_{\text{MSY}}$ ✓

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Intuition.**
Optimal management leaves **more biomass in the water** (conservation) and uses **less effort** than the bio-maximum — but generates the maximum possible sustainable economic rent.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Faustmann NPV.**
$$\text{NPV} = \frac{P(T) S(T) - C}{1 - e^{-rT}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Differentiate w.r.t. $T$.**
Applying the quotient rule and setting the numerator to zero:
$$[PS'](1 - e^{-rT}) + r e^{-rT}(PS - C) = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Faustmann condition (assuming constant $P$).**
$$P \cdot S'(T^{\star}) = \frac{r \cdot [P \cdot S(T^{\star}) - C]}{1 - e^{-rT^{\star}}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Interpretation.**
The forest should be cut when the *marginal value increment from one more year of growth* equals the *opportunity cost of waiting* (interest on the entire land NPV).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Effect of higher discount rate.**
A higher $r$ raises the right-hand side of the Faustmann condition (the opportunity cost of waiting). To restore equality, the marginal value $P S'(T)$ must be larger, which occurs at *smaller* $T$. So $T^{\star} \downarrow$ — **shorter rotation, earlier harvest.**

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Effect of adding carbon value.**
If the forest is valued for carbon sequestration, an additional revenue term $V_C = P_{CO_2} \cdot \beta \cdot X$ appears. This raises the NPV and shifts the marginal value curve up, delaying harvest. **$T^{\star} \uparrow$** — longer rotation, greater standing biomass, more carbon storage.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Synthesis.**
A purely financial rotation (high $r$, no carbon) is *short* and *biologically poor*. A social rotation (low $r$, + carbon) is *long* and *biodiversity-rich*. The two diverge precisely because private discount rates exceed social rates and markets ignore the carbon externality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Many students confuse MSY and MEY derivations. The MSY is found by *maximising $h_s(X)$*, the MEY by *maximising $\pi(X)$* — the *same $X$* appears in both, but the objective function differs.
> **Part (b):** The Faustmann condition is often mis-quoted as $S'(T) = rS(T)$ — this is the *single-rotation* rule, **not** the *Faustmann* rule. The full Faustmann condition includes the geometric-series factor $(1 - e^{-rT})^{-1}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Renewable stock** grows logistically: $F(X) = rX(1 - X/K)$; the **maximum growth** occurs at $X = K/2$, giving **MSY** = $rK/4$. *[Recall: half-K, quarter-rK.]*

- **Gordon's open-access model** yields $X_{\infty} = c/(pq)$, where rent = 0 — the **tragedy of the commons** in equation form.

- **MEY** is *always* above MSY in stock and *below* MSY in effort — economically optimal fisheries are **less exploited but more valuable** than biologically optimal ones.

- **Faustmann rotation** uses an infinite geometric series $(1 - e^{-rT})^{-1}$ to discount the perpetual cycle of harvests. The full Faustmann condition is:
$$P \cdot S'(T^{\star}) = r \cdot \text{NPV}_{\text{land}}$$

- **Discount rate** is the most policy-sensitive parameter: high $r$ ⇒ short forestry rotation, fast stock depletion in fisheries; low $r$ ⇒ long rotation, conservation-friendly stock.

- **Externalities** in forestry (carbon, biodiversity, watershed) and fisheries (by-catch, habitat) are corrected by **Pigouvian taxes** (= MEC at efficient $X$) or by **property rights** (ITQs, REDD+).

- **Comparative statics to remember** for any KTU 14-mark question:
  - $p \uparrow$ → $X_{\text{MEY}} \uparrow$, $E_{\text{MEY}} \uparrow$, rent $\uparrow$
  - $c \uparrow$ → $X_{\text{MEY}} \uparrow$, $E_{\text{MEY}} \downarrow$, rent $\downarrow$
  - $K \uparrow$ → MSY $\uparrow$, MEY $\uparrow$
  - $r$ (forestry) $\uparrow$ → $T^{\star} \downarrow$

- **Three policy regimes, one diagram**:
  - Open access: $X = c/(pq)$, rent = 0
  - MSY management: $X = K/2$, small positive rent
  - MEY management: $X = (K/2)(1 + c/(pKa))$, maximum rent

- **Numerical sanity check**: if your computed $X_{\text{MEY}}$ is *less than* $K/2$, the algebra is wrong — recheck the cost term $c/(pKa)$.

- **Syllabus buzzwords** to drop in answers for examiner goodwill: *Gordon–Schaefer model*, *Faustmann condition*, *Maximum Sustainable Yield*, *Maximum Economic Yield*, *rent dissipation*, *ITQ*, *Pigouvian tax*, *MEC = MSC at optimum*, *REDD+*, *carrying capacity*, *social discount rate*.

- **Common 14-mark structure expected by KTU 2024 examiners**: (i) define terms (2 marks), (ii) state the relevant equation (2 marks), (iii) derive critical stock/effort (5 marks), (iv) interpretation + policy (3 marks), (v) neat labelled diagram (2 marks). Always allocate ~5 minutes for the diagram — examiners reward visual clarity generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trade and Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trade and Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Trade and Environment** is the interdisciplinary field within Environmental Economics that examines the bidirectional relationship between international trade liberalization, global commerce flows, and ecological/environmental quality. It investigates how the cross-border movement of goods, services, and capital influences environmental outcomes (pollution emissions, resource depletion, biodiversity loss) and conversely, how environmental policies, regulations, and standards reshape comparative advantage, trade patterns, and economic welfare.

The field is anchored on three foundational KTU 2024 framework pillars:

| Pillar | Core Premise |
|---|---|
| **Trade as Engine of Growth** | Liberalization → Higher GDP → Greater capacity for environmental spending |
| **Trade as Polluter** | Scale Effect → Higher production/consumption → Greater emissions |
| **Trade as Technology Transfer** | Composition & Technique Effects → Cleaner methods, greener products |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Trade and Environment is the study of how openness to international markets alters the **scale**, **composition**, and **technique** of economic activity, and through these channels, the environmental footprint of nations.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### The "Two Rivers Meeting" Analogy
Imagine two mighty rivers converging — the **River of Trade** (commerce, exports, imports, FDI) and the **River of Environment** (forests, clean air, oceans, biodiversity). When they meet, three outcomes are possible:

1. **Clean Confluence** → Trade brings green technology and prosperity that funds conservation (the optimistic view)
2. **Muddy Confluence** → Trade expands dirty industries and pollutes pristine regions (the pessimistic view)
3. **Regulated Confluence** → Smart policies redirect trade flows toward sustainable pathways (the policy view)

Think of a developing country, say **Vietnam**, importing cheap solar panels from China. *Does this trade harm Vietnam's environment?* Initially, disposing old panels might pollute. But long-term, replacing coal plants with solar **reduces** emissions dramatically. This tension captures the essence of Trade and Environment analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU Engineers:** As future technologists, you will design products, supply chains, and processes in a globally traded economy. Understanding whether your innovation causes **carbon leakage** or promotes **green competitiveness** is critical for NEP 2020-aligned sustainability competencies (CO5: Environment & Sustainability).

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics Used in Trade-Environment Analysis

- **Ecological Footprint (EF)** — measured in **global hectares (gha)**
- **Carbon Intensity of Trade (CIT)** — measured in **kg CO₂ per USD of export**
- **Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)** coefficient — measured in **inverted-U shape parameter**
- **Pollution Haven Index (PHI)** — measured in **normalized pollution concentration ratio (0–1)**
- **Trade openness ratio** — `(Exports + Imports) / GDP`

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Antweiler-Becker-Copeland-Taylor (ABCT) Framework

The canonical theoretical decomposition used in KTU syllabus is the **Scale-Composition-Technique (SCT) decomposition** of trade-induced environmental impact. Let $Z$ denote aggregate pollution emissions of a country. Then:

$$Z = \underbrace{S}_{\text{Scale}} \times \underbrace{\left(\frac{Y}{S}\right)}_{\text{Income per unit scale}} \times \underbrace{\left(\frac{Z}{Y}\right)}_{\text{Emissions per unit income}}$$

Where:
- $S$ = scale of economic activity (population × per capita production)
- $Y$ = total national income
- $Z/Y$ = pollution intensity of output

After trade liberalization, the **Scale Effect** rises (output expands), the **Composition Effect** shifts production toward the country's comparative-advantage sector, and the **Technique Effect** changes the pollution intensity per unit of output.

## 2.2 The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)

The EKC hypothesis postulates that environmental degradation first rises, then falls, as income per capita increases — forming an inverted-U.

$$Z_i = \alpha + \beta_1 Y_i + \beta_2 Y_i^2 + \beta_3 X_i + \varepsilon_i$$

Where the inverted-U requires:
$$\beta_1 > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \beta_2 < 0$$

The turning point income is:

$$Y^{*} = -\frac{\beta_1}{2\beta_2}$$

For policy: countries below $Y^{*}$ face rising pollution with growth; above $Y^{*}$, pollution falls.

## 2.3 Pollution Haven Hypothesis (PHH)

The PHH states that stringent environmental regulations in developed countries push dirty industries to relocate to developing countries with lax rules — making the latter **"pollution havens."**

Formally, net FDI inflow into polluting sector $j$ in country $i$:

$$\text{FDI}_{ij} = f\left(\text{MarketSize}_i,\, \text{LaborCost}_i,\, \frac{1}{\text{RegulatoryStringency}_i},\, \text{TradeOpenness}_i\right)$$

Where higher regulatory stringency **reduces** polluting FDI.

## 2.4 Porter Hypothesis (Counter to PHH)

The Porter Hypothesis argues that well-designed environmental regulation can **enhance** competitiveness by stimulating innovation, productivity gains, and first-mover advantages in green technologies.

$$I_{ij} = \phi_0 + \phi_1 \text{Reg}_i + \phi_2 \text{Reg}_i^2 + \phi_3 K_{ij} + u_{ij}$$

Where $I_{ij}$ = innovation in green technology, $\text{Reg}_i$ = regulatory stringency. Porter's "strong" version requires $\phi_1 < 0, \phi_2 > 0$ (inverted-U of regulation on innovation).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Boundary Condition | Engineering / Policy Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **SCT Decomposition** $Z = S \times (Y/S) \times (Z/Y)$ | Scale, Composition, Technique | All terms positive | Diagnoses trade impact on environment |
| 2 | **EKC Equation** $Z = \alpha + \beta_1 Y + \beta_2 Y^2$ | Income $Y$ | $\beta_1 > 0, \beta_2 < 0$ | Predicts long-term pollution trajectory |
| 3 | **EKC Turning Point** $Y^{*} = -\beta_1 / (2\beta_2)$ | Critical income | Real positive | Identifies when growth becomes green |
| 4 | **Trade Openness Ratio** $\text{TO} = (X+M)/Y$ | Exports $X$, Imports $M$ | $0 < \text{TO} < \infty$ | Quantifies trade integration |
| 5 | **Pollution Haven Index** $\text{PHI}_i = P_i / P_{\text{world}}$ | Country pollution $P_i$ | $0 < \text{PHI} \leq 1$ | Detects haven formation |
| 6 | **Carbon Leakage Rate** $\text{CLR} = \Delta E_{\text{foreign}} / \Delta E_{\text{domestic}}$ | Emission changes | $0 \leq \text{CLR} \leq 1$ | Measures policy effectiveness |
| 7 | **Carbon Intensity of Trade** $\text{CIT} = E_{\text{export}} / V_{\text{export}}$ | Embodied emissions | $\text{CIT} > 0$ | Tracks embodied pollution in trade |
| 8 | **Porter Innovation** $I = \phi_0 + \phi_1 R + \phi_2 R^2$ | Regulation $R$ | $\phi_2 > 0$ | Justifies smart regulation |
| 9 | **Comparative Advantage w/ Pollution** $\text{RCA}_j = (X_{ij}/X_i) / (X_{wj}/X_w)$ | Revealed comp. adv. | $\text{RCA} > 1$ | Identifies dirty export sectors |
| 10 | **Ecological Footprint** $\text{EF} = \text{BC} + \text{EF}_{\text{imports}} - \text{EF}_{\text{exports}}$ | Biocapacity BC | EF vs BC | Tracks sustainability of trade |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked in Part A about "channels through which trade affects environment," always list all three: **Scale**, **Composition**, and **Technique** — even a 3-mark answer expects this triplet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Scale-Composition-Technique Decomposition (SCT)

We start from a national accounting identity. Let:
- $Z$ = total pollution emissions (tonnes)
- $Y$ = GDP (USD)
- $S$ = scale variable = population $L$ (number of people)
- $e = Z/Y$ = emission intensity per dollar of output
- $y = Y/L$ = income per capita

We seek an exact multiplicative decomposition that lets us attribute changes in $Z$ to three channels.

### Step 1: Express $Z$ in terms of scale and intensity

By definition, emission intensity is:

$$e = \frac{Z}{Y}$$

### Step 2: Multiply and divide by scale $S$

We insert $S$ into the identity without changing its value:

$$Z = \frac{Z}{Y} \times \frac{Y}{S} \times S$$

### Step 3: Substitute notation

Recognize that $\frac{Y}{S} = y$ (per capita income). So:

$$Z = e \times y \times S$$

### Step 4: Take natural logs for additive decomposition

For analytical tractability in growth-accounting style analysis:

$$\ln Z = \ln e + \ln y + \ln S$$

### Step 5: Take time-differences for dynamic decomposition

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$\frac{\dot{Z}}{Z} = \frac{\dot{e}}{e} + \frac{\dot{y}}{y} + \frac{\dot{S}}{S}$$

Where $\dot{x}$ denotes time derivative of variable $x$. This means:

> **Scale Effect** = growth rate of $S$ (population/production scale)
> **Composition Effect** = growth rate of $y$ (income per capita, alters output mix)
> **Technique Effect** = growth rate of $e$ (emission intensity per unit output)

### Step 6: Interpret for trade

Trade liberalization operates as follows:
- **↑ Scale Effect** — Trade expands markets, increasing production volume → raises $Z$
- **Composition Effect** — Country specializes in its comparative-advantage sector (dirty or clean)
- **Technique Effect** — Access to imported cleaner technology → lowers $e$

Hence the **net effect on environment** depends on which channel dominates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Environmental Kuznets Curve Turning Point

We begin from the standard quadratic specification:

$$Z_i = \alpha + \beta_1 Y_i + \beta_2 Y_i^2 + \varepsilon_i$$

### Step 1: Recognize shape

A quadratic in $Y$ with $\beta_2 < 0$ opens downward (inverted-U).

### Step 2: Find first-order condition for maximum

Differentiate $Z$ with respect to $Y$ and set equal to zero:

$$\frac{dZ}{dY} = \beta_1 + 2\beta_2 Y = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $Y^{*}$

Rearranging:

$$2\beta_2 Y^{*} = -\beta_1$$

$$Y^{*} = -\frac{\beta_1}{2\beta_2}$$

### Step 4: Verify it is a maximum (not minimum)

Second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2 Z}{dY^2} = 2\beta_2$$

For inverted-U (maximum), we require $2\beta_2 < 0$, i.e., $\beta_2 < 0$. Combined with $\beta_1 > 0$, the curve rises then falls.

### Step 5: Numerical Example

Suppose empirical estimates yield $\beta_1 = 0.45$ and $\beta_2 = -0.000003$ for SO₂ emissions in a study. Then:

$$Y^{*} = -\frac{0.45}{2 \times (-0.000003)} = -\frac{0.45}{-0.000006} = 75000$$

So the country reaches its pollution peak at per capita income of **USD 75,000** — relevant for high-income economies.

### Step 6: Numerical Example (Alternative)

For CO₂ emissions, $\beta_1 = 1.2$, $\beta_2 = -0.000005$:

$$Y^{*} = -\frac{1.2}{-0.00001} = 120000$$

Peak emissions at **USD 120,000 per capita** — explaining why CO₂ (a stock pollutant) has a higher turning point than SO₂ (a local pollutant).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Carbon Leakage Rate

Suppose country A imposes a carbon tax that reduces its domestic emissions by $\Delta E_{\text{domestic}}$. Some production relocates to country B, raising its emissions by $\Delta E_{\text{foreign}}$.

### Step 1: Define leakage

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{foreign}}}{\vert \Delta E_{\text{domestic}} \vert}$$

### Step 2: Range

- $\text{CLR} = 0$ → No leakage (fully effective policy)
- $\text{CLR} = 1$ → Complete leakage (policy useless globally)
- $\text{CLR} > 1$ → Super-leakage (paradoxical global emissions rise)

### Step 3: Empirical estimate example

EU ETS evaluation: $\Delta E_{\text{domestic}} = -200$ MtCO₂, $\Delta E_{\text{foreign}} = 40$ MtCO₂.

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{40}{\vert -200 \vert} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20$$

So **20% leakage rate** — meaning 80% of the EU's domestic cuts translated into real global reductions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hypotheses Comparison

| Dimension | **Pollution Haven Hypothesis** | **Porter Hypothesis** | **EKC Hypothesis** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Trade harms environment | Smart regulation helps both | Trade-environment follows inverted-U |
| **Mechanism** | Relocation to lax countries | Innovation offsets compliance costs | Income-driven preferences & tech |
| **Time horizon** | Short to medium run | Long run | Long run |
| **Empirical support** | Mixed; weak for OECD | Strong for flexible policies | Strong for local pollutants (SO₂); weak for CO₂ |
| **Policy implication** | Harmonize standards upward | Use market-based instruments | Grow first, clean later (controversial) |
| **Key criticism** | Ignores technology transfer | Requires well-designed rules | "Grow now, clean later" is ethically dubious |
| **KTU relevance** | High (CO5 sustainability) | High (CO6 innovation) | High (CO5 environment) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the Pollution Haven Hypothesis. Mention two pieces of empirical evidence.**

**Model Answer:**
The Pollution Haven Hypothesis (PHH) states that countries with stringent environmental regulations lose competitiveness in pollution-intensive industries, which relocate to countries with lax rules — turning the latter into "pollution havens."

**Empirical evidence:**
1. Walter and Ugelow (1979) found weak support — pollution-intensive FDI is only modestly affected by regulation stringency.
2. Mani and Wheeler (1998) found that dirty industries in OECD countries declined while dirty output share rose in developing nations during 1965–1995.

**Marking note:** Definition = 2 marks, one evidence = 1 mark. Maximum credit for any one correctly cited study.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: List the three channels through which international trade affects the environment, as per the ABCT framework.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Scale Effect** — Trade expands the volume of production and consumption, increasing aggregate resource use and emissions.
2. **Composition Effect** — Trade shifts the national output mix toward the country's comparative-advantage sector (which may be clean or dirty).
3. **Technique Effect** — Trade facilitates transfer and adoption of cleaner technologies, reducing emissions per unit of output.

**Marking note:** Naming each channel = 1 mark each. Total 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts. Sub-part (a) typically carries **7 marks** and sub-part (b) carries **7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) hypothesis in detail. Derive the turning point income and discuss its limitations as a policy guide.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The EKC is the empirical hypothesis that environmental degradation first increases with economic growth, reaches a peak, then declines — forming an inverted-U relationship between per capita income $Y$ and pollution $Z$.

**Step 2 — Equation (2 marks):**

$$Z = \alpha + \beta_1 Y + \beta_2 Y^2 + \varepsilon$$

Conditions: $\beta_1 > 0$, $\beta_2 < 0$.

**Step 3 — Turning Point Derivation (2 marks):**

$$Y^{*} = -\frac{\beta_1}{2\beta_2}$$

**Step 4 — Limitations (1 mark):**
- Valid mainly for local pollutants (SO₂); not for CO₂ or biodiversity loss
- "Grow now, clean later" raises intergenerational equity concerns
- Turning point is empirically unstable across countries
- Ignores distributional and ecological threshold effects

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Compare and contrast the Pollution Haven Hypothesis (PHH) and the Porter Hypothesis with suitable diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — PHH Definition (1.5 marks):**
The PHH argues that stringent environmental regulation in developed countries raises compliance costs, pushing pollution-intensive industries to relocate to developing countries with lax rules. Result: pollution havens form.

**Step 2 — Porter Hypothesis Definition (1.5 marks):**
The Porter Hypothesis argues that well-designed, market-based environmental regulation stimulates innovation, offsets compliance costs, and enhances long-run competitiveness.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | PHH | Porter |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | Short run | Long run |
| Regulation effect | Competitiveness loss | Competitiveness gain |
| Direction of FDI | Southward flow | Green-tech investment |
| Empirical evidence | Mixed | Strong for flexible instruments |

**Step 4 — Diagram (2 marks):**
Sketch the SCT decomposition flow showing how Porter's innovation offsets the scale effect, while PHH channels dirty FDI southward.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Using the Scale-Composition-Technique (SCT) framework, analyze how trade liberalization impacts the environment. Show all derivation steps.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Let $Z$ = total emissions, $Y$ = GDP, $S$ = scale, $e = Z/Y$ = emission intensity.

**Step 2 — Identity (1 mark):**

$$Z = \frac{Z}{Y} \times \frac{Y}{S} \times S = e \times y \times S$$

**Step 3 — Log-linearization (1.5 marks):**

$$\ln Z = \ln e + \ln y + \ln S$$

**Step 4 — Time-derivative decomposition (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{\dot{Z}}{Z} = \frac{\dot{e}}{e} + \frac{\dot{y}}{y} + \frac{\dot{S}}{S}$$

**Step 5 — Channel Analysis (2 marks):**
- **Scale Effect** $\dot{S}/S > 0$: Trade expands output → more pollution.
- **Composition Effect** $\dot{y}/y$ may be positive or negative depending on specialization.
- **Technique Effect** $\dot{e}/e < 0$: Imported cleaner tech lowers intensity.

**Step 6 — Net conclusion (1 mark):**
Net environmental outcome is ambiguous; depends on which channel dominates.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: What is carbon leakage? Compute the carbon leakage rate for the following scenario: A country reduces domestic emissions by 150 MtCO₂ through a carbon tax, but foreign emissions in trading partners rise by 30 MtCO₂ due to production relocation. Discuss two policy remedies.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (1.5 marks):**
Carbon leakage is the increase in emissions in foreign jurisdictions that results from domestic climate policies causing production relocation or consumption shifts.

**Step 2 — Formula (1 mark):**

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{foreign}}}{\vert \Delta E_{\text{domestic}} \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Computation (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{30}{\vert -150 \vert} = \frac{30}{150} = 0.20$$

So **CLR = 20%**.

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
For every 1 tonne of domestic emission reduction, 0.20 tonnes leak abroad. Net global reduction = 150 − 30 = **120 MtCO₂**.

**Step 5 — Policy Remedies (2 marks):**
1. **Border Carbon Adjustments (BCAs)** — Levy carbon-equivalent tariffs on imports from lax jurisdictions (e.g., EU CBAM).
2. **International coordination** — Link carbon markets or unify carbon prices via Article 6 of the Paris Agreement.
3. *(Optional 3rd)*: Output-based rebates for trade-exposed, energy-intensive domestic industries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Part B (Trade & Environment):**
> 1. **Forgetting all three SCT channels** — Listing only "scale" and "technique" but missing "composition" costs you 1–2 marks.
> 2. **No derivation shown for EKC turning point** — Examiners want the explicit calculation $Y^{*} = -\beta_1/(2\beta_2)$, not just the final number.
> 3. **Confusing PHH with Porter Hypothesis** — These are *opposite* hypotheses; mixing them up is a fatal conceptual error.
> 4. **Not interpreting CLR result** — Computing $0.20$ is incomplete; you must state net global emission reduction in absolute tonnes.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — Always state "MtCO₂", "USD per capita", "global hectares" etc.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams** — KTU examiners award up to 2 marks per sub-part for a clear, labeled diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Three SCT Channels** = Scale + Composition + Technique (memorize the triplet)
- ✅ **EKC turning point** = $Y^{*} = -\beta_1 / (2\beta_2)$, inverted-U shape
- ✅ **EKC holds for local pollutants** (SO₂) but **weak for global pollutants** (CO₂)
- ✅ **Pollution Haven Hypothesis** → Dirty industries relocate to lax countries
- ✅ **Porter Hypothesis** → Smart regulation can enhance competitiveness via innovation
- ✅ **Carbon Leakage Rate** = $\Delta E_{\text{foreign}} / \vert \Delta E_{\text{domestic}} \vert$, range 0 to >1
- ✅ **Trade openness ratio** = $(X + M) / Y$
- ✅ **Ecological Footprint** compares biocapacity use to biocapacity available
- ✅ **WTO + MEAs** must coexist — GATT Article XX allows environmental exceptions
- ✅ **Carbon Border Adjustments (CBAs / CBAMs)** are emerging policy tools
- ✅ **Free trade vs environment** is not a zero-sum game — depends on policy design
- ✅ **NEP 2020 / KTU CO5** — Engineers must analyze trade-environment linkages in projects

### Final Mnemonic for Quick Recall

> **"SCT-EKC-PHH-PORTER-CLR-CIT"**
> **S**cale, **C**omposition, **T**echnique → **E**KC → **P**ollution **H**aven **H**ypothesis ↔ **P**orter **H**ypothesis → **C**arbon **L**eakage **R**ate → **C**arbon **I**ntensity of **T**rade.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Income Effects and Environmental Kuznets Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT861 - ENVIRONMENTAL ECONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Income Effects and Environmental Kuznets Curves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)** is an empirical hypothesis proposing an **inverted-U-shaped relationship** between per capita income (a proxy for economic development) and environmental degradation. The hypothesis states that as a country's income per capita rises in its early stages of development, environmental degradation first **increases**, reaches a **maximum turning point**, and then **declines** as income continues to grow.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT861 - Module 4):**
> The Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC) is a hypothesized relationship between various indicators of environmental degradation and per capita income, suggesting that environmental degradation first rises and then falls as income increases, producing an inverted U-shape. The hypothesis was originally proposed by **Grossman and Krueger (1991)** in the context of NAFTA, drawing structural analogy from **Simon Kuznets's (1955)** work on income inequality and economic growth.

The general functional form is:

$$E_t = f(Y_t)$$

where $E_t$ represents the environmental degradation index or pollutant emissions per capita at time $t$, and $Y_t$ is the real per capita Gross Domestic Product (GDP).

### 1.2 Key Metrics and Indicators

| Metric | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Income | $Y_t$ | Real GDP per person, usually in USD |
| Environmental Degradation | $E_t$ | Pollutant concentration, emissions, or composite index |
| Turning Point Income | $Y^{*}$ | Income level at which $E_t$ reaches its maximum |
| Turning Point Elasticity | $\epsilon$ | Percentage change in $E_t$ per 1% change in $Y_t$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Magic Number:** Empirically, the EKC turning point for many **local pollutants** (e.g., SO₂, suspended particulate matter, NOₓ) typically occurs between **USD 8,000 and USD 12,000 per capita** (constant 2010 USD PPP terms). For **global pollutants** (e.g., CO₂), empirical evidence of a robust turning point is weak or absent.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Growing Teenager" Story

Imagine a child growing up in a small, clean village. As the family income rises:

- **Childhood (low income):** The child lives in a pristine environment, plays in clean streams, breathes pure air. The village has little industry, so pollution is minimal.
- **Early growth (rising income):** The family starts a small business, buys a motorbike, and the village becomes a small town. A factory opens, traffic increases, and waste begins to accumulate. The environment is dirtier than before — even though the family is wealthier!
- **Maturation (higher income):** The family becomes rich. They move to a planned city with strict regulations, install wastewater treatment, switch to electric vehicles, plant trees, and demand parks. The environment begins to recover.
- **Adulthood (very high income):** The city is now cleaner than the original village, even though the economy is 50 times larger.

> This is the **inverted U-shape** of the EKC: pollution rises with growth, peaks, and then falls.

### 1.4 Three Structural Channels (The "SCT" Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Every EKC explanation MUST discuss the three countervailing effects of growth on the environment.

1. **Scale Effect (Negative):** Larger economic scale means more resource extraction and waste generation. This *increases* degradation.
2. **Composition Effect (Mixed):** As economies develop, the relative size of polluting sectors (agriculture → heavy industry → services) shifts. Initially, industrialization worsens pollution; later, the service sector dominates and is cleaner.
3. **Technique Effect (Positive):** Wealthier societies adopt cleaner technology, better pollution-control equipment, and more efficient production methods. This *decreases* degradation per unit of output.

The EKC shape emerges because the **scale effect dominates at low income**, while the **composition and technique effects dominate at higher income**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reduced-Form EKC Equation

The most commonly estimated empirical specification is a **quadratic polynomial** in log-log form:

$$\ln(E_t) = \alpha + \beta \ln(Y_t) + \gamma \left[\ln(Y_t)\right]^2 + \varepsilon_t$$

**Conditions for an Inverted U:**
- $\beta > 0$ (initial positive slope)
- $\gamma < 0$ (eventual negative slope)
- Both must be statistically significant

**Derivation of the Turning Point (Income at Maximum Degradation):**

$$\frac{\partial \ln(E_t)}{\partial \ln(Y_t)} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_t) = 0$$

Solving for $Y_t$:

$$\ln(Y^{*}) = -\frac{\beta}{2\gamma}$$

$$\boxed{Y^{*} = \exp\left(-\frac{\beta}{2\gamma}\right)}$$

**Elasticity of Environmental Degradation with Respect to Income:**

$$\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \frac{\partial \ln(E_t)}{\partial \ln(Y_t)} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_t)$$

> When $\varepsilon_{E,Y} > 0$: degradation is increasing with income (left of turning point).
> When $\varepsilon_{E,Y} = 0$: at the turning point.
> When $\varepsilon_{E,Y} < 0$: degradation is decreasing with income (right of turning point).

### 2.2 The Three Effects — Detailed Mathematical Treatment

Let $P$ denote total pollution, $Y$ income, $S$ economic scale, $C$ sectoral composition, and $T$ technology.

$$P = S \times C \times T$$

Taking logarithmic differentiation:

$$\frac{\dot{P}}{P} = \frac{\dot{S}}{S} + \frac{\dot{C}}{C} + \frac{\dot{T}}{T}$$

| Effect | Mathematical Sign | Economic Intuition | EKC Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scale Effect** | $\frac{\dot{S}}{S} > 0$ | More output → more emissions | Dominant (left of $Y^{*}$) |
| **Composition Effect** | $\frac{\dot{C}}{C} \lessgtr 0$ | Shift from primary → secondary → tertiary | U-shaped itself |
| **Technique Effect** | $\frac{\dot{T}}{T} < 0$ | Cleaner technology reduces emission intensity | Dominant (right of $Y^{*}$) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Quadratic EKC | $E = \alpha + \beta Y + \gamma Y^2$ | Reduced-form empirical model |
| Log-Log EKC | $\ln E = \alpha + \beta \ln Y + \gamma (\ln Y)^2$ | Constant-elasticity form (preferred) |
| Inverted U Condition | $\beta > 0,\ \gamma < 0$ | Required for U-shape |
| Turning Point Income | $Y^{*} = -\beta / (2\gamma)$ | Vertex of the parabola |
| Turning Point (log-log) | $\ln Y^{*} = -\beta / (2\gamma)$ | Vertex in elasticity form |
| Marginal Effect | $\frac{dE}{dY} = \beta + 2\gamma Y$ | Slope at any income level |
| Income Elasticity | $\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln Y$ | % change in $E$ per 1% change in $Y$ |
| Maximum Degradation | $E_{\max} = \alpha - \beta^2 / (4\gamma)$ | Peak pollution level |
| Concavity Check | $d^2 E / dY^2 = 2\gamma < 0$ | Confirms maximum (not minimum) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Tip (KTU 2024):** Examiners frequently award partial credit if you correctly write the **inverted U conditions** ($\beta > 0$, $\gamma < 0$) and the **turning point formula** ($Y^{*} = -\beta / 2\gamma$), even if the rest of the derivation is incomplete. Memorize these two items.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

1. **Policy Threshold Setting:** Governments use estimated $Y^{*}$ values to set deadlines for environmental regulations aligned with projected income growth.
2. **International Trade:** The pollution-haven hypothesis suggests dirty industries migrate to low-income countries without EKC turning points.
3. **Decoupling Indicators:** The EKC is a benchmark for testing whether absolute decoupling of growth from environmental pressure is empirically possible.
4. **SDG Monitoring:** SDG Indicator 17.14.1 (Policy Coherence for Sustainable Development) uses EKC-type modeling to track sustainability progress.

### 2.5 Theoretical Underpinnings — Why Should an Inverted U Exist?

Four microeconomic mechanisms drive the EKC:

1. **Preference Shifts:** As income rises, marginal utility of consumption falls, while marginal utility of environmental quality rises. Environmental quality behaves like a **luxury good** with income elasticity greater than 1.
2. **Structural Transformation:** Share of services (low-emission intensity) rises with income, share of manufacturing (high-emission intensity) first rises then falls.
3. **Regulatory Demand:** Becker-style political economy models show that pollution-control expenditure rises more than proportionally with income, as the median voter demands cleaner environment.
4. **International Spillovers:** Richer countries transfer cleaner technologies to poorer ones through FDI, trade, and aid.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Turning Point of the Quadratic EKC

**Starting Equation:**

$$E_t = \alpha + \beta Y_t + \gamma Y_t^2 + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 1 — Take the first derivative of $E_t$ with respect to $Y_t$:**

$$\frac{dE_t}{dY_t} = \beta + 2\gamma Y_t$$

This derivative represents the **slope** of the EKC at any income level $Y_t$. It measures how much environmental degradation changes when per capita income increases by one unit.

**Step 2 — Set the first-order condition (FOC) for a maximum equal to zero:**

The turning point is a maximum, so the slope of the curve at that point must equal zero. This is the standard calculus condition for an extremum.

$$\frac{dE_t}{dY_t} = 0$$

$$\beta + 2\gamma Y^{*} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for the turning-point income $Y^{*}$:**

We isolate $Y^{*}$ on the left-hand side by moving $\beta$ to the right and then dividing by $2\gamma$:

$$2\gamma Y^{*} = -\beta$$

$$\boxed{Y^{*} = -\frac{\beta}{2\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the second-order condition (SOC) to confirm a maximum (not a minimum):**

The second derivative of the EKC with respect to $Y_t$ is a constant equal to $2\gamma$:

$$\frac{d^2 E_t}{dY_t^2} = 2\gamma$$

For the turning point to be a **maximum** (inverted U), this second derivative must be **negative**:

$$2\gamma < 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \gamma < 0$$

Combined with the requirement that the initial slope be positive ($\beta > 0$), the full condition for a valid inverted-U EKC is:

$$\beta > 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \gamma < 0$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum degradation level $E_{\max}$:**

Substitute $Y^{*} = -\beta / (2\gamma)$ back into the original EKC equation:

$$E_{\max} = \alpha + \beta Y^{*} + \gamma (Y^{*})^2$$

$$E_{\max} = \alpha + \beta \left(-\frac{\beta}{2\gamma}\right) + \gamma \left(-\frac{\beta}{2\gamma}\right)^2$$

$$E_{\max} = \alpha - \frac{\beta^2}{2\gamma} + \gamma \cdot \frac{\beta^2}{4\gamma^2}$$

$$E_{\max} = \alpha - \frac{\beta^2}{2\gamma} + \frac{\beta^2}{4\gamma}$$

$$E_{\max} = \alpha - \frac{2\beta^2}{4\gamma} + \frac{\beta^2}{4\gamma}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\max} = \alpha - \frac{\beta^2}{4\gamma}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Income Elasticity in the Log-Log Form

**Starting Equation:**

$$\ln(E_t) = \alpha + \beta \ln(Y_t) + \gamma \left[\ln(Y_t)\right]^2 + \varepsilon_t$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $\ln(Y_t)$:**

$$\frac{\partial \ln(E_t)}{\partial \ln(Y_t)} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_t)$$

**Step 2 — Interpret the derivative as an elasticity:**

In a log-log model, the derivative of $\ln(E)$ with respect to $\ln(Y)$ is precisely the **point elasticity of environmental degradation with respect to income**:

$$\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_t)$$

**Step 3 — Find the income level at which the elasticity equals unity (unitary elasticity):**

$$\beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_u) = 1$$

$$\ln(Y_u) = \frac{1 - \beta}{2\gamma}$$

$$Y_u = \exp\left(\frac{1 - \beta}{2\gamma}\right)$$

> At $Y < Y_u$, environmental degradation rises faster than income (inelastic with respect to degradation response).
> At $Y > Y_u$, environmental degradation rises slower than income or actually falls.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A country estimates the following EKC for SO₂ emissions per capita (kg/person):

$$\ln(E_t) = 4.20 + 1.85 \ln(Y_t) - 0.32 \left[\ln(Y_t)\right]^2$$

Income $Y_t$ is in thousands of USD per capita.

**Task:** Find the turning point income, the income elasticity at $Y_t = 15$, and determine whether degradation is increasing or decreasing at that level.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the coefficients:**

$$\alpha = 4.20,\quad \beta = 1.85,\quad \gamma = -0.32$$

**Step 2 — Check inverted U conditions:**

$\beta = 1.85 > 0$ ✓
$\gamma = -0.32 < 0$ ✓

Both conditions satisfied → inverted U exists.

**Step 3 — Compute the turning point income:**

$$\ln(Y^{*}) = -\frac{\beta}{2\gamma} = -\frac{1.85}{2 \times (-0.32)} = -\frac{1.85}{-0.64} = 2.8906$$

$$Y^{*} = e^{2.8906} = 18.01 \text{ thousand USD per capita}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the elasticity at $Y_t = 15$ thousand USD:**

First, $\ln(15) = 2.7081$.

$$\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_t) = 1.85 + 2(-0.32)(2.7081)$$

$$= 1.85 - 0.64 \times 2.7081$$

$$= 1.85 - 1.7332 = 0.1168$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the elasticity:**

Since $\varepsilon_{E,Y} = 0.1168 > 0$, environmental degradation is **still increasing** with income at $Y_t = 15$, but only by about 0.12% for every 1% rise in income. The country is approaching the turning point but has not yet reached it.

**Step 6 — Compute the maximum SO₂ level (for reference):**

$$E_{\max} = \exp\left(\alpha - \frac{\beta^2}{4\gamma}\right) = \exp\left(4.20 - \frac{(1.85)^2}{4 \times (-0.32)}\right)$$

$$= \exp\left(4.20 - \frac{3.4225}{-1.28}\right) = \exp(4.20 + 2.6738) = \exp(6.8738)$$

$$E_{\max} \approx 967.5 \text{ kg per capita (peak SO}_2 \text{ at } Y^{*} = 18{,}010 \text{ USD)}$$

### 3.4 The Three-Effect Decomposition Identity (Analytical Matrix)

| Income Range ($Y_t$) | Scale Effect | Composition Effect | Technique Effect | Net Effect on $E_t$ | EKC Phase |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Y_t < Y_1$ (very low) | Strong (+) | Weak (+) | Negligible | Positive | Rising steeply |
| $Y_1 \le Y_t < Y^{*}$ | Strong (+) | Moderate (+) | Weak (−) | Positive | Still rising, slope decreasing |
| $Y_t = Y^{*}$ | Moderate (+) | Neutral (0) | Moderate (−) | **Zero** | **Maximum (turning point)** |
| $Y^{*} < Y_t \le Y_2$ | Moderate (+) | Weak (−) | Strong (−) | Negative | Declining |
| $Y_t > Y_2$ (very high) | Weak (+) | Strong (−) | Very strong (−) | Strong negative | Well below initial levels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The technique effect is essentially zero in low-income countries because (a) capital is scarce and devoted to production, not abatement, and (b) the most polluting technologies are exported to them under the **pollution-haven hypothesis** (Walter & Ugelow, 1979).

### 3.5 Robustness Checks and Critiques

| Test | Question Addressed | Standard Method |
|---|---|---|
| Functional Form | Is the inverted U robust to model specification? | Add cubic term $\delta [\ln Y]^3$ |
| Endogeneity | Is income truly exogenous? | Use lagged income as IV |
| Heterogeneity | Do all countries share one EKC? | Panel fixed effects, random coefficients |
| Threshold Effects | Is there a structural break? | Bai-Perron break-point test |
| Spillovers | Does country $i$ affect country $j$'s EKC? | Spatial econometrics |
| Stationarity | Are variables $I(0)$ or $I(1)$? | Panel unit root tests (Im-Pesaran-Shin) |
| Cointegration | Is there a long-run EKC relationship? | Pedroni, Westerlund cointegration tests |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** A common student mistake is to interpret the coefficient on $\ln(Y)$ as the elasticity in a quadratic log-log model. It is NOT. The correct elasticity at any point is $\beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y)$, which depends on $Y$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**"What is meant by the Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)? State its key empirical findings."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Environmental Kuznets Curve (EKC)** is an inverted-U-shaped relationship between per capita income and environmental degradation. It hypothesizes that as a country develops economically, environmental degradation first **increases** up to a turning point income (typically between USD 8,000–12,000 per capita), and then **decreases** as income continues to rise. This is attributed to the relative strength of three effects: the **scale effect** (which always raises pollution with growth), the **composition effect** (the structural shift from agriculture to industry to services), and the **technique effect** (cleaner technology adoption at higher incomes). Empirically, local pollutants like SO₂, NOₓ, and suspended particulates show robust inverted U-shapes in cross-country panels, while global pollutants like CO₂ do not exhibit a clear turning point. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for three effects, 1 for empirical evidence.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Distinguish between the scale effect, composition effect, and technique effect in the EKC framework."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Effect | Direction | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale Effect** | Negative for environment | Larger economic output → more resource use and waste |
| **Composition Effect** | Mixed (U-shaped) | Shift from primary sector (low pollution) → industry (high pollution) → services (low pollution) |
| **Technique Effect** | Positive for environment | Richer economies adopt cleaner, more efficient technologies |

The interaction of these three effects produces the **inverted U-shape** of the EKC. At low incomes, the scale effect dominates. At higher incomes, the technique and composition effects overcome the scale effect, leading to declining degradation. *(3 marks: 1 per effect.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

**INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **theoretical basis** of the Environmental Kuznets Curve. Discuss the **role of income, technology, and structural change** in shaping the EKC. Use diagrams to support your answer.

**(b)** [7 marks] A country estimates the following EKC model for forest cover loss (in hectares per 1000 people):

$$\ln(E_t) = 2.40 + 0.95 \ln(Y_t) - 0.18 [\ln(Y_t)]^2$$

where $Y_t$ is GDP per capita in thousand USD. Determine: **(i)** whether the EKC has a valid inverted-U shape, **(ii)** the turning-point income $Y^{*}$, and **(iii)** the income elasticity of degradation when $Y_t = 8$ thousand USD.

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Basis of the EKC (7 marks)

**1. The Kuznets Origin (1 mark):**

Simon Kuznets (1955) proposed an inverted U-relationship between per capita income and income inequality. Grossman and Krueger (1991) and Panayotou (1993) empirically adapted this idea to environmental degradation, producing the **Environmental Kuznets Curve**.

**2. The Three Countervailing Effects (3 marks):**

The EKC arises from the net interaction of three effects of income growth on environmental pressure:

- **Scale Effect:** As output expands, resource extraction and waste generation rise, increasing pollution. Mathematically, $\partial P / \partial S > 0$, where $S$ is economic scale.
- **Composition Effect:** As an economy grows, its sectoral composition shifts: agriculture → manufacturing → services. Since services are less pollution-intensive than manufacturing, this shift eventually *reduces* pollution intensity.
- **Technique Effect:** At higher incomes, societies invest in cleaner production technologies, end-of-pipe abatement, and pollution-control regulations. The pollution emitted per unit of GDP (emission intensity) falls.

**3. Income Elasticity of Environmental Quality Demand (1 mark):**

At low incomes, the marginal utility of consumption exceeds that of environmental quality, so growth damages the environment. At high incomes, environmental quality becomes a **luxury good** with income elasticity greater than unity, so citizens demand stricter regulation and cleaner goods.

**4. Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   E
   |     . . .
   |   .     .  
   |  .       .       <-- Inverted-U EKC
   | .         . .
   |.           .  .
   |_____________.____ Y
                Y*
```

The diagram shows environmental degradation $E$ rising with income $Y$ until turning point $Y^{*}$, beyond which $E$ falls.

---

#### Part (b) — Quantitative Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**

$$\ln(E_t) = 2.40 + 0.95 \ln(Y_t) - 0.18 [\ln(Y_t)]^2$$

**Coefficients:** $\alpha = 2.40$, $\beta = 0.95$, $\gamma = -0.18$.

**Part (i) — Inverted U Validity (2 marks):**

- $\beta = 0.95 > 0$ ✓ (initial positive slope)
- $\gamma = -0.18 < 0$ ✓ (eventual negative slope)

Both conditions are satisfied → **A valid inverted-U EKC exists.** The country will experience rising forest loss at low income, peak loss at the turning point, and declining loss at high income.

**Part (ii) — Turning Point Income $Y^{*}$ (3 marks):**

$$\ln(Y^{*}) = -\frac{\beta}{2\gamma} = -\frac{0.95}{2 \times (-0.18)} = -\frac{0.95}{-0.36} = 2.6389$$

$$Y^{*} = e^{2.6389} \approx 13.99 \text{ thousand USD per capita}$$

$$\boxed{Y^{*} \approx \text{USD } 13{,}990 \text{ per capita}}$$

**Part (iii) — Income Elasticity at $Y_t = 8$ (2 marks):**

$$\ln(8) = 2.0794$$

$$\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y_t) = 0.95 + 2(-0.18)(2.0794)$$

$$= 0.95 - 0.36 \times 2.0794$$

$$= 0.95 - 0.7486 = 0.2014$$

Since $\varepsilon_{E,Y} = 0.2014 > 0$, at $Y_t = 8$ thousand USD per capita, **forest cover loss is still increasing** with income, but only mildly (a 1% increase in income causes only a 0.20% rise in forest loss). The country is on the left side of the turning point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Discuss the **empirical evidence** for and **criticisms** against the Environmental Kuznets Curve. How does the EKC differ for local versus global pollutants?

**(b)** [7 marks] Using the EKC framework, explain how a developing country like **India** can leapfrog to a "**green growth**" trajectory. What policy instruments are necessary to bypass the high-pollution phase typically observed in industrializing economies?

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Empirical Evidence and Criticisms (7 marks)

**1. Empirical Evidence Supporting EKC (3 marks):**

- **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂):** Grossman & Krueger (1991) and Selden & Song (1994) found robust inverted-U patterns, with turning points around USD 8,000–10,000 per capita.
- **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀):** Studies by Dasgupta, Laplante, & Wheeler (2001) and World Bank confirm inverted U-shape.
- **Deforestation:** Cropper & Griffiths (1994) and Bhattarai & Hammig (2001) found a Kuznetsian pattern for tropical forest cover loss.
- **Heavy Metals and Water Pollutants:** Mixed evidence; some pollutants (e.g., fecal coliform) show the inverted U, while others (e.g., heavy metal discharge) do not.

**2. Criticisms of the EKC (3 marks):**

- **Local vs. Global Pollutants:** CO₂, the most pressing global pollutant, shows no robust turning point. Global emissions continue to rise with world GDP.
- **Ecological Thresholds:** The EKC assumes smooth recovery, but ecosystems may suffer irreversible damage before the turning point (e.g., coral reef collapse, glacial melt).
- **Pollution Haven Effect:** Dirty industries migrate from rich to poor countries, suppressing the latter's turning point and masking the rich countries' true pollution footprint (consumption-based accounting).
- **Rebound Effect (Jevons Paradox):** Efficiency gains in production may be offset by increased consumption, keeping total pollution high.
- **Income Elasticity Variation:** Environmental preferences vary widely across societies, so a single EKC may not be universally applicable.
- **Aggregation Bias:** Composite indices may mask divergent trends across individual pollutants.

**3. Local vs. Global Pollutants — Distinction (1 mark):**

| Pollutant Type | Examples | Dispersal | EKC Valid? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Local | SO₂, NOₓ, PM₁₀, fecal coliform | Within ~50 km of source | Yes, strong |
| Regional | Acid rain precursors, smog | 100–1000 km | Yes, moderate |
| Global | CO₂, CFCs, methane | Worldwide | No, weak or absent |

---

#### Part (b) — Leapfrogging to Green Growth (7 marks)

**1. The Concept of Leapfrogging (2 marks):**

A developing country can **bypass the high-pollution phase** of the EKC by adopting modern, clean technologies directly, skipping the dirty intermediate technologies that today's rich countries used during their industrialization. For example, India can go straight to **renewable energy, electric mobility, and digital services** without ever building a coal-heavy industrial base.

**2. Conditions for Successful Leapfrogging (2 marks):**

- **Access to Finance:** Green Climate Fund, multilateral lending, and carbon markets must be available.
- **Technology Transfer:** FDI and international R&D collaboration should bring clean technologies to the developing country.
- **Institutional Capacity:** Strong environmental governance is required to enforce standards.
- **Human Capital:** Skilled workforce capable of operating and maintaining clean technology.

**3. Policy Instruments (3 marks):**

- **Carbon Pricing:** A Pigouvian carbon tax or an Emissions Trading System (ETS) internalizes the externality.
- **Renewable Portfolio Standards (RPS):** Mandate a minimum share of renewable electricity.
- **Subsidies for Clean Tech:** Production Tax Credits (PTC), Investment Tax Credits (ITC) for solar, wind, and EVs.
- **Removal of Fossil Fuel Subsidies:** Phased elimination of coal, diesel, and kerosene subsidies.
- **Green Public Procurement:** Government purchases prioritize low-carbon goods.
- **Stricter Emission Standards:** Bharat Stage VI norms for vehicles, tighter industrial emission caps.
- **R&D Investment:** Mission Innovation, National Hydrogen Mission, and PM-KUSUM schemes.

**Conclusion:** If India leapfrogs, its EKC will be "**flattened**" — the inverted U will be much shallower, with the turning point reached at a much lower income level and a lower peak pollution.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Turning Point:** The most common mistake is writing $Y^{*} = \beta / (2\gamma)$ instead of $Y^{*} = -\beta / (2\gamma)$. If you make this error, your turning point will have the wrong sign and you will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Slope and Elasticity:** In the log-log model, $\beta$ is NOT the elasticity. Always remember the elasticity formula $\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln(Y)$.
> 3. **Ignoring Second-Order Condition:** Stating $\beta > 0$ and $\gamma < 0$ is necessary but you should also verify $d^2 E / dY^2 < 0$ to confirm a maximum, not a minimum.
> 4. **Forgetting the Limitation of EKC for CO₂:** Examiners often ask why the EKC fails for climate change. The answer lies in the **global vs. local pollutant distinction** and the absence of a self-correcting mechanism at the planetary scale.
> 5. **No Diagram = Mark Deduction:** In Part (a) of 14-mark questions, an unlabeled or missing EKC diagram typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Income Effects & EKC**

- ✅ **Definition:** EKC = inverted-U relationship between per capita income and environmental degradation.
- ✅ **Originators:** Kuznets (1955, income inequality) → Grossman & Krueger (1991, environment) → Panayotou (1993, empirical).
- ✅ **Three Effects:** Scale (bad), Composition (mixed), Technique (good). The EKC shape depends on their relative strength.
- ✅ **Functional Form:** $E = \alpha + \beta Y + \gamma Y^2$ (quadratic) or $\ln E = \alpha + \beta \ln Y + \gamma (\ln Y)^2$ (log-log).
- ✅ **Inverted U Conditions:** $\beta > 0$ and $\gamma < 0$.
- ✅ **Turning Point Formula:** $Y^{*} = -\beta / (2\gamma)$ (quadratic) or $\ln Y^{*} = -\beta / (2\gamma)$ (log-log).
- ✅ **Income Elasticity:** $\varepsilon_{E,Y} = \beta + 2\gamma \ln Y$ in the log-log form.
- ✅ **Local Pollutants:** SO₂, NOₓ, PM₁₀ show robust EKC; turning point ~ USD 8,000–12,000.
- ✅ **Global Pollutants:** CO₂, CFCs, methane show **no robust EKC** — empirical evidence of turning point is weak or absent.
- ✅ **Criticisms:** Pollution-haven hypothesis, ecological thresholds, rebound effect, Jevons paradox, aggregation bias, time lags.
- ✅ **Policy Implication:** Leapfrogging through green technology adoption can flatten the EKC.
- ✅ **Empirical Estimation:** Requires panel data, unit-root tests, cointegration analysis, and robustness checks (cubic term, fixed effects, IV).
- ✅ **Luxury Good Hypothesis:** Environmental quality is a luxury good with income elasticity greater than unity at high income levels.
- ✅ **Becker's Political Economy:** Median voter income determines regulatory stringency, which rises with growth.
- ✅ **Sustainable Development Implication:** EKC supports the "**Environmental Transition**" thesis but does **not** guarantee environmental sustainability.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Income Effects and Environmental Kuznets Curves (PESFT861)**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Race to the Bottom (RTB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Race to the Bottom (RTB)** is a socio-economic phenomenon in which sovereign states progressively weaken their domestic environmental regulations, labor standards, or fiscal policies in a competitive, sequential manner to attract or retain multinational capital, foreign direct investment (FDI), and industrial activity, ultimately resulting in a globally suboptimal equilibrium of degraded environmental quality.

In simpler terms, imagine a group of neighboring towns each trying to attract a big factory. Town A says, "We'll let you dump waste in our river!" Town B, not wanting to lose out, says, "We'll let you dump even more!" Town C says, "We won't even charge you taxes!" Each town keeps "winning" the factory by offering weaker rules, but by the end, *everyone's* river is polluted and *nobody's* tax base is healthy. That downward spiral is the **Race to the Bottom**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from KTU Syllabus:** RTB is a *non-cooperative game* — each country's individual rational choice produces a collectively irrational (Pareto-inferior) outcome. This is a classic **Prisoner's Dilemma** applied to environmental regulation.

### Origin of the Concept
The term gained prominence during the debate over the **North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA, 1994)** when critics argued that Mexico's lower environmental standards would pressure the United States and Canada to weaken theirs to remain competitive.

---

## 1.2 Pollution Haven Hypothesis (PHH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> **Pollution Haven Hypothesis (PHH)** is the theoretical proposition that, in the absence of harmonized international environmental standards, profit-maximizing multinational corporations (MNCs) systematically relocate pollution-intensive production processes to jurisdictions (countries or regions) with the **weakest environmental regulations** and **lowest effective pollution abatement costs**, thereby transforming these jurisdictions into "pollution havens."

**Plain-English Analogy — The Trash Tourist:**
Picture a waste disposal company in Country A facing a $100 per ton disposal fee. Right across the border, Country B charges only $5 per ton. The company loads its toxic waste onto trucks and "vacations" it to Country B. Country B becomes a *pollution haven* — a magnet for dirty industry — because its lax rules act like a discounted entry ticket. **PHH** is exactly this, but for whole factories instead of garbage trucks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Distinction:**
> - **Race to the Bottom** = the **policy behavior of countries** (loosening standards).
> - **Pollution Haven** = the **firm-level response** (relocating dirty industries to lenient hosts).
> They are *intertwined causes and effects*: RTB by governments creates PHH opportunities for firms.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **EETR** | **Effective Environmental Tax Rate** — the actual pollution tax per unit of emissions net of subsidies | Standard metric to compare stringency across countries |
| **FDI** | Foreign Direct Investment | Capital flow channeled by regulatory arbitrage |
| **Carbon Leakage** | Increase in emissions in one region caused by reduction in another | Direct empirical consequence of PHH |
| **Comparative Abatement Cost (CAC)** | Cost to a firm of reducing one unit of pollution | Lower CAC in havens drives PHH |
| **Heckscher–Ohlin Framework** | Trade model predicting countries export goods that intensively use their abundant factor | Foundation for PHH theory |
| **Pollution Intensity** | Emissions per unit of output (e.g., tCO₂/$ revenue) | Sorting variable for industrial location |
| **Porter Hypothesis (Counter-view)** | Stricter regulation can *enhance* competitiveness via innovation | Direct challenge to PHH |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Neoclassical Foundation (Heckscher–Ohlin + Pollution)

In the standard **Heckscher–Ohlin (H–O)** model, countries specialize based on factor endowments. When **pollution abatement is treated as another "factor"**, the model predicts:

- **Pollution-intensive industries** will migrate toward countries with relatively *abundant environmental capacity* (i.e., lax standards).
- This trade-induced reallocation is the **theoretical engine** of the Pollution Haven Hypothesis.

### 2.1.2 The Copeland–Taylor Model (1994) — The Workhorse of PHH

Brandon Copeland and M. Scott Taylor formalized PHH using a general-equilibrium North–South trade model. Key assumptions:

1. The world has two regions: **North (developed, stringent)** and **South (developing, lenient)**.
2. Two goods: **X (clean)** and **Y (dirty/pollution-intensive)**.
3. Production of Y generates emissions; production of X does not.
4. The South has weaker environmental policy → lower abatement cost.

**Predicted outcome:** The South exports the dirty good Y, the North exports the clean good X, and the South becomes the **pollution haven**.

---

## 2.2 The Race to the Bottom — Game-Theoretic Framing

The RTB is best modeled as a **non-cooperative repeated game** between $N$ countries, each choosing a regulation level $r_i \in [0, \bar{r}]$.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Players:** $N$ countries competing for a mobile investment project (a polluting MNC).
2. **Strategy space:** Each country sets environmental stringency $r_i$.
3. **Payoff structure:**
   - If country $i$ offers *lower* $r_i$ than rivals, it captures the firm (payoff = +FDI, jobs, tax revenue).
   - If country $i$ offers *higher* $r_i$, the firm leaves (payoff = 0).
4. **Nash Equilibrium:** Each country sets $r_i = 0$ (or the lowest feasible level) regardless of what others do — because unilateral deviation upward loses the firm.
5. **Pareto-Optimal Outcome (Cooperative):** All countries maintain moderately high $r_i$ — preserving environmental quality while sharing the FDI pie.
6. **Gap:** The Nash equilibrium is **Pareto-inferior** to the cooperative outcome → **Prisoner's Dilemma**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "Why does RTB occur?", always anchor your answer in the **Prisoner's Dilemma logic**. Examiners reward the language of "individually rational, collectively irrational."

---

## 2.3 Counter-Theories (Required for Balanced Answers)

### 2.3.1 Porter Hypothesis (Michael Porter, 1991)
> Properly designed environmental regulation can **trigger innovation** that offsets compliance costs and enhances competitiveness — *the opposite of PHH*.

### 2.3.2 Pollution Halo Effect
> Multinationals from strict countries may *export superior environmental practices* to host countries, *improving* rather than degrading local standards.

### 2.3.3 Factor Endowment Dominance
> Empirical work by **Eskeland & Harrison (2003)** and **Jaffe et al. (1995)** finds that for most industries, *labor cost and skill* dominate *environmental cost* in location decisions → PHH effects are weak.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examiner-tested. Memorize the bold variables and units.**

### Formula Sheet Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | Effective Environmental Tax Rate | $$\text{EETR}_i = \frac{T_i + S_i - \text{Sub}_i}{P_i \cdot \text{Emissions}_i}$$ | $T_i$ = pollution tax revenue ($/yr), $S_i$ = pollution charges, $\text{Sub}_i$ = subsidies ($/yr), $P_i$ = unit price of pollutant | Cross-country stringency comparison |
| **F2** | Pollution Intensity (Sorting Index) | $$\text{PI}_j = \frac{E_j}{Q_j}$$ | $E_j$ = emissions of industry $j$ (tonnes), $Q_j$ = output (₹ or units) | Identifies "dirty" industries for PHH analysis |
| **F3** | Comparative Abatement Cost | $$\text{CAC}_{ij} = \frac{c_i^{abate}}{c_j^{abate}}$$ | Ratio of firm $i$'s abatement cost to competitor $j$'s | PHH migration trigger |
| **F4** | Copeland–Taylor World Emissions | $$E^W = E^N(\tau_N) + E^S(\tau_S)$$ | $\tau_N$, $\tau_S$ = emission tax rates in North/South | Total emissions for leakage calc |
| **F5** | Carbon Leakage Ratio | $$\text{LR} = \frac{\Delta E^S}{\Delta E^N} \bigg/ (-1)$$ | Change in South emissions per unit reduction in North | Measures PHH intensity |
| **F6** | Nash Equilibrium Regulation (RTB) | $$r_i^* = \arg\max_{r_i} \pi_i(r_i \mid r_{-i}) = r_{\min}$$ | Each $i$ minimizes own $r_i$ given rivals' $r_{-i}$ | Justifies $r=0$ outcome |
| **F7** | Social Welfare (Cooperative) | $$W^{coop} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ U_i(r_i) - C(r_i) + V_{FDI,i} \right]$$ | $U_i$ = utility, $C$ = environmental damage, $V_{FDI}$ = investment gain | Benchmarks cooperative optimum |
| **F8** | FDI Attractiveness Index | $$\text{FDI}_i = \alpha \cdot \text{Market}_i - \beta \cdot r_i - \gamma \cdot w_i$$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma > 0$; $w_i$ = wage; $r_i$ = regulation | Shows trade-off governing RTB |
| **F9** | Marginal Damage Condition (Optimal Tax) | $$\tau^* = \text{MD}(E)$$ | Pigouvian tax = marginal environmental damage | Why regulation is socially valuable |
| **F10** | Porter Net Effect | $$\Delta \pi = \underbrace{\text{Innovation Gains}}_{+} - \underbrace{\text{Compliance Cost}}_{-}$$ | Net $\Delta \pi > 0$ supports Porter Hypothesis | Refutes naïve PHH |

> **Note on Table Notation:** Vertical bars in mathematical expressions (e.g., $\vert r_i \vert$) are written using the `\vert` LaTeX command to avoid breaking markdown table parsing.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Policy Utility

| Domain | Application of RTB/PHH |
|---|---|
| **Climate Policy** | EU's **CBAM (Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism)** — designed explicitly to neutralize PHH leakage |
| **Industrial Siting** | MNCs use PHH logic to choose between manufacturing in India vs. Vietnam vs. Mexico |
| **ESG Investing** | Investors screen out firms operating in pollution havens to avoid reputational risk |
| **WTO Disputes** | PHH accusations underlie disputes over environmental subsidies and "eco-dumping" |
| **Sustainable Engineering** | Engineers must design *abatement tech* portable across jurisdictions to comply with harmonized standards (e.g., ISO 14001) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Nash Equilibrium of the Race to the Bottom

**Setup:** Two countries, $A$ and $B$, compete for one polluting firm. Each sets a regulation level $r_i \in [0, \bar{r}]$ where $r_i$ represents the **environmental tax rate** ($/unit of emission). The firm chooses the country with the **lower effective cost**.

### Step 1 — Define the Firm's Net Cost

The firm faces total cost $C_i(r_i)$ in country $i$:

$$C_i(r_i) = C_{\text{prod}} + r_i \cdot e$$

where $C_{\text{prod}}$ is production cost and $e$ is total emissions.

### Step 2 — Define Country Payoffs

If country $i$ wins the firm, it gains investment value $V > 0$ but suffers damage $D(r_i)$ from weakly enforced regulation:

$$\pi_i(r_i, r_{-i}) = \begin{cases} V - D(r_i) & \text{if } r_i < r_{-i} \\ -D(r_i) & \text{if } r_i > r_{-i} \\ \tfrac{1}{2}V - D(r_i) & \text{if } r_i = r_{-i} \end{cases}$$

Assume $D(r)$ is **decreasing** in $r$ (lower regulation → more pollution → more damage).

### Step 3 — Best Response of Country $A$

If $B$ chooses $r_B$, country $A$'s best response is:

$$r_A^*(r_B) = \arg\max_{r_A} \pi_A(r_A, r_B)$$

Since winning requires $r_A < r_B$, and winning payoff $V - D(r_A)$ is maximized by minimizing $r_A$, country $A$ sets:

$$r_A^* = r_B - \varepsilon$$

where $\varepsilon \to 0^+$ (just below $r_B$ to clinch the firm).

### Step 4 — Symmetric Argument

By symmetry, $B$'s best response is $r_B^* = r_A - \varepsilon$. The only mutual best response is the **lowest feasible level**:

$$r_A^* = r_B^* = 0$$

### Step 5 — Equilibrium Conclusion

$$\boxed{r_A^{NE} = r_B^{NE} = 0}$$

This is the **Nash Equilibrium of the RTB**. Both countries deregulate to the floor — the canonical Race to the Bottom outcome.

### Step 6 — Pareto-Optimal Benchmark

Suppose the countries could cooperate and split the firm evenly while maintaining $r^{coop} = r^* > 0$. Joint payoff:

$$W^{coop} = V - D(r^*) + V - D(r^*) = 2V - 2D(r^*)$$

Nash payoff:

$$W^{NE} = 0 - D(0) + 0 - D(0) = -2D(0)$$

Since $V > 0$ and $D(0) \gg D(r^*)$, we have:

$$W^{coop} > W^{NE}$$

**The cooperative outcome strictly Pareto-dominates the Nash outcome → Prisoner's Dilemma confirmed.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Copeland–Taylor Two-Good, Two-Country Model

**Setup:** World has North (N) and South (S). Two goods:
- $X$ — **clean** (no emissions in production)
- $Y$ — **dirty** (emits $\alpha$ units per unit output)

Both regions produce both goods but with different relative efficiency.

### Step 1 — Production Possibilities

Let $L_i$ be labor endowment, $\theta_i$ be productivity parameter. Production of $Y$ in region $i$ generates emissions:

$$E_i = \alpha \cdot Y_i$$

### Step 2 — Government Behavior

The South (lenient) sets emission tax $\tau_S = 0$. The North (stringent) sets $\tau_N > 0$ at Pigouvian level $\tau_N = \text{MD}(E)$.

### Step 3 — Profit Maximization in Dirty Sector

A representative firm's profit in region $i$:

$$\pi_i^Y = p_Y \cdot Y_i - w_i L_i^Y - \tau_i \cdot \alpha Y_i$$

where $w_i$ is wage. Profit per unit:

$$\pi_i^Y / Y_i = p_Y - w_i \cdot (1/a_i^Y) - \tau_i \cdot \alpha$$

### Step 4 — Free-Trade Equilibrium Condition

Under free trade with common price $p_Y$, capital and firms move to where profit is highest. Setting $\pi_N^Y = \pi_S^Y$:

$$p_Y - w_N / a_N^Y - \tau_N \alpha = p_Y - w_S / a_S^Y - 0$$

Solving:

$$\tau_N \alpha = \frac{w_N}{a_N^Y} - \frac{w_S}{a_S^Y}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

If the North's wage-productivity gap exceeds the tax cost, **production of Y migrates South**. This is the **Pollution Haven effect**:

$$\boxed{Y_S \uparrow, \quad Y_N \downarrow, \quad E^W \text{ partially shifts from N to S}}$$

### Step 6 — Carbon Leakage

Define leakage as:

$$\text{LR} = \frac{\Delta E^S}{\Delta E^N \big|_{\text{policy}}} = \frac{\partial E^S / \partial \tau_N}{-\partial E^N / \partial \tau_N}$$

Empirical literature (Aldy & Pizer, 2015) estimates LR in the range **5%–25%** for unilateral carbon policies.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Conditions Strengthening vs. Weakening PHH

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite 14-Mark Framework:** When asked "Is PHH true?", structure your answer around this matrix.

| Condition | Strengthens PHH | Weakens PHH |
|---|---|---|
| **Mobility of dirty industries** | High (e.g., steel, cement, basic chemicals) | Low (services, software, finance) |
| **Trade openness** | High → easy to relocate | Restricted by tariffs/quotas |
| **Environmental policy gap** | Wide North–South gap | Narrow (e.g., EU harmonization) |
| **Factor cost gap** | Environmental cost dominates | Labor/skill dominates (Eskeland–Harrison) |
| **Technology of abatement** | Inelastic, hard to transfer | Elastic, transfer via FDI (Porter) |
| **Consumer environmental awareness** | Low | High (green demand offsets PHH) |
| **Capital market response** | Indifferent | ESG screening penalizes haven-located firms |
| **Domestic institutional capacity** | Weak in host | Strong, can impose own standards |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Illustration: EETR Computation (Exam-Ready)

**Problem (KTU-style):** Country A levies a pollution tax of ₹500 crore, charges ₹200 crore in effluent fees, gives ₹100 crore in subsidies, and has pollutant price ₹50/kg with total emissions 10 million kg. Country B has tax ₹100 cr, charges ₹50 cr, no subsidies, pollutant price ₹100/kg, emissions 5 million kg.

**Solution:**

For Country A, the numerator (net environmental revenue):

$$N_A = 500 + 200 - 100 = 600 \text{ crore}$$

Denominator:

$$D_A = 50 \times 10{,}000{,}000 = 500 \text{ crore}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{EETR}_A = \frac{600}{500} = 1.20$$

For Country B:

$$N_B = 100 + 50 - 0 = 150 \text{ crore}$$

$$D_B = 100 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 500 \text{ crore}$$

$$\text{EETR}_B = \frac{150}{500} = 0.30$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{EETR}_A > \text{EETR}_B$ → Country B has weaker effective regulation → **potential PHH host**. Dirty industries should migrate from A to B *ceteris paribus*.
